agenda_text: Regular Meeting Breakdown Procedural Items Closed Session (Executive Session) Receive Information Regarding District-Wide Intruder Detection Audit Report The board will privately receive results of a security audit examining how well the district detects potential intruders across its campuses. This session is closed to the public because Texas law allows school boards to discuss security measures and audits without public disclosure, to avoid compromising campus safety. Legal Update The board's attorney will provide a confidential briefing on any pending or anticipated legal matters involving the district. This is a routine closed-session item that allows the board to receive legal advice without publicly disclosing sensitive information. Conduct Level III Grievance Hearing A Level III grievance is the highest level of internal appeal a district employee or parent can file within CISD before seeking outside review. The board will privately hear this appeal as permitted by Texas law for personnel or student-related complaints. Discuss Principal Candidates The board will privately discuss candidates being considered for principal positions at several CISD campuses. Personnel deliberations are conducted in closed session under Texas law to protect the privacy of the individuals being considered. Action on Executive Session Items Consideration and Possible Action on Level III Grievance After the closed grievance hearing, the board will return to open session to vote on a resolution or decision regarding the Level III grievance. All final votes must occur in open session under Texas law. Special Recognition 2026 USA Co-ed Cheerleading National Champions — Caney Creek High School The Caney Creek High School co-ed cheerleading team will be recognized for winning a national championship in USA Cheer competition. This is a ceremonial honor with no board vote required. 2026 NCA Game Day Cheerleading National Champions — College Park High School The College Park High School cheerleading team will be recognized for winning a national championship in the NCA (National Cheerleaders Association) Game Day category. This is a ceremonial recognition. 2026 UIL Girls Wrestling State Champion — The Woodlands High School A student-athlete from The Woodlands High School will be honored for winning a UIL (University Interscholastic League) state championship in girls wrestling. This is a ceremonial recognition. 2026 UIL Boys and Girls Wrestling State Champions — College Park High School Student-athletes from College Park High School will be recognized for winning UIL state championships in both boys and girls wrestling. This is a ceremonial recognition. 2026 UIL Boys Freestyle Swimming State Champion — Oak Ridge High School A student-athlete from Oak Ridge High School will be honored for winning a UIL state championship in boys freestyle swimming. This is a ceremonial recognition. 2026 UIL Girls Team Swimming State Champions — The Woodlands High School The Woodlands High School girls swim team will be recognized for winning the UIL state team swimming championship. This is a ceremonial recognition. Consent Agenda Consider and Approve Minutes The board will approve the official written record of its previous meeting(s). This is a standard housekeeping item required to maintain an accurate public record. Consider and Approve Amendment to the Budget The board will consider a mid-year adjustment to the district's budget. Budget amendments are common throughout the school year as revenues and expenditures shift from original projections; board approval is required for any changes. Receive Human Resources Report and Consider and Approve Employment of Professional Personnel The board will receive a staffing update and vote to officially approve the hiring of professional staff (such as teachers or administrators). Board approval is required for employment of professional personnel under Texas law. Receive Financial Reports The board will receive routine financial reports showing the district's current revenue, expenditures, and overall fiscal position. This is an informational item required for board oversight of district finances. Consider and Approve Texas Teacher Evaluation and Support System (T-TESS) Appraisers T-TESS is the state's official framework for evaluating teacher performance. The board must formally approve the list of staff members who are certified and authorized to conduct teacher evaluations under this system. Request Ratification of the District's 2026-2027 Certification of Provision of Instructional Materials Form Texas requires school districts to certify annually that they are providing students with required instructional materials. The board will formally ratify this certification for the upcoming 2026-2027 school year. Consider Authorizing the District to Enter into an Interlocal Agreement with Region 16 Education Service Center and Interlocal Agreement with ESC 16's Sponsored Cooperative TexBuy This item authorizes CISD to participate in a purchasing cooperative (TexBuy) managed through Region 16 Education Service Center. Cooperative purchasing agreements allow the district to buy goods and services at pre-negotiated prices, which can reduce costs and administrative burden. Human Resources Discuss and Possibly Approve Principal for Bradley Elementary The board will vote on appointing a principal to lead Bradley Elementary School. Principal appointments are significant personnel decisions that require board approval. Discuss and Possibly Approve Principal for Suchma Elementary The board will vote on appointing a principal to lead Suchma Elementary School. This is a personnel action requiring board approval. Discuss and Possibly Approve Principal for Cox Intermediate The board will vote on appointing a principal to lead Cox Intermediate School. This is a personnel action requiring board approval. Discuss and Possibly Approve Principal for Travis Intermediate School The board will vote on appointing a principal to lead Travis Intermediate School. This is a personnel action requiring board approval. Discuss and Possibly Approve Principal for Stockton Junior High School The board will vote on appointing a principal to lead Stockton Junior High School. This is a personnel action requiring board approval. Discuss and Possibly Approve Principal for York Junior High School The board will vote on appointing a principal to lead York Junior High School. This is a personnel action requiring board approval. Discuss and Possibly Approve Principal for Grand Oaks High School 9th The board will vote on appointing a principal to lead the 9th grade campus at Grand Oaks High School. This is a personnel action requiring board approval. Administration Receive Information and Approve the Campus Mascot for Kacy Arnold Elementary The board will officially select and approve a mascot for Kacy Arnold Elementary, which appears to be a newer or recently named CISD campus. The mascot is an important part of a school's identity and community culture. Receive Information and Approve the Campus Mascot for Lynn Cartwright Junior High School The board will officially select and approve a mascot for Lynn Cartwright Junior High School, another campus that likely requires a new identity as a newly established or named school. Receive District and Campus Future Enrollment Projection Information from Zonda Demographics The board will hear a presentation from Zonda Demographics, a professional firm, on projected future student enrollment across CISD campuses. Enrollment projections are used to plan staffing, facility needs, and budgeting. Discuss and Possibly Consider Local School Library Advisory Council (LSLAC) Texas law allows school districts to establish Local School Library Advisory Councils to provide input on library materials and policies. The board will discuss the formation or status of this council for CISD. Discussion of Commemorative Months and Other Issues Under School Board Policy EMB The board will discuss how the district recognizes or observes commemorative months (such as heritage or awareness months) and related matters as governed by district policy EMB. This may involve reviewing or clarifying current policy on how these observances are handled in schools. Teaching and Learning Consider and Possibly Approve Library Materials in Accordance with Texas Education Code §33.026, School Board Policy EFB, and Senate Bill 13 The board will vote on approving specific materials for inclusion in CISD school libraries, following requirements set by Texas law (including Senate Bill 13, which established new rules for library material review). This item involves decisions about what books and resources are available to students in district libraries. Receive Information Regarding the Beginning and Middle of Year Northwest Evaluation Association Measures of Academic Progress Growth and Reading Fluency Assessment, House Bill 3 Progress Measure 1.2 and 2.2, and Bluebonnet Learning ELAR K-5 The board will receive a data report on how CISD students are performing on several academic assessments, including NWEA MAP (a widely used standardized test measuring academic growth), reading fluency scores, and state-required literacy metrics tied to House Bill 3. This is an informational presentation to keep the board updated on student academic progress. Business/Finance Receive Personnel Audit Presentation by the Docentus Group The Docentus Group, an outside consulting firm, will present findings from a personnel audit of the district. Personnel audits examine staffing structures, compensation, and workforce efficiency, and are typically used to help the district make informed decisions about human resources and budgeting. Planning and Construction Consider and Approve the Guaranteed Maximum Price Amendment for the Irons, Peet, York, and Conroe High School 9th Renovations Project and Authorize the Superintendent to Complete Negotiations and Execute the Contract Documents The board will vote to set a Guaranteed Maximum Price (GMP) — a construction contract term that caps the total cost the district will pay — for renovation projects at Irons, Peet, and York Junior Highs and Conroe High School's 9th grade campus. This is a significant spending decision that also authorizes the Superintendent to finalize and sign the contracts. Consider and Accept as Complete the Armstrong, Austin, Birnham Woods, and Broadway Elementary Renovations Project The board will formally vote to accept renovation work at four elementary campuses — Armstrong, Austin, Birnham Woods, and Broadway — as fully complete. Official board acceptance is required to close out construction contracts and release any remaining contractor payments. Consider and Accept as Complete Conroe High School Tennis Courts at the 9th Grade Campus Project The board will vote to officially accept the construction of tennis courts at Conroe High School's 9th grade campus as complete. As with other completed projects, this formal acceptance closes out the construction contract.

Generated 2026-03-29 01:18 UTC Unofficial Meeting Notes Conroe Independent School District Board of Trustees Special Meeting — March 2, 2026 Note: These are unofficial notes based on an auto-generated transcript. Meeting Called to Order - Board President called the meeting to order at 6:00 p.m. Note: President later corrected the record, stating the actual time was 5:00 p.m. and the clock had been off throughout the hearing portion. - A quorum was present. - Invocation led by Trustee Dungan; pledges led by Trustee Dawson. Part 1: Public Hearing — Montgomery Trace Section 4 Detachment/Annexation Petition Purpose of Hearing The Board convened a public hearing pursuant to Texas Education Code §§13.03 and 13.051 to consider a petition seeking to detach Montgomery Trace Section 4 from Conroe ISD and annex it to Montgomery ISD. Citizen Participation No members of the public signed up to speak during the public comment period. Petitioner Presentation — Wisdom Idollet Resident, Montgomery Trace Section 4 Mr. Idollet presented on behalf of the neighborhood. Key points: - Neighborhood profile: Approximately 80 homes; borders Montgomery ISD to the north and west; accessible only via Hempie Egypt Road, which is otherwise entirely within Montgomery ISD. - Geographic anomaly: The CISD boundary appears to have crossed a creek specifically to include this neighborhood, which Mr. Idollet described as historically unusual. - Bus routing: CISD buses must travel 4–8 extra miles through other districts to serve the neighborhood. - Petition signatures: 53% of registered voters signed meeting the TEC majority threshold ; approximately 63% of actual current residents signed. About 12% were never contacted. - Reasons cited by residents for wanting to transfer: - Montgomery ISD schools are closer on average - Most residents already socialize, shop, and attend church in Montgomery - Perceived safety advantage near Montgomery schools based on CrimeGrades.org - Higher proficiency/SAT scores at Montgomery schools they would be zoned to - Bus routing inefficiency for CISD Challenges acknowledged by Mr. Idollet: 1. Property value ratio: The neighborhood falls approximately 4 students short of meeting the TEC ratio requirement. He noted that a majority of CISD board members signing the petition could satisfy this requirement under TEC. 2. Meets and bounds description: CISD legal counsel found the petition's legal description inadequate. Mr. Idollet acknowledged the petitioners are not lawyers or surveyors and worked from the neighborhood plat. 3. Debt transfer: Montgomery ISD expressed concern about inheriting CISD debt associated with the territory. Board Questions for Mr. Idollet - Trustee unidentified : Asked whether residents were aware of the tax rate increase. Mr. Idollet confirmed all signatories were informed; average annual increase would be approximately $568 per household . Most were agreeable; only 2–3 families objected on those grounds. Residents over 65 with locked-in taxes were generally supportive. - Trustee: Asked about Montgomery ISD's open enrollment policy. Mr. Idollet clarified that Montgomery ISD closed its full open enrollment 1–2 years ago and now has a limited, application-based process. He stated the decision before the board was partly premised on open enrollment still being available, which he said is no longer the case. - Trustee: Asked how many K–12 children are in the neighborhood. Mr. Idollet estimated 22 currently enrolled in CISD , with some already transferred to Montgomery ISD before open enrollment closed, and some homeschooling families. - Trustee: Asked about the historical reason the boundary crossed the creek. No one present had that information. District Presentation — General Counsel Key points presented on behalf of CISD: - Montgomery ISD voted against the annexation , citing concerns about inheriting CISD debt and other issues. - Tax rate impact: CISD's tax rate is currently lower than Montgomery ISD's. The average home in the area is valued at approximately $420,000, resulting in an estimated $568/year increase per homeowner if annexed. - Assessed value of territory: Approximately $50 million assessed; approximately $38 million taxable after exemptions . - 94 total parcels in the subdivision per Montgomery Central Appraisal District records. - 26 students were identified as currently riding CISD buses from the subdivision this school year; however, the statute requires using the prior year's last day of school enrollment figure — 22 students — for the ratio calculation. - Statutory ratio requirement TEC Chapter 13 : The ratio of taxable property value of the territory to total CISD taxable value must not exceed the ratio of school-age children in the territory to total CISD enrollment. In this case, the property value ratio is higher than the student ratio , meaning the statutory threshold is not met and the board lacks authority to proceed. - Montgomery ISD transfer option: General Counsel noted Montgomery ISD appears to still have a transfer application online. Mr. Idollet disputed this, stating full open enrollment has ended and the current process is limited and application-based. - Debt transfer question: A trustee asked whether CISD could simply retain the debt rather than transfer it. General Counsel was researching the statute in real time and could not give a firm answer. Staff noted that if CISD retains the debt but loses the taxable value and ADA, it would face a "double whammy" — losing both revenue streams while retaining the obligation. Board Discussion - Trustees discussed the historical oddity of the boundary crossing the creek and noted CISD has similar situations elsewhere e.g., buses traveling through Splendora ISD to reach Candy Creek area . - Trustees noted concern that the open enrollment premise cited in the district's presentation may be inaccurate based on Mr. Idollet's information. - One trustee highlighted the need for a firm legal answer on whether the debt must transfer before any future action could be considered. Motion and Vote Motion made by Board President : The board determine that the petition filed by Wisdom Idollet seeking detachment of Montgomery Trace Section 4 from Conroe ISD and annexation to Montgomery ISD fails to satisfy the mandatory property value ratio requirement set forth in Texas Education Code Chapter 13, including §§13.03 and 13.051, in that the ratio of taxable property value of the territory proposed to be detached to the taxable property value of the affected district does not meet the statutory threshold; and further that the board adopt the findings of fact and resolution reflecting that determination and decline to proceed with the requested detachment and annexation. Vote: 6–0 in favor motion carries Outcome: The petition was denied on statutory grounds. The hearing was formally concluded. Part 2: Citizen Participation Special Meeting No members of the public had signed up to speak. General Counsel explained the item falls under board duties, which qualifies as deliberation regarding the duties of public officers under §551.074. CISD Police Department Ambassador Awards Chief Matt Blake recognized four employees: - Shirley Herbert – Crossing guard, originally a prevention control monitor who returned after retirement - Officer Joshua Broussard – Campus officer at Conroe High School; named Police Officer of the Year - Corporal Randy Longden – K-9 handler explosive detection with 19 years at CISD - Officer Deborah Quiller – Campus officer at Moorhead Junior High; recognized for community outreach Human Resources Ambassador Awards Executive Director Paula Green recognized: - Terl Jackson – Teacher Incentive Allotment TIA Coordinator; recognized for expertise in TIA implementation and regional training - Jill Bartlett – Employee Benefits Specialist 11 years ; recognized for employee advocacy and coordinating the district's annual health fair Citizen Participation The following individuals addressed the board 2-minute limit : - Jason Harvey – Spoke in support of fine arts programs; shared a personal family story about a son with autism whose first words came through music and who went on to become an accomplished performer. - Sarah Dillard – Opposed displaying the Ten Commandments under SB10; raised First Amendment concerns and asked the board to wait for the pending court injunction. Also objected to religious content in the Bluebonnet curriculum, requesting alternate non-religious lesson options. - Lorraine Rice – Expressed support for Ten Commandments displays in schools; argued the commandments reflect founding principles and general moral values. - Patricia Spore – Introduced herself as a credentialed school chaplain; spoke in support of chaplains in schools and their non-proselytizing, supportive role. - Hannah Wear – Spoke in support of SB111 special education legal fee transparency ; shared a personal experience of a decade-long special education legal dispute in another district that she argued cost hundreds of thousands of taxpayer dollars. - Rebecca Smith – On behalf of several organizations including Love Hills Youth Liberty Library Project and local Republican women's groups; announced a donation of 330 Ten Commandments posters to CISD at no cost, meeting SB10 requirements. Directed viewers to restoreamericanschools.com. - Amanda Cosy – CISD parent class of '04 ; opposed mandatory Ten Commandments displays; cited her right as a parent to direct her child's religious upbringing and asked the board to advocate for families of all faiths. - Crystal Iker – Introduced the CISD Special Education Parent Advisory Council SEPAC , a new parent-led group created in partnership with the district. First meeting: September 15, 6 p.m., Jet Center . Website: conroisdcpac.com. - Susan Hedgi – Bus driver speaking again about transportation concerns; noted insurance costs are consuming raises and that transportation staff still need more support. - Diane Daniels – Expressed support for Ten Commandments posters; encouraged the board to collaborate with like-minded districts to pursue legislative changes, especially regarding technology/tablet use. - Jonathan Covy – Policy Director, Texas Values; stated the Ten Commandments law is on solid constitutional ground following the Kennedy v. Bremerton 2022 Supreme Court decision; presented a $6,000 check from Texas Values toward poster purchases. - Destiny Milton – Opposed proposed board policy BDD revisions and the potential hiring of attorney Cara Belaloo as general counsel; cited alleged conflicts of interest including Belaloo's role at Texas Education Executives TEE , connections to Texas Public Policy Foundation, and her consulting firm Root Strategy. - Kristen Brown – SHAC committee member; thanked the board for reviewing the SHAC membership. Also raised concerns about the version of the Ten Commandments Catholic vs. Protestant , and echoed concerns about BDD/Belaloo. - Aggie Gambino – CISD parent; spoke in support of SEPAC and the special education legal transparency policy EHBA ; shared that a prior due process case cost the district $50,000 and was not known to the board until settlement. Thanked Trustees May, Odenweller, Nelson, and Dr. Vinson. - Susan Scruggs – Expressed support for Ten Commandments posters; also spoke positively about moment of silence/prayer, cell phone policy, food additive removal, and removal of gender-transition instructional materials. - Mark Fusa – Spoke in support of special education legal transparency policy; shared personal experience with a due process case involving his autistic son and alleged the district spent hundreds of thousands of dollars fighting it. - Robert Naveski – Read the Ten Commandments aloud; argued that only 4 of the 10 relate to civil/moral law and the rest are specific to Christian faith, calling the display "indoctrination." Said he would consider it a violation of his parental rights. Consent Agenda Items modified: Item K RFP 25-06-3, bus routing/scheduling software was pulled for separate discussion. Two items were added to consent: Administration Item 5C HB Early Childhood/College/Career/Military Readiness Board plans and Item 6A Washington High School optional flexible school day program application . Vote: Motion by Trustee Nelson, seconded by Trustee Horton. Result: Approved 7–0 Pulled Item: Bus Routing and Scheduling Software RFP 25-06-3 - Current routing software Compass, $20,000/year since 2014 is sunsetting — the company is going out of business; service guaranteed through end of this school year. - Proposed new software: implementation cost ~$147,000; ongoing annual cost ~$57,000. - Smart Tag was noted as not submitting a bid; its routing component was described as insufficiently robust. - Trustees discussed whether to delay approval pending a transportation efficiency audit , which CFO is evaluating over the coming week. - The board agreed the software will still likely be needed regardless of audit findings. Motion: Trustee Nelson moved to postpone the software purchase pending review of efficiency audit findings. Seconded by Trustee Dungan. Vote: Approved 7–0 postponed Superintendent's Report STAAR Scores Presentation Three assistant superintendents presented results: Elementary Dr. Winkler : - 82% of elementary schools earned an A or B rating — a 6% increase from last year; 31 of 38 schools. - 70 distinctions earned district-wide; 4 campuses earned every eligible distinction. - Third grade reading: 2-point increase in "Meets" level. - Third grade math: 3.3-point gain in "Meets," 5-point gain in "Masters." - Fourth grade math: 3-poin

Preliminary 2025-2026 Budget Overview Item 6A & Compensation Plan Item 6B Non-action/informational items presented concurrently Presenters: Karen Garza Chief Financial Officer , Amy Campbell Director of HR Services, TASB , Paula Green Executive Director of HR Legislative Context — House Bill 2 HB2 - 89th Texas Legislative Session ended June 2, 2025; over 9,000 bills filed, ~1,300 passed. - HB2 allocated $4.2 billion for school funding: - $3.4B for teacher pay; $800M for support staff - $2.6B to address funding gaps new "Allotment for Basic Costs" at $106/enrolled student - $850M for special education - $1.7B for targeted programs CTE, teacher prep, early learning - Total: $8.5 billion in additional public school funding this biennium - New allotments created: - Teacher Retention Allotment: $2,500/teacher 3-4 years experience , $5,000/teacher 5+ years — permanent, protected funding - Staff Support Retention Allotment: $45 × adjusted ADA for non-classroom employees - School Safety Allotment: increased to $33,540/campus + $20/ADA up from $10,000/campus - Special Ed FIE Reimbursement: $1,000 per individual evaluation - Teacher Incentive Allotment TIA expanded with new "Acknowledged" category; With $780M in voter-approved bonds yet to be issued, the increased homestead exemptions could require a debt service tax rate increase in future years. This was described as an unforeseen consequence of the state changing rules mid-bond program. Tax Rate Compression — Methodology Change The state changed how it calculates the local property value survey. Districts will now report year-over-year values using the new exemption amounts not apples-to-apples comparisons , resulting in a projected net decrease in property values for CISD. Higher pay for special education paraprofessionals in high-needs settings +$1,000 stipend increase 4. Prioritize hourly/lowest-paid employees operations, paras, campus secretaries 5. New pay structure for technology employees 6. Groundwork for every-other-year raise cycle aligned to HB2 funding Proposed increases: | Pay Group | General Increase | | --| --| | Teachers 5+ years | 7% | | Teachers 0-4 years | 4% | | Admin Support, Instructional Support | 6% | | Admin Education, Admin Business, Technology, Police | 4% | - Starting teacher pay: $64,000 up from ~$61,300 - Teachers with 0-2 years receive $2,700 increase 4%, locally funded - Teachers with 3-4 years: $2,500 state-funded + local adjustments - Teachers with 5+ years: $5,000 state-funded - Total compensation plan cost: $33.1M ~$22M supported by state allotments - Educator career pathway adjustments ensure counselors and other teacher-adjacent staff receive comparable increases - High-need special ed teaching stipend increase: $280,000 - Total budget impact: 6.4% Every-other-year raise cycle: Administration recommended frontloading raises in 2025-26, with the understanding that 2026-27 would maintain raises but not provide new ones. A retention stipend from fund balance was suggested for the off-year. B. Hurricane Preparedness for the District Mr. McCord, Mr. Milsaps, Mr. Schrader Season Outlook: - NOAA and Colorado State University predict an above-average hurricane season. - 2025 is projected to be a neutral year neither El Niño nor La Niña , meaning average conditions. - Saharan dust events can help suppress hurricane formation. Historical Context — Hurricane Beryl 2024 : - Beryl was the earliest Category 5 hurricane ever recorded June and meandered in the Gulf for 13 days before making landfall July 8 as a Category 1 near Matagorda. - Gave little warning on direction, heading directly toward the county. Legal/Partnership Framework: - House Bill 1471 2007 : Requires organizations like CISD to potentially provide resources e.g., buses to the community, region, or state during emergencies. - 2018 Resolution: Mutual aid agreement between CISD and Montgomery County for reciprocal assistance during emergencies. CISD Pre-Storm Preparations Mr. McCord & Mr. Schrader : - Generators serviced monthly; contractor performs major checks twice yearly. - Pallets of water stored in warehouse for immediate use. - Three emergency trailers maintained: - "Flood fighter" trailer used successfully during Knox water release — handled internally without outside contractor - Generator trailer with portable generators and extension cords for temporary campus power - Additional response trailer - Goal: Keep students in school whenever possible, as parents may be at work. - Pre-storm: Secure potential projectiles around campuses; check food storage in freezers/coolers. - Strategic staff placement: Maintenance staff positioned by geographic zone e.g., East County staff check East County schools to minimize response time. Post-Storm Response: - Pre-arranged reunion plans for staff. - Maintenance checks for water intrusion specific vulnerable locations identified, e.g., Washington campus gym floor . - After-action reviews conducted after every significant storm; lessons learned are incorporated into updated checklists. - Communication via text strings; digital radios added after cell signal loss during Hurricane Ike. - Inspection programs on cell phones allow documentation with or without cell signal can submit later — proved invaluable for FEMA and insurance claims. - FEMA claims require GPS coordinates, before/after photos for every item of damage. - Perimeter fencing reinforced with bottom rails funded by safety and security grant ; some fence failures occurred during Beryl, but reinforcements should hold against all but severe storms. - Network Operations Center NOC : Located on-site, backed by generators, enables electronic communications during emergencies. Montgomery County Emergency Management Capabilities Mr. Milsaps : - 78,000 sq. ft. warehouse stocked with emergency supplies "Costco for emergency management" : - 78 pallets of ice currently on hand - Extensive water supply rotated to prevent expiration - Stock levels being raised to hurricane season levels - County utilizes FEMA and state resources to restock after events. - 100% professional, career-level first responders across the county. - EOC staff deploys to disasters nationwide for experience. - Evacuation considerations: Galveston County increasing evacuation time from 36 to 72 hours due to causeway bridge construction; Montgomery County is a pass-through county, not an evacuation county — residents encouraged to shelter in place. - County traffic management plan being updated for increased pass-through traffic. - Fuel resources: On contract through mutual aid with CISD; county can move fuel independently and maintains a stockpile; offered to help CISD if generator fuel contractors are delayed. - Buses: County has used CISD and Splendora ISD buses in past emergencies; does not anticipate needing CISD buses unless there is a major countywide event.

Compliance Subcommittee The subcommittee met on April 22 and provided updates on several topics: Credit by Exam / 4-Year and 6-Year Plans: - A knowledge gap exists among students and parents regarding course planning and credit-by-exam options in junior high and high school. The subcommittee requested clarification from the district. Vaping: - The district provided vaping data, which is under review. - The subcommittee wants to ensure consistent messaging is delivered to all CISD students regarding the health and legal consequences of vaping. Parent Consent Forms: - The subcommittee suggested adding QR codes to consent forms to make materials more accessible. The district Mr. Kmart is expected to add these for the 2025–26 school year. Recess Recommendation Major Discussion Item : The subcommittee presented a detailed, research-based recess recommendation grounded in CDC guidance and Texas Education Code requirements. Key points from the presentation: - Recess and PE have distinct benefits; recess provides unstructured time that builds resilience, creativity, and problem-solving. - CDC recommends recess benefits extend K–12, not just elementary. - CDC national guidance recommends 20+ minutes of daily recess and advises against withholding recess for disciplinary or academic reasons. Proposed Recommendation by School Level: | School Level | Current Daily Recess | SHAC Recommended | | | | | | Elementary | 30 minutes | No change continue as-is | | Intermediate 5th–6th | 10–15 min most campuses ; 20 min at Collins | 20 minutes daily | | Junior High | None dedicated | Offer 20 minutes of daily opportunity to get outside | | High School | None dedicated | Offer opportunities to get outside creatively | Additional Recommendations: - Do not exclude students from recess for disciplinary, academic, or makeup work reasons. - Keep lunch and recess as two separate periods for K–6. - Notify parents if recess is withheld from a student. Extensive Discussion Ensued: - Dr. Todd principal raised concerns about manpower, safety, and the logistics of supervising students outside, particularly at junior high and high school levels. She noted some campuses already offer creative options e.g., Stockton allows students access to courtyards, cafeteria, library, and gym before school . - Dr. Klesky intermediate principal, 28 years experience raised significant concerns about scheduling constraints due to the newly adopted Bluebonnet Learning Program , which mandates a minimum of 120 minutes of daily language arts for K–5 doubling current time for 5th grade . Combined with the recommended 90 minutes for math, there is very limited time remaining in the school day. She noted: - Fine arts at her campus have been cut 50% to accommodate the new mandate. - Science and social studies instruction time will likely be reduced. - Teachers are already incorporating brain breaks and movement into instruction as best practice. - She expressed concern that if the recommendation becomes a mandate, it could create an impossible scheduling situation. - A nurse/parent member expressed support for the idea but concern about safety and accountability, particularly at high school campuses—how to ensure students don't leave campus, how to account for everyone. - Miss McMullen argued strongly that SHAC's role is to recommend the gold standard based on research, and that it should be the CISD Board's responsibility to reconcile conflicting mandates e.g., Bluebonnet curriculum vs. recess time . She stated the Board made the curriculum adoption decision and should deal with the repercussions. - A member raised concerns that the state legislature may use SHAC recommendations to criticize districts that can't comply, given impossible conflicting mandates and flat state funding since 2019. - Dr. Chavela cautioned that for junior high and high school, unstructured time could result in students simply being on their phones, which would defeat the purpose. She also noted that for older students, electives band, choir, art, robotics serve as their outlet and "recess." - Multiple members shared examples of existing creative solutions: Knox Junior High's outdoor lunch period, College Park's courtyard, Grand Oaks' advisory period 25 minutes daily , outdoor PTO-purchased eating spaces, and teachers incorporating outdoor lessons. - A member suggested adding language: "Each campus can work toward goal recommendations given individual campus time constraints" to acknowledge real-world constraints. Final Motion on Recess Recommendation: - Motion: To approve the SHAC recess recommendation as presented, with the added language: "Each campus can work toward goal recommendations given individual campus time constraints," and with accompanying documentation listing implementation concerns manpower, safety, lunch waste, scheduling challenges with newly implemented curriculum . - Vote: Approved unanimously no nays, no abstentions . 2. Item 2: Amendment to District Access Control Procedures and Update to Multi-Hazard Emergency Operations Plan MEOP — CTE Roll-Up Door Solution Presenter: Ethan district safety/security staff Background - TEA defines roll-up doors found in CTE areas such as automotive and welding shops as exterior doors , which under TEA Chapter 61 must be closed, latched, and locked. - However, these doors cannot remain closed during instruction because ventilation is required for student safety in welding, automotive, and similar programs. - This created a compliance challenge that needed to be addressed. Proposed Solution - A team including Ethan , Tally Stout CTE Director , and Marshall Schrader Executive Director of Maintenance developed a plan. - Security scissor/folding gates will be installed on either side of roll-up doors. When the roll-up door is open for ventilation, the security gate will be pulled across and locked, creating a secure barrier. - TEA Region 6 confirmed this is a viable, compliant solution under Chapter 61. - Gates will feature smaller diamond openings to reduce visibility and enhance security. - All high schools with CTE roll-up doors will receive the gates. Proposed Language for Access Control Procedures "Instructional areas that require ventilation, for example welding, automotive tech, are allowed to remain open for ventilation only when actively monitored, behind installed, locked and secure area fencing, or behind installed security folding gates as approved by the Conroe ISD Safety and Security Committee." Funding - The project will be funded through Cycle 2 grant funds an allowable expenditure . Timeline - Installation is planned for over the summer , pending committee approval. Maintenance department is ready to begin immediately. Discussion Highlights - Committee members expressed enthusiasm for the solution. - Dr. Povich confirmed the high school side is supportive of the solution. - Another committee member noted it is both a compliance win and a practical win for maximizing CTE instruction, with the added benefit of grant funding. Vote - Motion: Made and seconded. - Vote: Approved unanimously no opposition . Item 3: Approval of Conroe ISD Multi-Hazard Emergency Operations Plan MEOP for 2025–2026 School Year Presenter: Ethan Summary - The updated MEOP was shared with committee members in advance via a link. - No substantial changes were made — updates were primarily maintenance items: updating years, names, dates, and ensuring compliance with current state requirements. - The plan serves as the district's framework that supplements the state plan. - No feedback or recommended changes were received from committee members prior to the meeting. Vote - Motion: Dr. Stickler - Second: Matt Benson - Vote: Approved unanimously no opposition . Action Item 3A: Consider and Possibly Take Action Regarding MAP Testing for the District Presentation A team from the district's assessment, curriculum, accountability, school improvement, and Teacher Incentive Allotment TIA departments presented. Presenters included Wendy Tisdel Coordinator of Assessment , Darren Carlile Director of Curriculum & Instruction , Lauren Heler Coordinator of Accountability , Linda Stewart Director of School Improvement , and Terrell Jackson TIA Coordinator . The presentation was intentionally limited to four slides, as extensive discussion and data sharing had occurred over the preceding two weeks since the prior board meeting. Key Points - Three types of assessment were outlined: - Curriculum-embedded assessments e.g., Quarterly Assessments/QAs — aligned to instructional materials, formative or summative - Interim/benchmark assessments e.g., MAP — diagnostic, adaptive, show whether students are on pace for end-of-year standards - Summative assessments e.g., STAAR — used for accountability - MAP Measures of Academic Progress is a dynamic, adaptive assessment that: - Measures both achievement and growth over time - Predicts STAAR performance and can predict ACT/SAT performance starting in 2nd grade - Identifies individual student strengths and weaknesses - Provides data at student, class, school, and district levels - Replaces certain existing assessments shown on a replacement chart - Instructional time savings: MAP replaces assessments that collectively consume approximately 21 instructional days for reading and 14–15 instructional days for math at the elementary level. - Quarterly Assessments QAs will continue alongside MAP because they serve a different purpose — assessing whether the district's written curriculum aligns with actual teaching and helping refine pacing in the scope and sequence. QA4 serves as the final exam at the secondary level grades 7–12 , with options for teachers to add questions and for students to exempt if eligible. Survey Results Discussion - Pilot campuses had to administer both MAP and existing assessments, which generated some negative feedback about time burden. - The primary takeaway was that purpose and communication need improvement — many teachers and administrators did not fully understand the distinct purposes of different assessments. - Positive feedback was received about MAP's ability to show student growth, identify what students are ready to learn next, and provide actionable data for differentiated instruction. TIA Teacher Incentive Allotment Connection Terrell Jackson explained how MAP testing supports the district's TIA expansion: - Current state: 869 eligible TIA assignments at 26 campuses; 384 teachers receiving designations this year - Proposed expansion for 2025–26: An additional ~630 eligible TIA teaching assignments, bringing the total to approximately 1,500+ eligible assignments - Expansion of current campuses to all six TIA categories +280 assignments - Adding campuses with 35% economically disadvantaged students +280 assignments - Adding special education hard-to-fill positions Life Skills, RISE, SDC +90 assignments - MAP should help improve data validation correlation, which could allow more aggressive future expansion - TIA funds come directly from the state to designated teachers; strong data validation is essential - Board members emphasized the importance of accessing TIA money, noting legislators expect districts to use this funding mechanism for teacher pay Board Discussion Highlights - Trustee Dungan thanked the team and highlighted instructional time savings - Trustee Nelson expressed strong support, noting the tool replaces rather than adds tests, saves time, and costs nothing additional covered under existing allotment - Trustee Huey thanked the team for additional mid-year pilot data provided since the last meeting; gave a "shout out" to Dr. Lori Jacobs principal at San Jacinto for her support of the initiative - Trustee Dawson asked about why QAs continue if MAP provides similar insights; was told QAs serve a distinct curriculum-alignment purpose - Trustee May expressed continued concern about the volume of district testing and the pacing element of QAs, and asked about QAs being required as grades. She was assured exemption policies remain in place and that QA4 serves dual purpose as a final exam. - Multiple trustees emphasized that MAP would help unlock TIA funding for teacher pay Vote Motion by Trustee Nelson: To adopt Northwest Evaluation Association Measures of Academic Progress MAP and Growth for the 2025–26 school year. Key points: - Multiple Woodlands-area elementary schools are significantly over capacity Bush, Powell, Buckaloo, Gadus, Mitchell Intermediate - Many schools have limited or no room for additional portables due to tight footprints - Bush Elementary is at 110% capacity and bounded by churches on both sides with no room for portables - Bush needs enrollment relief to begin construction of a gymnasium part of Bond 2023 - Iseler opens at 779 students target was 750 under Scenario 4.1 - Scenario 4.1 impacts the fewest number of currently enrolled students - Iseler was built at 950-seat capacity, consistent with district standards - The community feedback portal was open from September 1 through January; 8 meetings were held in-person and virtual Significant Board Discussion: - Trustee Horton raised concerns about split feeders 5 of 7 elementary schools would split in The Woodlands area under 4.1 , long bus times for students up to an hour reported by parents , and negative impacts on community-building and attendance. - Trustee Horton asked about leaving the Powell-to-Bush and Gadus rezoning off 4.1 to preserve flexibility for future feeder cleanup. Trustee May's Opening Remarks: - Emphasized importance of K-3/6 literacy - Cited Texas Education Code 11.1051 regarding academic oversight - Noted CISD literacy scores are consistently well above state averages and often ahead of peer districts - Referenced the 87th Legislature science of reading/reading academies and 88th Legislature HB 4545/HB 1605 — banned 3-cueing, focused on phonics - Highlighted that approximately 40% of elementary students are not reading on grade level - 50% of Spanish-speaking third graders not meeting second-grade level expectations Dr. Young's Presentation on Bluebonnet Curriculum: Background: - Dr. Young is a career educator 30 years , retired as executive director of student support services and special education - Provided physical copies of Bluebonnet curriculum materials to each board member — teacher guides and student activity books the only board in the state to receive a complete set - Units included: "Serving Our Neighbors," "America Our Great Country," "Fighting For A Cause," "Innovation and Industry and Energy," "Ancient Rome," "American Independence," "Sharing Stories," "Kings and Queens," poetry, and "The Lion, the Witch and the Wardrobe" novel study Key Points Discussed: - Pilot Results: Temple ISD and Luling ISD pilots showed students grew by more than one year in reading capacity during the first semester - Difference from Amplify: Bluebonnet is the Texas version of Amplify Texas, with Texas-specific content, names, stories, and additional materials. The underlying reading system from Amplify is nationally recognized as excellent. - What Makes It Different: Builds on prior knowledge lesson-by-lesson; covers 100% of TEKS vs. previous materials that only needed to cover 55% ; provides everything in one place - Pacing: Teachers in pilot programs have been able to stay on pace with the weekly structure - Religious Content: Approximately 10% cites religious texts; six religions covered Buddhism, Christianity, Hinduism, Islam, Judaism, Sikhism ; religious text is used to support literature, not to teach religion; the Bible is the most referenced book in Western literature - Not Common Core: Would not have passed the SBOE rubric if it contained Common Core elements - Cost: Free to download/access electronically; approximately $8 per student for printed copies; significantly less expensive than typical curriculum materials ~$300 per teacher kit average - Print vs. Digital: Available in both formats; teachers can print, copy, and annotate freely with no copyright restrictions - Parent Access: Includes letters to parents with discussion questions; state law requires online parent portal access - Cross-Curricular Design: Integrates social studies, science vocabulary, and other subject content into reading materials — based on research about benefits of learning to read through social studies text - Teacher Flexibility: New teachers can follow it as written; veteran teachers can supplement and add their own materials; accommodations for IEPs and gifted students are supported - Built-in Assessments: Weekly/unit assessments built into the curriculum, which Dr. Young noted sets it apart from many other curricula - Professional Development: Free training available through TEA; first-come, first-served basis; HISD and Fort Bend ISD also looking to adopt - Over 500 educators contributed to developing the content Comparison with Savvas SAVVAS : - Both Bluebonnet and Savvas are the only two curricula currently approved as comprehensive HQIM High Quality Instructional Materials - Savvas also covers 100% of TEKS per rubric requirements - Dr. Young stated she is not familiar with any pilot behind Savvas - She expressed personal confidence in Bluebonnet based on studying it since 2019 Dr. Young offered to return for future presentations or public hearings on other curriculum options. The department is working toward full coverage at all campuses. - 26 campus control monitors security staff in blue shirts serve as eyes and ears at secondary campuses. - 104 crossing guards serve throughout the county. - The department is a TCOLE contract training provider — one of only 216 out of 2,795 law enforcement agencies in Texas. Officers average approximately 100 hours of training per year state minimum is 40 . - Detectives and select officers are now members of the FBI Threat Mitigation Task Force new this year , providing access to federal databases and cross-jurisdictional information sharing for social media threats. - The department is a 911 answering point one of five in Montgomery County , enabling instant inter-agency communication. - Memoranda of Understanding MOUs exist with every law enforcement agency in Montgomery County for mutual aid. - The department participates in the Montgomery County Special Threat Response Plan — described as the first of its kind in Texas — a multi-jurisdictional active shooter response plan involving law enforcement, fire, and EMS. Community Outreach: - Safety programs, first responder expos, student leadership events - October 26: Cars and Coffee event + Trunk or Treat 91 cars participating - Annual Coats for Kids drive — coats can be dropped off at any campus, the admin building, the police department, or by calling for pickup - Holiday Gift Drive to follow Hiring: - Contact: police.conroeisd.net or call 936-709-8900 for information on becoming a crossing guard, patrol coordinator, or officer. - Committee members noted the extensive vetting process required to hire officers, emphasizing the importance of getting it right. Committee Discussion: - The chair praised school police officers for the special care required when working with children, noting that for many students, school officers may be their first positive interaction with law enforcement . Item 3: Walk-Through Weapons Detection Demonstration Overview Presented by: Mr. McCord, with input from Ethan and Chief The committee received information on three types of weapons detection systems, following up on discussion from the June meeting. Demonstrations were conducted at district facilities. 1. Garrett Walk-Through Metal Detectors Traditional Magnetometer - Currently in use at CISD stadiums - Cost: ~$6,000 per unit no ongoing subscription - Pros: Reasonably priced; no subscription fees; gives general location of detected item high/medium/low - Cons: Heavy; not easily deployable; finicky and requires constant interaction; long reset time between individuals slow throughput ; high false-positive rate; battery power unreliable needs AC ; nearly impossible to find repair technicians; best for indoor use 2. Evolve Machine Learning System - Cost: ~$70,000 for single-lane unit; ~$90,000 for dual-lane; ongoing software fee of $5,425–$7,500/year - Pros: Frictionless no reset time, two people can pass abreast simultaneously ; provides iPad with exact location indicator "red cube" on detected items; heavier/more stable in wind; handles outdoor use; machine learning improves over time - Cons: Very expensive; requires wired AC power; requires dedicated iPad monitor with high focus; heavy - Deployed at: NRG Stadium Texans games, Rodeo , Disney World, Minute Maid Park, The Cynthia Woods Mitchell Pavilion - CISD trial: Deployed at Woodforest Bank Stadium at a College Park football game 3. Open Gate Machine Learning System - Cost: ~$20,000+ per unit; no ongoing subscription optional extended warranty ~$1,600/year - Pros: Frictionless; easily transportable; battery powered uses large rechargeable batteries ; lighter weight; indoor/outdoor capable - Cons: Less stable in wind; battery-only no AC option ; does not provide location of detected item; less visually substantial - CISD trial: Experienced some wind issues during first outdoor deployment Key Takeaways from Discussion: - All systems require personnel to monitor gates plus an armed officer present at each screening point — the Chief strongly emphasized this as a critical safety requirement. - Sensitivity trade-off: Higher detection sensitivity catches more items but increases false positives and slows throughput. The "Holy Grail" is a setting just above the threshold where phones and keys would pass but dangerous items would be caught. - This is research, not purchasing — the district does not currently have allocated funds for these systems. - Potential future use: Home-base units at each high school for random checks; also deployable to stadiums and other campuses as needed. Daily screening of all students would be a significant community decision with structural building implications. - No system is perfect — even schools with daily weapons detection have experienced incidents. Camera-Based Detection Systems: - Previously researched discussed at June meeting ; companies quoted $7 million startup for one system and $400,000–$500,000/year for others. - Significant limitations identified: most systems required weapons to be brandished/visible not concealed ; did not detect edged weapons; limited range. - A committee member identified as a board member who attends Austin hearings requested the list of companies previously reviewed, noting she had seen demonstrations of systems capable of detecting concealed weapons. Staff agreed to provide this information. - A committee member asked about rental/lease/per-event options with third-party security companies providing equipment and personnel; staff noted evolve and open gate offer year-long leases without personnel but a per-event model would need further research. - Durability concerns were raised about transporting sensitive equipment between campuses. Item 3: Update on Planning and Construction Operations Review Presenter: Mr. Robertson external consultant hired to review the district's planning and construction operations for transparency and efficiency, particularly in light of the nearly $2 billion bond construction program Scope of Review: - Organizational structure and resources of the Planning & Construction P&C department - Benchmarking against similar-sized districts in the Greater Houston area student count and capital construction volume - Procurement document compliance with statutes, regulations, and board policy - Selection process for general contractors, architects, and engineers - Interviews with P&C personnel and staff in interfacing departments maintenance, instructional - Attended schematic design meetings, 75% page-turn reviews, and owner-architect-contractor meetings including Conroe High School project Key Findings: 1. Organization & Staffing: - Many project coordinators are recent hires, some new to school construction - Executive Director of P&C is conducting on-the-job training weekly meetings on pay applications, change orders, contractual responsibilities - More development needed before project assignments begin ~October/November timeframe 2. Department Relationships: - Reviewed relationships between P&C and Maintenance critical because maintenance lives with what P&C builds and between P&C and the Instructional side P&C must deliver schools that meet instructional programs 3. Procurement Process: - Found to be fair, compliant, and delivering best value - Results in a diversity of contractors, architects, and engineers — quality companies in each category - Evaluation criteria and point values are widely accepted and appropriate - Review committee is diverse includes Finance, Instructional, and Maintenance representatives — not just P&C , which prevents score manipulation - A committee member asked specifically about the "best value" formula; Mr. Robertson confirmed it is standard and appropriate 4. Delivery Method: - Reviewed 10 projects approved over two years: 7 were Construction Manager at Risk CM at Risk , 3 were Job Order Contracting JOC - JOC is appropriate for kitchen rehabilitation projects $1-3M range - CM at Risk is the primary and appropriate method for multi-year capital construction programs Preliminary Recommendations: 1. Staff Development Already acted upon - Training by construction attorney Michelle Morris Rogers Morris & Grover on CM at Risk contracts, legal obligations, cost of work, general conditions, change requests, allowances - Training already conducted for P&C and other leadership team members - Recommended ongoing periodic reviews with construction attorney - Recommended AGC Associated General Contractors project management training for approximately 4 project coordinators 2. Reclassify Contract Specialist Position In progress - Courtney contract specialist performs well beyond her job description — manages bond funds, contract processes, took on playground certification, working on her degree - Recommended moving from clerical category to professional/administrative level comparable to project coordinators - Position sent to TASB for proper pay scale classification 3. Add Senior Project Manager/Coordinator - Recommended adding someone with a degree in construction science, architecture, or engineering - Market is thin — districts struggle to find qualified candidates - A district to the west is paying project managers $20,000-$30,000 more annually and getting better credentials 4. Executive Director of Operations Position Already acted upon - Board recently approved this position - Frees Mr. McCord to focus on supervising both Maintenance and P&C, enhancing critical department relationships 5. Delivery Method Diversification In progress - Recommended using Competitive Sealed Proposals CSP for some smaller, standalone projects e.g., elementary gym additions, tennis courts - A tennis court project for Conroe High School is already tracking on the CSP path - Mr. Robertson agreed to provide formal guidance on triggers/thresholds for when different delivery methods should be used 6. Fund P&C Administration with Bond Funds Future consideration only - Districts without in-house P&C typically pay program administrators 5-6% of total bond cost - In-house administration is considerably less expensive - Funding staff with bond funds e.g., 75% salary from bond funds would ease general fund pressure and allow flexible staffing - Dr. Null stated clearly this is NOT an option for the current bond — the district promised the community no salaries would be paid from bond funds. This would only be considered for future bonds and would need to be advertised to the community upfront. Timeline: - Full report expected mid to late summer — Mr. Robertson needs to observe more meetings and review additional documents. Two Proposed Co-Sourced Audits with Discussion: 1. CISD Budget Process - Internal auditor would examine controls, procedures, and processes - Weaver SME would benchmark against peer districts and identify best practices - Significant committee discussion: - Chairman Chase questioned the "so what" — the district hasn't significantly missed its budget; questioned whether this is a true key risk area - Noted district already has external feedback that financial processes are "best in class" - Budget ranked 1 on risk assessment partly due to CFO leadership change, which may skew results - Miss Garza finance noted the district essentially does zero-based budgeting and allocates state-determined funding; suggested the real risk may be in PEIMS reporting which generates funding rather than the budget allocation process - Dr. Null suggested the real risk may be in tracking whether the district is leaving money on the table due to inadequate tracking of PEIMS data inputs — noted a new position was created specifically for this - Outcome: Committee agreed to refine the scope through further discussion between Chairman Chase and the internal auditor; may narrow focus to PEIMS reporting and efficiency in actual expenditure line items rather than the budget formulation process itself 2. HR Hiring and Retention - Internal auditor would examine controls background checks, proper pay scale, job history verification - Weaver HR SME would benchmark against peers on hiring/retention practices - Committee was supportive of this audit Other Planned Audits for 2024-2025: | Audit | Timing | Notes | | -| --| -| | Apps Security and Control co-sourced with Weav

Item 1: Approval of Minutes Action: Minutes from the previous Safety and Security meeting November 2023 were considered for approval. Vote: Motion made and seconded. Approved unanimously with no opposition. Item 2: Cell Phone Safety Awareness Initiative Presenter: Ms. Rodriguez and Dr. Medford for cell phone guidelines overview Key Points: - Based on recommendations from the SHAC School Health Advisory Council , the district implemented several cell phone safety awareness efforts: - Ongoing social media campaign educating schools and parents about cell phone safety tips - Community presentations equipping parents with tools to navigate the digital age - Cell Phone Safety Week planned for the 2024–2025 school year similar to Suicide Prevention Week and Red Ribbon Week , led by school counselors to empower students with responsible cell phone usage Cell Phone Guidelines approved by the Board : - PreK–6th grade: Cell phones off and away for the entire school day - Junior High: Cell phones away all day except during lunch and passing periods campus discretion on specifics - High School: Cell phones away except for clear instructional purposes - Junior high and high school guidelines were rolled out at the semester break - Positive feedback received from teachers and parents - Community surveys were conducted during the development process to gather parent input - End-of-year feedback from faculty and community will be gathered to refine guidelines for next year Discussion: The chair noted this was a great example of the system working — an idea originating from the SHAC, flowing to the board, going through a large committee process, and being implemented in buildings. Guidelines are a starting point and may evolve over time. Item 3: Artificial Intelligence Software Exploration Presenters: Ethan Safety staff and Chief Block Background: - Conroe ISD has 4,500+ cameras with approximately 12,000+ camera views/channels - Five AI vendors were evaluated for potential integration with the district's camera systems Vendors Reviewed: | Vendor | Key Details | Pricing | | --| | | | 21st Century AI | 90% camera coverage integration | $7.8 million upfront; 10% annual maintenance for 5 years; $10,000 per campus installation | | Omnilert | Initial meeting held; vendor stopped responding after January 5 | No final pricing received | | Actuate | Pricing per camera per month | ~$18/camera/month weapons detection only ; estimated ~$500,000+/year for district | | Zero Eyes | Tiered pricing by camera streams | 5-year: $2.6M annually; 3-year: $3.15M annually; plus $15,000 platform fee possibly per campus | | Drift Net proprietary | Installs own camera hardware; includes visitor management could replace Raptor ; claims ability to detect edged weapons/knives | $400,000–$550,000/year; 3-year contract: $1.24M–$1.7M | Key Findings & Concerns: - All systems require a weapon to be visibly brandished — they cannot detect concealed weapons in backpacks, under clothing, etc. - Four of the five vendors offer substantially similar technology; Drift Net is the proprietary outlier - Systems can generate false positives from BB guns, airsoft guns, toy weapons, etc. - A human element is always required — alerts must be vetted by a person before response - By the time AI detects a brandished weapon, the threat is already active — these systems do not prevent attacks , they enable faster notification and response - Network concerns raised about Drift Net's proprietary cameras potentially taxing the district's network - Data privacy concerns raised regarding who owns/stores video when using external camera systems, particularly with students involved Weapons Detection Systems walk-through type : - Brief discussion of weapons detection systems similar to those at Astros games - Estimated cost: ~$20,000 per movable unit - Significant staffing requirements — each unit needs dedicated personnel, ideally police officers - Challenge of large high schools with 120+ exterior doors noted No action taken — informational item only. Item 4: Staying Safe School Safety Response Training Texas School Safety Center Presenters: Ethan and Chief Block Background: - At the committee's direction, Ethan and Chief Block attended the "Staying Safe" training at the Region 6 Service Center in Huntsville, provided by the Texas School Safety Center. Key Findings: - The curriculum aims to empower students in active shooter response situations - Uses familiar framework Run/Hide/Fight or Avoid/Deny/Defend but changes "Defend" to "Disrupt" - The "Disrupt" concept would be introduced at the intermediate and junior high levels and up not elementary - The curriculum encourages students to think critically and make independent decisions during a crisis - Would facilitate small group discussions where teachers guide students on how to respond, including the "fight/disrupt" option Concerns Raised: - Language change from "Defend" to "Disrupt" could create confusion with the existing Standard Response Protocol - The presenter themselves inconsistently used terminology , creating confusion during the training - Does not include all five phases of emergency management — focused only on response, not prevention or mitigation - Does not include parent involvement — many parents would likely have strong opinions about their children being taught these concepts - Concern about asking too much of teachers — could become traumatic for staff and students even in the training context - Cognitive ability concerns — questioning whether intermediate-age students can appropriately analyze crisis situations and make independent decisions - Risk of independent student action potentially conflicting with teacher instructions and endangering others e.g., a student trying to flee while a teacher directs lockdown - Other districts at the training table shared similar reservations - The program may be better suited for districts without robust existing safety infrastructure e.g., rural districts without dedicated police departments Recommendation: - Presenters and committee expressed that this program is not a good fit for Conroe ISD at this time given its infancy - May warrant revisiting in 4–5 years after further development and implementation elsewhere - Some elements could potentially be incorporated into existing training Mandatory Status: - The Texas School Safety Center made clear this training is voluntary, not mandatory , and there is no indication it will become mandatory No formal action taken — informational item. Item 5: Intruder Detection Audits for 2023–2024 School Year Presenter: Mr. McCord Key Points: - This is the second year of intruder detection audits - The district has 65 physical buildings ; 58 have been audited as of the meeting date - 7 remaining buildings will be audited in May - Results: 57 out of 58 audited buildings had no findings 98.27% success rate - The one finding occurred in November and was previously discussed specifics reviewed in prior executive session Notable Highlights: - No executive session was needed for this item — all information could be shared in open session - Results have exceeded expectations — described as "unbelievable" and "transformative" - Students have been exemplary — not a single instance of a student letting an unauthorized person into a building through a door - Credit given to students, staff, teachers, administrators, and maintenance crews for persistent effort despite the inconvenien

WORKSHOP SESSION Called to order: 7:04 PM Public Comment: No one signed up. Item 1: Employee Benefits Plan for 2024-2025 Presented by: Karen Garza CFO , with Derek Ekko Gallagher, health plan consultant and Tiffany Mattfeld Assistant Director of HR — Benefits Background - CISD operates a self-funded health plan , which gives the district more control over plan design, reserves, providers, and network selection compared to a fully-insured plan or TRS. - The district does not participate in TRS ActiveCare due to restrictive movement rules 5-year commitment , lack of control over rates/providers, and questionable financial history. - Stop-loss policy: $550,000 individual deductible; $5 million aggregate limit. - District contribution: $446/employee/month vs. state average of $225/month. Plan Performance - The health plan ended FY23 with a $7.8 million fund balance ~14% of annual expenditures . - Year-to-date surplus of approximately $2.4 million expected to decrease during high-utilization summer months . - Now in Year 3 with Blue Cross Blue Shield ; the HMO model has proven very successful. - The High Deductible plan runs at ~$412/employee/month; the Premier HMO runs better than the Blue Essentials HMO due to the narrower network and quality-focused care. Budget Projection for FY25 - Projected incurred claims: ~$53 million - Total fixed costs: ~$492,000 - Annual budget projection: ~$53.6 million - Projected budget surplus: ~$1.4 million current year budget was ~$55 million - RX rebate guarantees increased through the recent pharmacy RFP with Prime. Employee Benefits Committee Process - Met March 6 initial plan design review and March 26 final review and vote . - Unanimous vote from the committee to bring recommendations forward to the board. Recommended Plan Enhancements using ~$1.1 million of the $1.4 million surplus All Plans: - Add hearing aid coverage : $1,000 maximum per 35 months - Add cochlear implant coverage Blue Choice PPO High Deductible Health Plan : - Add $500 annual Health Savings Account HSA contribution per enrolled employee Blue Essentials HMO: - Reduce out-of-pocket maximum from $7,350/$14,700 to $7,000/$14,000 - Adjust durable medical equipment from 50% co-insurance to match plan co-insurance level Blue Premier HMO most significant enhancements : - Primary care co-pay reduced from $35 to $20 - Annual deductible reduced from $1,200/$3,600 to $900/$2,700 - Out-of-pocket maximum reduced from $6,250/$12,500 to $6,000/$12,000 - Durable medical equipment adjusted to match plan co-insurance Premiums - No premium increases recommended — premiums remain the same as current year. - Total estimated plan funding district + employee contributions : ~$54.6 million. District Benchmarking - CISD compares favorably to neighboring and peer districts on contributions and plan design. - Humble ISD's lower costs were explained as resulting from a reference-based pricing model that can expose employees to balance billing. - Enhancements are designed to make CISD competitive with TRS districts and surrounding districts. Board Discussion Highlights - Trustee Williams expressed concern about whether employees focus on premiums rather than plan enhancement details, arguing that reducing premiums would put more money directly in employees' pockets and help with retention/attraction. He drew an analogy to truck drivers who focus on take-home pay. - Dr. Null noted anecdotally that employees often hyperfocus on salary rather than insurance value, and that some leave CISD for a $500 raise elsewhere only to pay $1,200 more in insurance premiums. He also noted that network/doctor availability is what employees really care about. - Staff confirmed virtual/telehealth visits remain free under all plans. - Discussion about infertility treatment coverage — acknowledged as a real need but described as "incredibly expensive" compared to the very low cost of adding hearing aid coverage. - The hearing aid coverage addition was partly prompted by a 2017 state bill that required many plans to cover it, though CISD's self-funded plan is not subject to that mandate; employees had been receiving incorrect information from community providers. Action: Formal approval vote scheduled for the April 16 regular board meeting . Item 2: Budget Overview for 2024-2025 Presented by: Karen Garza CFO Financial Reporting Awards - Texas Comptroller Transparency Stars Traditional Finances, Debt Obligations, Contracts/Procurements - TASBO Excellence in Financial Management one of 32
