Meeting Called to Order - Time: 6:02 PM - Quorum confirmed ; notice posted in accordance with the Texas Open Meetings Act - Public Comment: No citizens signed up to address the board - Meeting adjourned: 8:43 PM Item 1: No Action — No Public Comments No members of the public signed up to speak. Item 2: GMP Process for Construction Manager at Risk CMAR Projects Presenter: Mr. Foster Construction/Facilities Overview of the GMP Development Process Mr. Foster explained how the Guaranteed Maximum Price GMP is generated for CMAR projects, addressing a question from a previous board meeting about why the district shares budget information with contractors. Key points: - Bond Planning: The district uses benchmark projects typically elementary schools built repeatedly to develop cost estimates based on historical costs, industry data, and current market conditions. These estimates are presented to the bond committee, then to the board, and ultimately to voters. - All-in Costs: Bond amounts voters approve include design, construction, and outfitting for teaching — not just construction alone. - CMAR Selection Process: After architects are selected, the district advertises for a CMAR. Up to five firms can be invited the legal maximum . Firms compete on three fee components: 1. Preconstruction Services Fee — A lump sum typically ~$20,000–$30,000 for an elementary for working with architects during design, doing constructability reviews, and estimating. 2. Construction Fee — The firm's corporate profit margin percentage of construction cost . 3. General Conditions — Monthly costs for superintendent, project manager, job trailer, portable toilets, etc. ~40-50 line items provided by the district for apples-to-apples comparison . - Current Market Conditions: Fees from competing firms have been very close together within ~1% spread on larger projects, ~2% on smaller ones . The market is highly competitive. - Example: Two projects received fees today — one ~$30 million and one ~$13 million construction target. Historical elementary school construction fees have been around 1.5%. - Evaluation: Price accounts for 20 out of 100 points in the selection scoring. The lowest bidder receives 20 points; others are proportionally reduced based on distance from the low bid. All three fee components are combined into the price score. Collaborative Design and Bidding - Once the team is assembled, the district, architect, and CMAR work collaboratively on plans. The contractor's presence allows real-time cost feedback on design options. - When plans are sufficiently complete, trade work is competitively bid. CISD handles advertisement; bids are submitted to the CMAR with a CISD representative present. The district retains copies of all bids. - January Example: Two elementary schools were bid on January 12 with Marshall Construction. After bid day "scrubbing," both projects are meeting their budget targets, with potential to come in under what was promised to voters. Financial Accountability - Mr. Foster emphasized post-construction financial review as his "favorite part": every charge is reviewed by CISD, internal audit, and external auditors before final payment. Any charge not benefiting the district can be struck. Board Discussion - Trustee Odenweller asked several clarifying questions about the fee structure, confirming that the construction fee is the primary profit margin and that general conditions include markup not strictly at-cost . - Discussion clarified that the CMAR contract is not a cost-plus arrangement. - Board members expressed surprise at the relatively low profit margins around 1.5% , which Mr. Foster confirmed is typical in the current competitive market. Item 3: Budget Update and Forecast Presenters: Mr. Darren Rice CFO , Ms. Garza, Mr. Reeves Recognition of Mr. Rice Dr. Null publicly acknowledged that Mr. Rice has announced his retirement effective end of summer 2024. Dr. Null praised his long career and accomplishments, including CISD being the first school district to receive all Transparency Stars and maintaining a low tax rate while supporting student success. 88th Legislative Session Recap What the Legislature Did: - Passed school safety bill HB 3 — provided small additional safety funding - Passed property tax relief bill SB 2 — 10.7 cents additional M&O compression + homestead exemption increase from $40K to $100K ~$18 billion cost to the state - This was a shift in funding contribution from local districts to the state — no net new revenue for school districts What the Legislature Did NOT Do: - No additional funding for employee raises - No additional funding to address inflation/rising education costs - Various models during the session projected anywhere from $50 to $350 increase in the basic allotment every $100 increase = ~$30 million for CISD , but none passed - Voucher issues were a significant impediment to school finance legislation passing 2023-2024 Budget Recap Budget Challenges at Adoption: - Unprecedented growth 3,000+ students in prior two years - No state funding mechanism for raises or inflation - ESSER funding cliff: ~225 positions $16 million absorbed into general fund - Certified property values: $54.9 billion 5.26% growth, accounting for new $100K homestead exemption - Tax rate: $0.9621 15.25 cent decrease - Balanced budget adopted Enrollment Variance: - Enrollment came in approximately 900 students short of projections - However, weighted funding from growth in special education, bilingual, and economically disadvantaged populations offset the revenue shortfall - Dr. Null noted predicting PreK and Kindergarten enrollment is nearly impossible since those students aren't yet in the system Expenditure Concerns Potential $23.5 Million Over Budget : | Item | Amount | | | --| | November 2023 employee raise no state funding | $15 million | | Payroll accrual adjustment will reverse next year | $4 million | | Teacher Incentive Allotment growth 95 new teachers; pass-through with offsetting revenue | — | | Additional special education personnel | $1.5 million | | Montgomery CAD fee increase operations + software | $1 million | | Property insurance increase | Included | | Total potential overage | ~$23.5 million | - Beginning unassigned fund balance: $153.8 million - Projected end-of-year unassigned fund balance: ~$130.4 million Fund Balance Analysis: - Target 20% of budget : $134.5 million - Projected balance at 8/31/24: ~$130.4 million below target - If projected forward to end of 2024-25 with recurring $15M raise cost: ~$19 million below the 20% target not accounting for any other increases Possible Solutions: 1. Legislative action most permanent solution 2. Return bond contingency fund $18.8 million moved to capital projects for Conroe HS potential overage — Mr. Foster's team believes savings/interest within the bond program can cover that overage, allowing the funds to return to general fund. However, this is one-time money. 3. Weighted funding offsets ~$6.5 million Medicaid/SHARS Funding Reduction - CISD participates in the SHARS School Health and Related Services program for Medicaid reimbursement - Projected ~$10 million in Medicaid revenue this year not included in regular operating budget due to program instability - $5 million in expenditures charged against this revenue primarily special education positions - In December, the state announced a $300 million statewide reduction due to a failed 2017 audit appeal lost

Vote: Motion to accept the audit report and recommend it to the full board for approval. - Result: Passed unanimously. Item 2: Nomination and Selection of Audit Committee Chair for 2024 - A committee member nominated the current chair Chairman Chase for re-election, citing his leadership and excellent performance. - The nomination was seconded. Vote: Motion to re-elect Chairman Chase as audit committee chair for 2024. - Result: Passed unanimously including the chair voting for himself, which was noted as "awkward" . Vote: Motion to approve the September 13, 2023 minutes. - Result: Passed unanimously. Item 5: Status Update on 2023–2024 Internal Audit Plan Internal Audit staff presented progress on the current audit plan. Key highlights: Activity Fund Audits - Three audits complete. - The auditor who handles activity funds also manages PTO support work, so August–December is PTO-focused and the second half of the year shifts to activity fund audits. This is the expected annual pattern. Payroll Audit - A deep dive was conducted, timed with a personnel transition Rachel taking over from Becky . - Finding: The overall control environment is very strong . The primary issue was that controls were in place but not formally documented e.g., sign-offs and dates not consistently recorded . Eleven controls have now been formalized. - Flowcharts were created for all core payroll functions, which will aid future training and audits. - The committee chair expressed comfort with the results, noting payroll is a prime area for potential issues. Accounts Payable Audit - Strong control environment found overall. - Flowcharts were created noted as especially helpful given an upcoming retirement in February . - Finding: Four people had access to change MCC Merchant Category Codes on travel cards; this was reduced to only two authorized individuals the CFO and the head of the travel department to ensure proper segregation of duties. - Travel Card System Explained: Conroe ISD does not use traditional credit cards. Instead, they use pre-loaded, one-time-use travel cards restricted to hotel purchases only. This system was implemented because hotels stopped accepting checks, and the district wanted to avoid the risks associated with open-ended credit cards. Cards are reconciled monthly against bank statements and monitored daily. Maintenance and Custodial Follow-Up Audit - All previously identified opportunities for improvement have been remediated and closed. - Inventory check at the new warehouse: 25 for 25 100% accuracy . - Two prior observations addressed: - Paper accident reports eliminated; now electronic only. - Attic stock leftover construction materials — tiles, paint, doors, etc. consolidation into the warehouse is ongoing, expected to be complete by August . Internal audit will verify controls once consolidation is done. New Warehouse Facility Presentation Marshall, Maintenance - Marshall provided an overview of the new consolidated maintenance/custodial warehouse: - Significantly larger than the old facility from ~40x40 to ~70x125 . - Consolidated three separate warehouses into one location with one inventory manager. - Strong physical controls: segregated parking lots, roll-up door access restricted by accordion barriers, camera coverage everywhere, limited access only coordinators and assistant directors for after-hours . - Items are signed out at a counter; issued to truck pools, then to work orders. - Lawn crew bay design: After a prior theft incident ~6-7 years ago, six trucks/trailers and lawnmowers stolen, later recovered via GPS , the new facility was designed so trucks pull directly into a secured metal building — eliminating the need for daily loading/unloading, saving approximately 30 minutes each morning and afternoon. All behind fenced areas with cameras. - Committee members were encouraged to visit the facility. Upcoming Audits - Cybersecurity Risk Assessment: Weaver conducting a boots-on-the-ground assessment kick-off meeting the following day . Previous year was a general plan review; this year will verify actual practices match stated procedures. - Field trips: Starting the following week. - Athletic ticketing: Q2 confirmed that cash has not yet been fully eliminated . - Child Nutrition follow-up: Q2. - Band inventory: Every other year cycle; this is an "on" year. - Print shop audit and Construction audit also planned. 2024–2025 Audit Plan Preview - The next committee meeting will include discussion of the FY 2024–2025 internal audit plan . - A risk assessment will be issued around April , listing departments/areas weighted by risk. - Committee members were asked to think about areas they would like internal audit to examine and submit suggestions. PTO/PTA and Booster Club Oversight Discussion - PTA vs. PTO: PTA groups join the national PTA Association with associated fees ; PTOs function independently. Both are parent-teacher groups. - Key distinction: PTO/PTA funds are not district funds . They belong to the individual organizations. - The district helps PTOs set up IRS filings, tax-exempt status under the CISD Educational Support Group umbrella, EIN numbers and provides training on fundraising rules and financial reporting. An outside person assists with IRS filings twice a year. - Principals provide oversight — ensuring proper bylaws, treasurer reports, and appropriate use of funds. District staff conducts a detailed financial overview for treasurers at the start of each year. - The district does not directly audit PTO/PTA funds and cannot direct how they spend their money, but PTOs function at the district's discretion. - Booster clubs operate under a separate structure from PTOs/PTAs. The district provides guidance but does not have direct oversight of their accounts. It was noted that high school booster clubs e.g., band booster clubs with 400+ members can handle millions of dollars in funds. Item 6: Next Scheduled Meeting - Confirmed: Wednesday, May 8, 2024 at 7:30 a.m. - A question was raised about whether the committee needed to meet before May regarding upcoming bond issuances. The response was that bond sales go directly to the full board — a bond sale was occurring that same day, and the next sale wouldn't be until the following year. No additional committee meeting was deemed necessary. Meeting Adjournment Vote: Motion to adjourn. - Result: Passed una

Item 1: Bond Program Timeline Overview Presenter: Dr. Null Superintendent Dr. Null provided a high-level timeline for Bond 2023: - Summer 2022: Facility assessments began - Late 2022: Bond Planning Committee formed - January 2023: Early design process started for initial buildings - February–Summer 2023: Bond Planning Committee work and community engagement - November 2023: Election — Propositions A, B, and C all passed, totaling approximately $1.97 billion - March 2024: Visible construction begins at new building sites - August 2025: Two new schools open - 2027: New high school opens - Summer 2028: All bond projects completed Dr. Null noted the district is approximately one-third of the way through the overall bond timeline. Item 2: Introduction of External Review of Planning & Construction Presenter: Dr. Null, with introduction of Robert Robertson Senior Associate, N2 Learning Purpose Rather than hiring an external auditor to review projects after completion as in past bonds , the district engaged an external reviewer to evaluate processes and department capacity during the bond program. This was coordinated with the Audit Committee chair, Mrs. Chase. Two key questions for the review: 1. Are the district's procurement and construction processes yielding the best possible prices? 2. Are current systems and staffing scalable to handle the significantly larger scope of Bond 2023? Mr. Robertson's Background - 23 years in outsourced facility management Corporate America, higher ed, and K-12 - 11 years as Associate Superintendent of Facilities in a neighboring school district - 1 year as Chief of Staff for that district - Retired 2022; now consulting with N2 Learning - Has known Mr. Foster CISD Planning & Construction Director for approximately 15 years Current Status of Review - Reviewing procurement processes, contracts, general conditions, pay applications - Gained access to ProjectMates software to review individual project documentation - Analyzing organizational chart, staffing qualifications, and benchmarking against similar-sized districts - Staff interviews scheduled for January 2024 - Currently in data collection and analysis phase Key Exchange - Trustee Heller asked how deep the audit goes regarding subcontractor invoices. Mr. Robertson clarified this is not a financial audit — he is reviewing processes and procedures, not auditing individual subcontractor invoices. - Mr. Robertson noted he will provide interim findings to the Audit Committee as sections are completed, and will flag any compliance issues immediately. So far, everything reviewed has been in line with regulations and statute. Item 3: Financial Overview — Bond Sales Process Presenters: Mr. Rice CFO and Mr. John Robuck District Financial Advisor since 2004 Bond Sale Timeline - Preliminary Official Statement POS : ~80-90 page document describing bond terms, district information, and investment considerations; distributed to thousands of potential investors - Permanent School Fund Guarantee: Application submitted to Texas PSF for $1,500; provides AAA bond insurance rating, lowering borrowing costs significantly - Rating Agency Calls: Scheduled with S&P and Moody's to review district financials, enrollment trends, taxable values, and growth - December 12 Board Meeting: Board will be asked to approve a Parameter Order — a bond order with flexibility parameters allowing bonds to be sold at optimal market timing without requiring a board meeting on sale day - Designates pricing officers Board President, Secretary, Superintendent, or CFO - Sets maximum amount and interest rate - Valid for approximately 180 days - December 29: Print final offering document - January 10, 2024 tentative : Bond sale date - February 6, 2024: Bond closing; district receives funds First Bond Sale Details - Amount: $595 million - Anticipated second sale next year : ~$700 million Who Buys District Bonds? Mr. Robuck explained that approximately 1,000 different firms may review the bonds, including mutual funds, insurance companies e.g., State Farm, Travelers , bond trading firms, other school districts, and individual residents. CISD has a priority policy giving residents first access to purchase bonds. 2023 Refunding Bond Results - Refunded $52.6 million in outstanding bonds - Sold $51 million in new refunding bonds at 4.28% all-in cost - Savings: $3.1 million net present value of 4.31% - Approximately $200,000 savings per year - No term extension — refunded bonds and new bonds both mature in 2039 - Closed November 21, 2023 Market Conditions Rates have dropped 40-50 basis points in the last two weeks, which is favorable for the upcoming January bond sale. Item 4: Planning & Construction Overview Presenter: Mr. Easy Foster, Director of Planning & Construction Department Staffing 11 total including Foster Key new/expanded positions: - Rebecca Cronza Hicks — Assistant Director for Planning new, 1 month with district; background in design firms; bridges curriculum/instruction language with architectural design - Ryan Adbury — Assistant Director for Construction contractor/surety background; ~2 years with district - 5.5 Project Coordinators on-site management - Courtney Rogers — Contract Specialist manages all financial transactions - Irene — Administrative support Dr. Null emphasized the importance of splitting planning and construction roles and noted that Ms. Cronza Hicks will serve as a consistent "voice of the schools" in the design process, supplementing assistant superintendents who are stretched thin. Trustee Heller raised concern about whether the lean staff 11 people is sufficient and noted the risk of losing institutional knowledge if one person leaves. Dr. Null acknowledged this is something Mr. Robertson may address in his review. Governing Codes & Jurisdictions The department operates under Education Code, Administrative Code, Government Code, Occupations Code, local board policy, and multiple municipal building codes City of Conroe, Oak Ridge, Shenandoah, The Woodlands Township, Montgomery County, South Montgomery County . Notable: As the district grows southward past Grand Oaks High School, it is approaching the City of Houston's extra-territorial jurisdiction ETJ , which extends beyond Harris County into Montgomery County. Houston has already begun monitoring CISD construction activity. Project Delivery Methods Board Policy CV Local establishes Construction Manager at Risk CM at Risk as the default best-value delivery method for projects over $100,000. Other methods competitive bidding, CSP, design-build, job order contracting may be used when recommended and approved by the board. Significant Discussion: CM at Risk vs. CSP Competitive Sealed Proposals Trustee Heller raised extensive questions about CSP vs. CM at Risk, noting she had spoken with board members from approximately eight other districts who use CSP and reported positive experiences, including receiving money back on CSP contracts. Mr. Foster's key points defending CM at Risk: - Open book accounting: All costs are transparent and subject to audit; if a project comes in under budget, the district can recoup savings - CSP contracts are typically lump-sum and not inherently subject to the same financial transparency - Partnership mode

Key Discussion Points: - The training would be a 6-hour train-the-trainer session held at a regional center Huntsville . - At least three staff members would need to attend tiered for high school, junior high, and elementary implementation . - Attending the training does not commit the district to implementing the program. Multiple districts have declined the program after review. - Chief Blakelock emphasized the importance of ensuring that if students are trained, it does not undermine teachers' ability to control and direct students during an emergency. - Concerns were raised about overtraining students at secondary campuses where threats are typically internal, potentially giving bad actors insight into response protocols. - No students would attend the training — this is for adult district staff only. Item 3: Bond Funding for Districtwide Safety and Security Presenters: Mr. McCord, Mr. Foster, Dr. Winkler Overview of Funding Sources Bond Funds: - $35 million allocated for safety and security in the November 2023 bond referendum. - Covers approximately 75 campuses over four years. - Key limitation: Bond funds cannot be used for personnel/salary — only capital improvements. - Reminder that even $35 million will not cover everything; decisions on priorities are necessary. State Grant — School Safety Standards Grant: - Awarded $2.8 million spring 2023 — used to encapsulate 27 elementary schools with 6-foot black galvanized slatted fencing and made progress on 3 additional schools. State Grant — SAFE Grant Cycle 1: - Awarded $10.1 million recently announced . - Planned uses: - Install impact-resistant film on all exterior glass doors and immediate adjacent windows district-wide all schools — achieving full Chapter 61 compliance well ahead of the August 2024 deadline. - Remaining funds to complete 6-foot slatted fencing at all remaining elementary and intermediate schools. - SAFE Grant Cycle 2 expected in spring 2024 formulaic, non-competitive ; district hoping for approximately $1 million. Chapter 61 Compliance Commissioner's Rules — School Facilities - Effective August 2024, all exterior perimeter doors with glass inserts and immediately adjacent windows must have either impact-resistant film or be behind a secure enclosed area. - The fencing creates anti-scaling enclosed areas not full perimeter — typically back/side of schools . - Egress gates are required for emergency exit. Impact-Resistant Film Testing: - Before demolishing an end-of-life building at Conroe High School, the district applied the film and had officers test it with metal bars, rocks, and other tools. - Officers found it virtually impossible to breach in a timely manner — described as phenomenal in delaying unauthorized entry. Secure Corridors Discussion Dr. Winkler - Current locked-door policy creates isolation for teachers and students, reduces instructional time lost minutes unlocking/relocking doors , and limits collaboration during intervention periods. - New elementary prototype features secured "pods" by grade level with keycarded entry points, interior bathrooms, and visibility. - Some existing campuses e.g., Ford Elementary, Haley Elementary could be retrofitted with this concept relatively easily. - Benefits: Added security layer, improved community/collaboration, better temperature regulation, increased instructional time. AI-Based Weapons Detection Discussion Raised by: Jason committee member - Described Zero Eyes and similar AI weapons detection software that overlays on existing camera systems. - Technology detects weapons via AI, then a human reviewer confirms before triggering alerts ~3-second detection . - Cost: ~$100 per camera subscription-based ; estimated ~$150,000/year per high school campus. - District has approximately 15,000 cameras currently used primarily for recording/deterrent — not actively monitored in real-time at most campuses. - Multiple vendors exist Zero Eyes, 21st Century AI, and 12+ others . - Bond funds cannot be used for software subscriptions — would need to come from operating budget or potentially grant funds. - Committee expressed strong interest in exploring further. Action Items No Formal Votes — Direction Given : - Mr. Foster received direction to proceed with planning for: - 100% exterior door access control on new builds and retrofits - Secure corridor retrofits at applicable campuses - Continuing camera lifecycle replacements - Parking lot gates - Communications equipment lifecycle management - District will explore AI weapons detection vendors through proper procurement/RFP process; potential subcommittee may be formed. - Staff to investigate whether grant funds could offset subscription-based costs. EISO Evaluating and Improving Student Outcomes Board Training — a state-required training conducted by John Connley from Region 6 Education Service Center 2. Board Workshop — presentations on district accountability data, ERG performance analysis, and STAAR results with departmental next steps Part 1: EISO Board Training Afternoon Session Presenter: John Connley, Region 6 Mr. Connley delivered the state-mandated EISO training, which board members must complete every two years. Three board members newer members had already received this training months earlier; the remaining four were trained at this session. Mr. Connley noted that an updated version of the EISO training is forthcoming from the state but has not yet been released. Key Discussion: Training Timing Board President Scott Hubert expressed frustration that the full board was not trained together, stating he wished all seven trustees could have gone through the training as a group. He noted the board's responsibilities have shifted "drastically" in recent years, with trustees being pulled into more areas like safety policy. Dr. Null Superintendent explained the scheduling was driven by different expiration windows — new members had a required window after being seated, while veteran members' training didn't expire until October. The expectation was that the state would release updated training before this session, which would have allowed a full-board retraining together. Trustee Datren Williams pushed back on the idea that board roles have changed, stating: "Our roles have not changed. I think people have a misconception of what those roles are." He argued that board members sometimes try to "creep into the day-to-day activities" which should be avoided, emphasizing the board's role is oversight, not implementation. President Hubert agreed the role is oversight but noted the board has been given additional responsibilities over time, particularly around safety. Both trustees agreed the board should maintain a 50,000-foot view. Training Content Highlights - Adult behavior's impact on student outcomes — Research over 25 years shows teacher, principal, superintendent, and more recently school board behavior significantly influences student achievement. - Mindset — Emphasized as a critical factor for educators and leaders. - STAAR Testing — Reviewed sample 3rd-grade reading and math questions, noting the reading comprehension demands embedded in math questions. Trustee Nelson highlighted vocabulary challenges for students. - A-through-F Accountability System: - Three domains: Student Achievement Domain 1 , School Progress Domain 2 , and Closing the Gaps Domain 3 - 70% = better of Domain 1 or Domain 2; 30% = Domain 3 - New change: Six performance levels instead of four Masters, Meets, High Approaches, Low Approaches, High Did Not Meet, Low Did Not Meet - New change: Larger campuses now contribute more to district weighting proportional to enrollment - CCMR cut point raised from 60% to 88%, generating significant pushback from districts statewide - Ratings delayed until later in October originally expected in September - College, Career, and Military Readiness CCMR : - College readiness: TSI criteria, AP scores of 3+, IB scores of 4+, dual credit 9+ hours , or UT OnRamps - Career readiness: Industry certifications, CTE coherent sequences, special education completers - Military readiness: Now requires actual enlistment previously just "interest" , ASVAB passage, and enlistment after graduation - Question from trustees about whether 17-year-old graduates who can't enlist until 18 receive credit — deferred to Dr. Povich for later in the meeting - Relative Performance Domain 2B : Adjusts for economically disadvantaged populations; Trustee Williams asked whether this effectively lowers the bar for disadvantaged students. Mr. Connley clarified it measures opportunity, not aptitude, and expectations same test remain the same. - Statewide 60x30TX Goals: By 2030, at least 60% of Texans ages 25-34 should hold a certificate or degree. Progress report shows 48.6% as of 2021 educated population and student debt metric trending the wrong direction 49%, down from 55% . - District Goals: HB 3 requires three goals — early childhood literacy, early childhood math proficiency, and CCMR. Recommended districts maintain 3-5 total goals to maximize effectiveness. Part 2: Board Workshop Evening Session, beginning ~6:09 PM Citizens Participation No one signed up to speak. Presentation 1: ERG Performance Analysis Presenter: Heather Tower, President of ERG ERG is a third-party Performance Management tool not an accountability system that has been operating since 1999 and is based in the Montgomery area. Key points: - ERG measures both effectiveness and efficiency , as required by Texas statute. - Uses relative performance measures — ranks districts against peers, adjusted for demographics economically disadvantaged population . - 87% of Texas districts are rated A or B under the state system, making it difficult to identify truly high performers. - ERG's balanced scorecard: 20% each for Approaches/Meets/Masters, 10% graduation rates, 10% each for TSI graduates, SAT, and ACT. - Conroe ISD Rankings 2022 data : - Effectiveness Index: 90.9th percentile, ranked 19 among the 200 largest districts - Productivity efficiency + performance : 97.5th percentile, ranked 6 effectively 5 after removing NISD data error - Conroe has been in the top tier consistently year after year - ERG noted Conroe outperforms neighboring districts that the state accountability system rates similarly. - Trustee Nelson asked about data sources — all data comes directly from TEA; ERG does not modify data even when errors are identified e.g., NISD misreported economically disadvantaged data . - Dr. Null confirmed the district contracts with ERG for deeper campus-level data, but ERG's district rankings are based solely on TEA data regardless of contract status. Presentation 2: State Accountability System Overview Presenter: Dr. Bethany Medford, Deputy Superintendent Brief overview of the accountability system structure Domains 1, 2A, 2B, and 3 — consistent with earlier EISO training content. Key notes: - TEA has delayed official accountability ratings release. - District rating is capped if any single campus receives a D or lower — this has impacted Conroe in the past. - Domain 3 Closing the Gaps, 30% examines: all students, lowest race/ethnicity group, second lowest, and the "super group" economically disadvantaged, emergent bilinguals, special education, and highly mobile students . - The transition table for measuring growth awards 1 point for students maintaining Meets/Masters, 0.5 points for maintaining in certain lower categories, and 0 for regression. - Conroe High School ~5,300 students accounts for approximately 10% of the entire district's accountability score. Presentation 3: School Improvement Department Presenter: Jeff Fuller, Executive Director of School Improvement - Department is relatively new with a small team 3 people plus data coaches and assessment staff . - Key lessons learned: - Without proper monitoring, schools can decline quickly - Administrators need support with observation and feedback cycles - 19 new principals and 64 new assistant principals hired in the last 2 years - Three tiers of campus support: - High support: 12 campuses — intensive involvement from deputy superintendent, assistant superintendent, and school improvement team - Medium support: 8 campuses — trending upward - Monitor: 4 campuse

Item 3: Status of 2022–2023 Internal Audit Plan and 2023–2024 Internal Audit Plan 2022–2023 Audit Plan Complete Activity Fund Audits: All completed. Katy Creek Upgrade Construction Audit: - Backend audit reviewing numbers against invoices, subcontractor contracts, etc. - CISD Planning & Construction was found to have great controls over their financials. - One observation noted: 2 out of 37 subcontractor pay applications lacked detailed information sufficient for an outside auditor to determine what costs were for. P&C staff understood the items, but documentation was insufficient for independent review. - Discussion about whether P&C can maintain this quality level if the district scales up with a potential new bond. The internal auditor suggested it would be beneficial to have an outside expert verify that materials ordered match what is delivered. PEIMS Attendance Audit: - Conducted annually to ensure attendance is taken accurately, as state revenue depends on it. - Methodology was refined this year: Missing submissions were cross-checked to confirm the teacher was actually present that day substitutes take manual attendance that doesn't register in the electronic system . - Sample: 10 schools over 5 different dates. - Error definition: Attendance taken more than one hour after the designated time when students are no longer present or truly missing submissions where the teacher was present. - Results: - ~4,600 class instances reviewed - 413 errors attendance taken outside the one-hour window - 214 actual missing submissions teacher present, attendance not taken - Overall error rate: 13.5% — an improvement from the prior year - High schools had higher error rates than elementary and junior high, attributed to the fact that high school teachers must take attendance multiple times per day 4–7 periods , making it more burdensome. - Some high schools now use a special bell at the official attendance time as a reminder. - Automated solutions e.g., smart ID tags/scanners were briefly discussed but deemed cost-prohibitive and potentially problematic tracking concerns, lost cards, etc. . - Dr. Null requested that this data be shared with assistant superintendents and principals, noting that principals are competitive and seeing campus-level comparisons could help motivate improvement. 2023–2024 Audit Plan In Progress Audits planned for the current year: 1. Activity Funds — one already completed 2. Payroll Audit — in final week of field work; focused on process, controls, segregation of duties, and flow-charting all processes for future reference. Last payroll audit was 3 years ago. A new payroll director Chase is also in place. 3. Accounts Payable — including a review of the travel card program 4. Cybersecurity Risk Assessment — Weaver engagement; kickoff meeting scheduled for January 11, 2024 , with field work starting January 15, 2024 5. Field Trip expenditures ~$1 million/year 6. Hometown Athletic Ticketing System — will have been in use for a year 7. Child Nutrition follow-up 8. Band Inventory — high schools in May, all others in November 9. Maintenance follow-up 10. Annual PEIMS attendance review 11. Construction Audit 12. Print Shop lowest priority; will be completed if time permits Notable Discussion — Travel Cards: - The district historically was "very anti-credit card" and had only 3 district credit cards for emergencies. - As hotels increasingly refuse checks, the district implemented pre-loaded travel cards with merchant category code restrictions cards only work at the designated hotel . - Cards are checked out by employees, loaded with the specific PO amount, and returned with receipts after travel. - Cards can be adjusted remotely in real time if needed and deactivated immediately if lost. - The district has approximately 100 cards stored but they carry zero balances until activated for specific travel. - The program started with student travel in spring 2023 and expanded to all employee travel as of September 1, 2023. - The district does not use P-cards purchasing cards . Maintenance and other departments use internal purchase orders for procurement. Item 4: Potential Independent Program Analysis of Planning & Construction Dr. Null presented a proposal to engage an independent industry expert to evaluate the Planning & Construction Department's front-end processes ahead of the potential new bond program ~$1.6–$1.8 billion in construction work . Background: - Community members during the 2019 bond cycle asked how the district ensures it gets the best prices. - Previous backend audits have confirmed the district gets what it pays for; this initiative focuses on the front end — procurement, guaranteed maximum pricing, contracting, and organizational structure. Proposed Scope: - Organizational structure and staffing adequacy - Procurement methods and bidding processes - Interview/selection processes for contractors and architects - Oversight of contractors and architects - Project management practices - Construction standards - Compliance with applicable law - Financial processes on the back end Key Details: - Kyle Holland, President/CEO of the Associated General Contractors of America Houston Chapter , helped identify a potential consultant — a former school district P&C leader ~15 years experience now working as a consultant evaluating such programs. - The consultant would report directly to the Audit Committee to maintain independence. - No contract has been signed yet; the committee was asked for feedback. - Mr. Foster head of P&C is reportedly supportive and sees it as an opportunity for improvement. Committee Discussion: - Trustee Chase emphasized interest in the exact scope of work, the consultant's evaluation expertise, and how "best value" is defined not just cheapest price, but also risk transfer . - Noted the district already uses Construction Manager at Risk CMAR as its default construction method. - Raised concern about Key Personnel Risk — the district is lean, and much institutional knowledge exists only in individuals' heads, which creates risk, especially with scaling up. - Dr. Null committed to sharing the proposed scope of work with Trustee Chase for review. works in approximately 15 seconds - Very minimal to no side effects if administered incorrectly i.e., person is not actually experiencing an opioid overdose Current Status 2022–2023 School Year - Narcan has been deployed at all secondary campuses grades 7–12 throughout the 2022–2023 school year - Available in school clinics, athletic training rooms where applicable , and carried by each CISD police officer two-dose packs - Narcan has already been used once on a CISD campus — McCord stated he could not definitively prove it saved the student's life but that "it made a huge difference" while waiting for 911 response - Credit given to Barbara Robertson Director of Nursing and Chief Blakelock for acquisition and deployment - Montgomery County has been a significant partner, providing doses at no cost; recently provided 72 additional doses through Homeland Security Senate Bill 629 - Signed by Governor Abbott on June 18, 2023 - Effective September 2023 - Requires school districts to maintain and be able to administer an opioid antagonist for grades 6 and below - Requires at least one authorized and trained individual per campus - Provides for possible funding CISD's Expansion Plan - CISD was already ahead of SB 629 requirements, having had Narcan at secondary campuses for a full year before the bill - Intermediate campuses were already being supplied before the bill was ratified - The district plans to expand Narcan to ALL campuses, including elementary schools , going beyond the SB 629 requirement of 6th grade and below - Rationale: People of all ages are on elementary campuses; children may have access to pills that appear to be ordinary medication but contain fentanyl - Chief Blakelock noted that prescription pills confiscated from students this year tested positive for fentanyl at the state lab — incidents were limited but confirmed - Full deployment anticipated by August 2023 for the start of the school year - Nurses and athletic trainers have had a positive attitude toward the program Key Safety Point - Under the law, a nurse or trained individual who reasonably suspects an opioid overdose may administer Narcan - Each officer carries a two-dose pack; it is possible both doses may need to be administered before EMS arrives Required Psychological First Aid Training Presented by Ethan Barton - Texas Education Code §37.108 f 6 C i requires members of the District Safety and Security Committee to complete psychological first aid training - Barton researched available courses and identified a manageable option approved by the Safety Center - Barton has already completed the training himself - Training details will be emailed to all committee members via Linda Godfrey with instructions - Training is self-paced/on your own time; includes a test and certificate upon completion - Completion is on the honor system , but members should retain certificates in case the state requests them - No firm deadline, but members are asked to complete it as soon as possible - Barton offered to assist any committee member who has questions Executive Closed Session - Committee entered closed session at 2:22 PM pursuant to Sections 551.076 and 551.089 of the Texas Open Meetings Act - Topics discussed in closed session: 1. 2022–2023 K-12 School District Audit Cycle Completion presented by Mr. Ferguson 2. Compliance Subcommittee — Opioids/Substance Use Education Presenter: Miss Taylor district staff Provided an overview of CISD's substance use education, prevention, intervention, and consequences: Prevention/Education: - Red Ribbon Week observed across campuses - Conroe Cares program covers positive character traits and peer pressure per TEC 29.906 - Health curriculum covers substance use topics - Tony Hoffman speaker visited high schools in February and junior highs in May - Hidden in Plain Sight mock bedroom presentations held in September north and south county - District Attorney and parent speakers presented on opioids and laws at Oak Ridge HS and Grand Oaks - M-COPE Montgomery County Opioid Prevention Endeavor also presented - A vaping deterrent program for students caught vaping is in progress Intervention: - Interventions are individualized based on at-risk behaviors identified at school - Collaboration with parents/guardians for appropriate support - Tri-County T-CHAT provides telemental health services to youth with parental consent Consequences: - Vaping without THC treated like tobacco : ~3-day in-school suspension - Marijuana or controlled substance possession/use: Mandatory DAEP placement; misdemeanor criminal offense - Felony-level controlled substance : Mandatory expulsion or JJAEP placement - THC in vape devices at felony level : Students placed on probationary expulsion — 15 days DAEP, return to campus on probation; second offense can result in JJAEP placement Additional Discussion Chris Poich, Assistant Superintendent for High Schools : - Very few repeat offenders go back to DAEP - JJAEP has capacity issues as it serves all Montgomery County districts - District hired additional staff for DAEP; exploring 30/45/60-day DAEP placements as an intermediate option between 15-day DAEP and JJAEP - Vaping is currently the 1 behavior leading to DAEP assignments - Increase in DAEP numbers attributed largely to district growth 27,000 to 71,000+ students B. Membership Subcommittee Presenter: Miss Kersey - Electronic SHAC application was posted May 1st and will remain open through June 30th . - Current SHAC members will receive an email this month asking about their intent to return for the next year. - After gathering responses and attendance records, the membership subcommittee will determine how many spots need to be filled, with selection beginning in July. - The subcommittee developed a membership selection rubric handout distributed : - Assessment categories include: experience with youth, community involvement, occupation, relevant skills, interest in subcommittees, interest in leadership roles, completeness, and professionalism - Rubric is a personal tool for individual subcommittee members to take notes on applicants — notes are not shared or stored - Includes a "reminders and references" section linking to Texas DSHS guidance on SHAC membership traits - The rubric was slightly amended from the version previously circulated based on feedback. C. Mental Health Subcommittee — Cell Phone Use Recommendations Presenter: Dr. Bryce Speer SHAC Chair and subcommittee members The mental health subcommittee reported investing an estimated 100+ volunteer hours developing cell phone use guidelines/recommendations. Context and Data Presented: - U.S. Surgeon General issued a new advisory in May 2023 on risks of social media to youth mental health depression, anxiety, lack of human connection - Critical brain development occurs ages 10–19 - 3+ hours/day on social media doubles the risk of poor mental health outcomes - 8th and 10th graders report averaging at least 3.5 hours/day on phones - Systematic review of 42+ studies found consistent links between social media use and poor sleep, reduced sleep duration, and depression - Screen time inversely correlated with outdoor time and physical activity; increased screen time linked to higher myopia risk - Study showed texting while walking impairs executive cognitive function safety concern in crowded schools - Meta-analysis on cyberbullying : positively related to depression; greater effect on females; risk increases with age; effects have increased over time and have not plateaued General Framework: Based on TEC 28.004 c 2 , the SHAC is responsible for recommending policies, procedures, and strategies to prevent mental health concerns. Recommendations Presented: 1. Emergency Access: Students will continue to have access to cell phones during emergencies; campus emergency protocols remain unchanged. 2. 504/IEP Students: Students on 504 plans or IEPs maintain phone use consistent with their approved plans. 3. K–8 Students: - Cell phones may be used respectfully and responsibly before school and after dismissal - No videoing, recording, or photographing during school hours - Phones should be on silent, kept in backpacks, and not visible during school hours classroom, lunch, hallways, locker rooms 4. 9th–12th Grade Students: - Same before/after school use as K–8 - Cell phone use should be avoided during the school day - Students may have access during designated periods ~20 minutes/day for pressing personal matters e.g., two 10-minute periods - Outside designated periods: silent mode, in backpacks, not visible - No recording, videoing, or photographing 5. Extracurricular Communications: - All CISD extracurricular communication with students should occur outside school hours using multiple methods monitors, announcements, Canvas, etc. - On rare occasions when communication must occur during school hours, students can check during designated cell phone periods 6. Video Presentation by Tessa Stucky, LPC - A ~21-minute video was shown from Tessa Stucky , a Licensed Professional Counselor in The Woodlands and author of For the Sake of Our Youth . - Key points from the presentation: - Teen suicide is at an all-time high; it is the second leading cause of death for adolescents nationally and in Texas. - Average screen time for teens is 7–9 hours/day ; some clients average 17–18 hours. - Average age of first exposure to pornography: 8 years old . - Screens provide dopamine but lack oxytocin needed for genuine human connection. - Technology use is linked to increased anxiety, depression, loneliness, self-harm, and suicidal ideation. - Sextortion and cyberbullying are serious and growing threats. - Not all technology is bad — the key is teaching purposeful, balanced use. - Recommended approach: delay social media access, teach responsible use, prioritize sleep, allow boredom, build resilience. - Upcoming event: Tessa Stucky will present at Grand Oaks High School on May 15 at 6:30 PM . Opening Remarks & Caney Creek High School Bomb Threat Incident The meeting was called to order and recognized as a specially called session of the Safety and Security Committee. Dr. Noel and Chief Matt provided a detailed account of a bomb threat received at Caney Creek High School earlier that day. Key details: - The threat was a phone call, believed to be part of multiple similar calls made nationally — not from a local caller. - The school was fully evacuated ~2,600 students, ~3,000 people total moved outside . - Responding agencies included: - Conroe ISD Police Department - Montgomery County Office of Homeland Security & Emergency Management - Montgomery County Fire Marshal bomb dog and bomb techs - ATF with explosive detection dog - Conroe ISD's own explosive detection canine - A total of 8–9 explosive detection canines were on scene - Caney Creek Fire Department - Montgomery County Hospital District - Precinct 2 Constable's Office - Montgomery County EMS - Campus staff, administration, athletic trainers, and nurses all supported the evacuation effort providing water, shade, medical standby, etc. - On the investigative side , CISD detectives traced the phone number to Little Rock, Arkansas , pinged the cell phone on a street there via the cell provider, and contacted Little Rock Police who took over the investigation. The FBI is involved given the interstate nature of the threat. - Potential charges include false alarm/report , which could be a federal crime due to the interstate element. Item 1: Approval of Previous Meeting Minutes - Motion made and seconded to approve the minutes from the last committee meeting. - Vote: Approved unanimously. Item 2: Student ID Badges — Discussion Mr. McCord presented previously shared information on student ID badges for continued committee discussion. This was not a formal vote but a feedback/consensus-gathering session. Current Status: - Conroe ISD already provides badges to all students and will continue next year. - Grades 7 and up : Badges include student photos functioning as ID badges . - Pre-K through 6th grade : Badges function primarily as bus passes and do not currently include photos though this could change . Survey of Other Districts: - A wide-ranging survey of Texas districts was presented Katy, Spring, Georgetown, Southlake, HEB, Dallas-area districts, and Montgomery County districts . - Requiring student ID badges is very common , particularly at the junior high/middle school level and above. - Conroe High School previously required ID badges when Dr. Noel served as principal there. Committee Discussion: - A teacher/parent committee member who teaches at Conroe High School 9th Grade Campus strongly supported requiring badges across the board , citing safety benefits — particularly for campuses with T-buildings portable buildings where students move between structures and may not be easily identified. - Mr. Hubert raised concerns about enforcement and discipline , asking how other districts handle non-compliance, given that discipline issues are already a significant burden on staff. - Dr. Noel acknowledged enforcement challenges but noted that ID badges can serve as an early, low-stakes boundary for students — establishing structure before more serious behavioral issues arise. He shared that when Conroe High implemented badges, they saw overall improvement in behavior . He noted the importance of positive reinforcement e.g., bringing donuts to classrooms with 100% compliance . - Kim committee member noted the importance of teaching the system consistently , recognizing that adolescents will forget badges and some may not have the $2 replacement fee, so systems and grace periods need to be in place. - Discussion acknowledged that this would be "one more thing" for administrators and staff to manage. Outcome: - The committee expressed general support no opposition voiced for moving the ID badge discussion forward. - Mr. McCord confirmed the item will be brought to the full Board of Trustees for consideration and decision in a future meeting. Item 3: Rave 911 App — Information Mr. McCord presented information on the Rave 911 app , which Montgomery County is providing to all school districts in the county. Key Features: - Any staff member can download the app to their phone and register to their specific campus. - In an emergency, a staff member can instantly alert 911 through the app. - The app simultaneously notifies all other staff on that campus of the emergency. - It automatically routes to appropriate responders based on emergency type e.g., medical emergencies notify Montgomery County Hospital District; active attacker scenarios notify local law enforcement including CISD police and sheriff's department . - The app works via cellular or Wi-Fi noted that Wi-Fi is "extremely robust" in CISD buildings, important for areas like Grand Oaks High School where cellular signal is weaker . - Uses geofencing — the app is campus-specific and works within the geofenced area. Traveling employees are covered at whichever campus they are physically located. - Also useful for non-emergency, discreet staff communication e.g., power outages, general notifications without using the PA system . A promotional video ~2 minutes was shown to the committee. Implementation Details: - Montgomery County is paying the majority of the cost; CISD's share the "delta" is approximately $20,000 , which has been earmarked from the existing safety/security budget. - Significant setup work is required, including geocoding much of which was already completed by Mr. Barton previously , technology integration, training, and addressing logistics for substitutes, Child Nutrition workers, custodians, and other traveling staff. - Willis ISD has already used Rave 911; their communications director was consulted. - Target implementation: Start of the 2023–2024 school year , contingent on overcoming remaining hurdles. - Mr. McCord described the project as being on approximately "the 20-yard line" — significant work remains. Closed Session Items under Texas Government Code The committee moved into closed session at 4:29 PM to discuss: - §551.076 : Deployment or specific occasions for implementation of security personnel or devices - §551.089 : Deliberation regarding safety/security devices or security audits The committee reconvened in open session at 4:47 PM . Item 5: Approval of Multi-Hazard Emergency Operations Plan 2023–2024 - Motion made, seconded. - Vote: Approved unanimously. No discussion. Item 6: Texas School Safety Center Intruder Detection Audits & Training Plans - Information was received regarding intruder detection audits from the Texas School Safety Center. - Motion made and seconded to approve training plans. - Vote: Approved unanimously. No discussion. Other Notable Items Mentioned - Safety technology : The district is actively evaluating various safety devices including AI-based tools, sound detectors, and various types of metal detectors including those used at venues like Disney World and Minute Maid Park . These are complex due to procurement/bid processes and funding. - Bond Committee : A bond committee is currently working on a potential future bond and has approximately $35 million earmarked for safety and security , in addition to safety features built into new construction. Adjournment - The meeting was adjourned after Item 6. - This is anticipated to be the final committee meeting of the school year unless a special need arises. - Next meeting expected in early June for the new year cycle . The committee will be notified.

Overview This was a board workshop not a regular meeting , focused primarily on reading instruction and literacy in Conroe ISD. The workshop featured presentations from community advocates, an outside literacy expert, and district staff. No formal votes were taken. The meeting lasted approximately 4.5 hours. Citizens Participation Dr. Sarah Nall — Parent and grandparent of CISD students. Referenced the podcast Sold a Story and advocated for the district to shift from balanced literacy to the science of reading. Cited research showing balanced literacy produces proficiency in less than half of students, while science of reading approaches yield ~95% proficiency. Noted that Highland Park ISD Dallas made this transition and that Texas House Bill 3 is pushing science of reading training. Amanda Mandy Shepard — Mother of three and CISD teacher. Two of her children have dyslexia. Shared her daughter's relatively smooth identification journey but described a years-long struggle to get her son identified. He spent three years in RTI, was initially deemed "globally low" and did not qualify for dyslexia services. A private evaluation confirmed dyslexia and auditory processing disorder. CISD would not accept the private evaluation; a fourth evaluation through the district finally identified him. Urged the district to streamline the process for parents. Item 2: Reading Presentation — Community Advocates and Outside Expert Community Parent Advocate Laurie - Founder of the Conroe ISD Parent Dyslexia Group and member of the Texas Dyslexia Coalition - Shared personal experience with two sons who struggled with reading; one required three years of private dyslexia therapy - Presented CISD STAAR reading data 3rd–5th grade over four years showing roughly 15% of students not passing reading STAAR, with economically disadvantaged students performing significantly worse - Emphasized that economically disadvantaged families cannot afford private tutoring Dr. Allison Peck — Chief Academic Officer, Neuhaus Education Center Key points from Dr. Peck's presentation: What is the Science of Reading? - Not just phonics — it is an organized body of knowledge drawn from 50+ years of research across developmental psychology, cognitive neuropsychology, linguistics, and educational intervention - Reading is NOT hardwired in the brain; it requires explicit instruction - English has learnable patterns that can be systematically taught Student Impact Statistics: - 5% of students learn to read effortlessly - 35% learn with some general instruction - 45-50% require explicit instruction in the structure of language - 10-15% need intensive intervention likely reading disability - Implication: Without science of reading instruction, ~40% of students may be adequately served; ~60% may not National Reading Panel "Big Five" 2000 : 1. Phonemic awareness 2. Phonics 3. Fluency 4. Vocabulary 5. Comprehension How instruction should be delivered: - Explicit - Systematic - Evidence-based - Sustained practice - Progress monitoring Demonstration: Dr. Peck walked the board through a closed syllable lesson as a concrete example of science of reading instruction, showing how patterns in English can be explicitly taught and then applied to decodable texts and multi-syllabic words. Key Assertions: - Two-thirds of students who cannot read proficiently by end of third grade will end up in jail or on welfare - Prevention in Pre-K through 1st grade is far more cost-effective than later remediation - If high-quality Tier 1 instruction follows science of reading, the number of students needing intensive intervention should decrease, approaching the true 10-15% who have reading disabilities - University teacher preparation programs have historically not adequately taught science of reading, placing the burden on school districts Board Member Discussion: - Board Member Williams asked about ESL students' contribution to struggling reader statistics - Board Member Datron pushed back on whether science of reading is a "silver bullet," noting that dyslexic students will still be dyslexic regardless of curriculum and that multiple teaching methods can be valid - Board Member Hubert asked for tangible ROI data from districts that implemented science of reading; Dr. Peck referenced Mississippi's statewide implementation but acknowledged meaningful results take ~2 years - Dr. Peck acknowledged science of reading won't eliminate all reading struggles but stated it will significantly reduce the number needing intervention and allow faster progress for those who do Kristen Meetings — Parent Advocate, Texas Dyslexia Coalition Member - Presented PEIMS Public Education Information Management System data showing CISD identifies only ~3.5% of elementary students as dyslexic, well below the expected 5-20% prevalence - That number jumps to ~9.5% at the middle school level, suggesting students are not being identified early enough - Argued the district is under-identifying dyslexic students in the critical early elementary years - Noted the district has 49 dyslexia teachers elementary and middle school who are at capacity - Reported that the district budgeted for a 3-year CALT Certified Academic Language Therapist training plan but did not apply for the HB 1525 dyslexia grant that neighboring districts received Significant Board Discussion: - Board Member Datron challenged the 20% dyslexia prevalence figure and questioned whether implementing science of reading would truly reduce or increase identification numbers - Multiple board members debated whether low identification numbers reflect under-testing or appropriate screening - Discussion about whether trained dyslexia staff leave for other districts after receiving CISD-funded training; the district acknowledged this is an ongoing problem - Board discussed whether non-compete or payback agreements could be used for staff who receive district-funded training; Dr. Null indicated the only enforcement mechanism would be to sue, which is impractical, though such agreements can serve as a deterrent Stacy Evans — Director of local dyslexia nonprofit, former educator, parent Presented recommendations based on observations and feedback from community members: Positive Observations: - October Dyslexia Awareness Month activities - Newly formed Dyslexia Parent Advisory Committee - Strong department leadership under Dr. Henry - Enthusiastic and motivated K-6 reading intervention teachers - Professional development conferences for dyslexia staff Recommendations: 1. Hire more reading intervention teachers, diagnosticians, and support staff 2. Communication — Provide parents with clearer information about processes, acronyms, timelines, parental rights, and options 504 vs. special education 3. Accept outside evaluations — Create district guidelines for private evaluations to reduce redundant testing and parent costs 4. Reduce re-testing — If a private evaluation meets standards, accept it rather than requiring additional district testing 5. Tier 1 instruction must follow science of reading methodology 6. Support and retain staff to prevent burnout and turnover Item 3: Reading in Conroe ISD — District Staff Presentation Presenters: - Dr. Upshaw — Assistant Superintendent of Curriculum & Instruction - Mark — Coordinator of Elementary Language Arts - Dr. Carlisle — Staff member literacy/intervention - Dr. Sharon Henry — Coordinator of Dyslexia Education - Dr. Kendra Wiggins — Director of Special Education CISD Reads & Writes Framework

Any drug not prescribed and purchased from a legitimate pharmacy carries risk, as illicit fentanyl is being mixed into many substances including cocaine and THC vape products. - How naloxone works: Displaces opioids from receptors controlling breathing, sedation, and blood pressure. Has no effect on someone without opioids in their system. - Legal basis: Health and Safety Code 483.102–483.106, policies FFAC. - Deployment: Narcan nasal spray only is now stocked at: - Junior high and high school clinics - High school athletic training rooms - All Conroe ISD police officers - Moorehead Stadium and WoodForest Bank Stadium - Training completed: All junior high and high school nurses and all CISD police officers trained on signs/symptoms of opioid overdose, administration of naloxone, and supporting the individual until EMS arrives. - Program launched January 4, 2023 when students returned to secondary campuses. - Shelf life: FDA now manufacturing with a 3-year expiration. - Dosing: Each box contains 2 doses; multiple boxes stocked per location because overdoses often come in pairs students sharing substances . Texas A&M training indicated both doses may be needed per person. - Summer training: District participated in a mock mass overdose exercise at Caney Creek in conjunction with Montgomery County — this experience helped drive urgency for the program. - Special thanks to Dr. Joy Lanto and the Texas A&M Opioid Task Force for ~10 months of collaboration to launch the program. Item 2B: MEOP Audit Submission to Texas School Safety Center Presenter: Ethan Martin, School Safety Coordinator Key Points: - The Texas School Safety Center required submission of the district's Multi-Hazard Emergency Operation Plan MEOP , basic plan, active threat annex, and active shooter appendix. - Submitted approximately one month before the deadline. - Initial result: District received a "deficient" notice 18 pages of deficiencies . Upon review, Martin found that many items flagged as deficient were actually included in the plan but not formatted to the state's preferred framework. - After reformatting to align with the state's template while maintaining CISD's existing approved plan, the submission was approved with no additional information required . - Total documentation submitted included campus-based MEOPs totaling approximately 16,000 pages . - Notable: Dr. Null reported that no district's plan was accepted on first submission. Multiple superintendents from other districts reached out for help; Ethan Martin personally assisted three other districts in getting their plans approved. - The Texas School Safety Center recently sent out a pre-submission checklist; Martin volunteered to provide feedback on the checklist. Next submission likely due October 2024 . Item 2C: Review and Input for 2023-2024 Multi-Hazard Emergency Operations Plan Presenter: Ethan Martin Key Points: - Committee members will receive the current MEOP framework document approximately 137–187 pages via email for review and input. - A deadline will be set for feedback submission. - The plan will be presented for committee recommendation/approval at the summer meeting . - Any proposed changes will be vetted through appropriate channels. - Format is largely dictated by the state; limited flexibility on structure. - Reminder: The document is internal/confidential — "your eyes only." Item 2D: Smart Tag System Implementation Bus Safety Presenters: Chris McCord Asst. Superintendent and Sam Director of Transportation Overview: Smart Tag is a tablet-and-badge system used on school buses for student identification, route management, and safety. Key Technical Details: - RFID badge cards issued to all ~71,000 students approximately 40,000 are bus riders - Cards contain only a 14-digit randomized ID number — no battery, no GPS, no tracking capability - Cards can only be read by CISD Smart Tag readers mounted on buses must physically touch reader - System uses cellular connectivity not Wi-Fi - Highest-level data security protocols followed Implementation Status: - Oak Ridge High School feeder: Successfully implemented several weeks - Grand Oaks feeder: Implemented starting January 2023; going well - The Woodlands feeder: Planned for the following week but delayed ~1 week due to weather badge printer company based in Austin unable to ship - Goal: Full implementation before spring break Safety Benefits Highlighted: 1. Parent notifications: Parents can opt in to receive text alerts when the bus approaches their stop — first time in 130 years parents will have this capability. Reduces time children wait exposed at bus stops. 2. Confirming correct drop-offs: Especially critical for PreK/K students and when substitute/standby drivers are used. System shows student photos and flags young students requiring authorized adult pickup. 3. Substitute driver support: Routes displayed on tablet; prevents wrong turns and getting lost real-world example of standby driver ending up in Willis area shared . 4. End-of-route child check: System alerts drivers if students are still scanned as on-bus at route end — added layer beyond physical walk-through checks. 5. Preventing unauthorized riders: Helps confirm only CISD students board buses. 6. Real-world success stories: - January 24 flood: Principals in Oak Ridge feeder used system to confirm bus status for worried parents during street closures - First day at Suchma Elementary: Parent's concern about locating children resolved within minutes - Loading time improvement: Bradley Elementary loaded buses in 11 minutes vs. usual 15 minutes District Statistics: - CISD buses drive 6.3 million miles/year 14 times to moon and back - ~25 million student on/off bus events per year - Currently at 24 driver vacancies best staffing level in years, down from 30-34 Discussion Points: - Opt-out: Available; only 1 parent has opted out across both implemented feeders so far - Lost badges: Each school has its own badge printer for quick replacement; $5 replacement fee planned but no student will be denied a badge; students without badges are still transported manual entry avail

Item 3: General Fund Capital Capacity Presenter: Darren Rice, CFO Fund Balance Analysis - Unassigned fund balance at 8/31/22: $173.3 million - 20% target: $124 million - Available above target: $49.3 million - Board-approved bond contingency fund November : $18.8 million - Land purchases for upcoming bond: ~$30 million - Remaining capacity above 20% target: ~$551,000 - A reimbursement resolution will be brought to the January board meeting to allow the $30 million in land purchases to be reimbursed from future bond proceeds Bond Ratings & PSF - District rating: AA+ one notch below AAA - The Permanent School Fund PSF guarantee is currently at capacity statewide — districts cannot access it - Without PSF, the district can still sell bonds but at a slightly higher rate ~3 basis points difference - Only a few school districts have AAA ratings on their own, and those are fully built-out communities no longer issuing bonds e.g., Highland Park 2023-24 Budget Challenges - Legislative session expected to bring: new school safety legislation, truth-in-taxation rework, additional tax compression, and growth challenges - No funding mechanism in the current formula for employee raises a 2% raise costs ~$12.5 million - No mechanism to adjust for 8% inflation - ESSER funding cliff: ~225 positions ~$16 million funded by ESSER III must transition to the general fund - Employee retention stipend currently ESSER-funded will also need a new funding source Capital Needs Without a Bond Annual estimated needs if no bond passes: | Category | Annual Cost | | | | | Portable buildings ~100/year for 3,000 new students | ~$25 million | | Technology new, replacement, infrastructure | ~$20 million | | Infrastructure roofs, full HVAC overhauls | ~$15 million | | Buses | ~$3 million | | Total | ~$63 million/year | Note: Bond expenditures for items with shorter lifespans buses ~10 years, technology ~5 years would be matched to appropriately short bond tranches. Capital Maintenance Fund History - Board established a $50 million Capital Maintenance Fund in 2019 to support needs not funded in the bond - $20 million seeded from fund balance; $10 million/year budgeted from general fund for 3 years - Conroe ISD: $8,701/student or $8,546 when the $10M capital maintenance diversion was active - Katy ISD: $9,623/student — a ~$70 million/year difference at Conroe's enrollment - Magnolia ISD: $9,124/student — a ~$30 million/year difference - Conroe has the second-lowest tax rate in the Houston area - Dr. Noel emphasized this is not an excuse but a reality that affects competitiveness in programs while praising staff for achieving strong results despite the funding gap VATRE Voter Approved Tax Rate Election — Golden & Copper Pennies - 9 copper pennies available: ~$4.5 million/penny $5.5M local minus $1M state recapture - Total potential additional revenue from all 12 remaining pennies: ~$67.5 million/year - Most peer districts have accessed additional pennies Humble, Katy, Spring Branch, Splendora, New Caney all above Conroe's level Board Discussion on VATRE: - Dr. Noel noted VADERs are difficult to pass Magnolia and Klein were unsuccessful; College Station succeeded by tying it specifically to staff raises - Multiple trustees expressed interest in exploring it but acknowledged political difficulty of asking for both a bond and a tax rate increase simultaneously, especially with rising property values - He thanked the audit committee, Dr. Knoll, and district staff for their support and collaboration. Band and Orchestra Instrument Inventory - High school instrument counts were conducted in May. Controls have improved significantly since the initial count in September 2020, when approximately 400 instruments were unaccounted for. The current number of missing instruments stands at 22 . - Committee members asked whether the 22 missing instruments came from one campus or multiple. Dr. Horton Fine Arts confirmed it was a mixture of campuses and attributed some of the issues to staff turnover and inherited accountability gaps. - Dr. Horton noted the missing instruments are believed to be primarily an accountability/paperwork issue between directors, students, and parents rather than theft. The number also includes instruments that may have been destroyed, broken, or stolen from students. - Key improvements cited by Dr. Horton: - For the first time in three years, all high school band and orchestra programs are operating in the correct school year with instruments in inventory. - Four new high school band directors came on board; two new directors at The Woodlands High School cleaned up unfinished inventory business from the previous directors in one day. - The district held off on critical needs purchases for programs with unresolved inventory issues in some cases for up to two months , creating a natural accountability incentive. - Directors are now proactively asking about inventory requirements rather than waiting to be asked. - Junior high and intermediate school instrument inventories are scheduled for November — a first at these levels. Dr. Horton has been meeting with intermediate and junior high directors to explain the process and expectations. - Discussion also touched on the need for clearer guidance on when to remove instruments from inventory e.g., when repair costs exceed replacement value . Activity Funds Audit 2021-2022 - Completed; reported as going smoothly with no notable issues. Facility Rentals Audit - Separated from the larger maintenance audit for manageability. - Finding: The facility is well-run, but rental rates have not been raised since 2017 . The recommendation is to reassemble a team to evaluate current costs energy, salaries for police/custodial/AV staff, administrator on duty and determine whether current rates cover actual costs. - It was clarified that facility rentals are not a revenue-generating enterprise — the goal is to ensure taxpayers are not subsidizing rentals. - Outdoor unfenced facilities are open to general public use; reserved/scheduled use requires a rental agreement. Teacher Training Center Construction Audit - No findings or observations. Mr. Hayden praised Mr. Foster Easy and his department for outstanding controls. Weaver external audit firm also assisted on this audit. 2022-2023 Internal Audit Plan Ongoing: - Maintenance Audit — Field work began the prior week. Covers approximately 40,000 work orders over a seven-month audit period. Weaver is assisting. Areas of focus include preventive maintenance ensuring required maintenance is happening on time, not evaluating the maintenance schedules themselves , contractor costs, and related controls. This removes deposits from Woodforest's balance sheet, giving the district flexibility to deposit unlimited amounts at the negotiated rate 90-day T-bill + 25 basis points . The program manages over $21 billion nationally. - This will be especially beneficial as tax collections increase toward year-end. Staff clarified the $50 million allocation was based on the amount the district doesn't expect to need to access, and investment options are constrained by the Public Funds Investment Act PFIA .
