Mr. Davila and his team were commended. Annual PEIMS Compliance - Recurring issues: Inconsistency and missing daily attendance submissions by teachers. - Discussion highlights: - Teachers have a 30-minute window each day to submit attendance electronically. - Dr. Knoll explained that while habitual non-compliance is addressed administratively, random misses occur due to classroom disruptions, substitutes, and the pace of teaching. - Some campuses have implemented additional reminders e.g., an "attendance bell," daily announcements . - Campus PEIMS teams hold quarterly meetings to reconcile and "true up" attendance records, ensuring accuracy even if timing was off. - Ms. Chase asked about the priority level of attendance submission for teachers and whether there are repercussions for non-compliance. Dr. Knoll confirmed corrective action exists for habitual offenders but acknowledged the challenge of hitting the exact window consistently. - Mr. Hubert asked about peer districts; Mr. Hayden said he's never heard of a district that does it "really great" and that CISD is likely ahead of many in even tracking and worrying about it. - Risk context: If the state audited daily attendance and rejected entries submitted outside the window, CISD could lose ADA Average Daily Attendance funding for those students on those days. Mr. Hayden noted he has never seen the state actually do this, but the risk exists. - The committee discussed whether the state could be asked to lengthen the attendance window or change funding methodology. - This will remain an annual audit focus. Band and Orchestra Inventory - Started Monday; high schools being audited now, junior highs in November. - Weaver is not used for inventory work to manage costs. Construction Audit - Started Monday; currently auditing the Teacher Training Center. Maintenance Inventory Observation - Mr. Hayden will observe the annual maintenance inventory on May 18. Items include consumables soap, paper towels, mops, brooms as well as higher-dollar equipment tools, mowers, heavy equipment . 4. ITGC IT General Controls Audit – Detailed Presentation Presented by: Maribel Castro Manager, Weaver IT Advisory and Rima Partner, Weaver IT Advisory Key Context: This was the first ITGC audit in approximately 13 years. Scope - Evaluated IT controls for CISD Technology Services across key systems: - eFinance Plus general ledger/financial system - eSchool Plus - Chronos time entry - School Cash parent payment portal - Absence Management - Special Education software - Child Nutrition Program Management software - Windows Active Directory / Network - Coverage period: September 1, 2021 – February 28, 2022 Overall Results - 22 expected controls evaluated - 13 designed appropriately - 8 operating effectively - 14 Opportunities for Improvement higher risk; management response required - 5 Observations lower risk; management response optional Note: Many findings were related to lack of formal documentation — teams were often following processes but had not formalized or documented them. Several remediation actions were already underway or completed at the time of the report. Opportunities for Improvement OFIs | | Area | Finding Summary | Status | | | | -| --| | 1 | IT Policies & Procedures | No formal IT policies/procedures defining minimum information security requirements. Some informal process documents existed but were not formally reviewed or approved. | In progress | | 2 | IT Asset Inventory | Network Operations Center did not maintain a formal inventory of assets. | In progress | | 3 | Change Management – Segregation of Duties eFinance Plus | Six users had access to both develop and implement changes to production in a source code repository tool integrated with eFinance Plus. No systematic enforcement of segregation of duties. | Remediated – access removed | | 4 | User Access Reviews | No formal periodic user access reviews performed for in-scope systems. Some informal reviews existed but were not documented. | In progress some quarterly, some monthly | | 5 | Access Revocation Non-SSO Systems | School Cash and Child Nutrition Program Management were not single sign-on; no formal process to notify teams when users were terminated and needed access removed. | In progress; School Cash moving to SSO | | 6 | Chronos Administrator Access | One user retained IT privileged access after transferring to a different department ~within the past year . | Remediated | | 7 | School Cash Administrator Access | A shared system account had elevated privileges that were not required for its purpose. | Remediated | | 8 | Access Provisioning School Cash | Formal approval documentation could not be obtained for one user's access, though the access itself was appropriate. | In progress | | 9 | Authentication/Passwords School Cash | Minimum password security requirements not configured. | In progress evaluating SSO or vendor password parameters | | 10 | Authentication/Passwords Child Nutrition | Same as above for Child Nutrition Program Management software. | In progress | | 11 | Physical Access – Server Room | Four users maintained server room access that was no longer required. | In progress | |

ESSER Funding Update Presented by Dr. Hines and Dr. Pam Zoda Director of Federal Programs ESSER I: - Bulk of funds went to funding attendance during the last nine weeks of 2020. - Small amounts used for private nonprofits and some equipment purchases. ESSER II ~$28–29 million : - ~$10 million taken off the top by the state for attendance offsets in 2020–21. - Funded supplemental campus formula staff teachers beyond 22:1 ratio , Chromebooks $5 million , reading libraries $2 million , a programmer, and the fall semester virtual academy. - For 2022–23: ~$3 million budgeted for campus formula staff released early for aggressive hiring , district support staff, indirect costs, and additional non-personnel items Wi-Fi improvements, Chromebooks . - ESSER II funds must be spent by end of 2022–23 school year. ESSER III ~$65 million : - Required to spend at least 20% on learning recovery; district is approaching ~50%. - Key allocations include: - Expanded summer school free tuition, increased teacher pay to attract staff - Campus formula staff supplements - ~$9.2 million distributed to campuses for learning recovery software, hardware, interventionists, counselors over a two-year planning period - District support staff positions in HR, payroll, elementary/secondary education - Health services department expansion $8 million - Equipment purchases $1.6 million - Virtual Academy: District plans to apply for a campus ID number to offer limited virtual school for grades 5–8, to be brought to the board at the next meeting - Employee retention stipends: $5 million placeholder - Attendance hold harmless: $4.5 million placeholder - Math support: Targeted funding for math learning recovery - T-Class pipeline program: Full-year student teacher residencies funded partly with ESSER - Additional assistant principals for campuses with heavy Teacher Incentive Allotment TIA workloads - For 2023–24: $4 million placeholder for stepping off the ESSER funding cliff, $2.2 million in indirect costs, summer school funds, and virtual academy support. - ESSER III funds must be spent by end of calendar year 2024. Key Discussion Points: - Board member asked whether ESSER-funded staff positions are short-term or long-term. Dr. Hines confirmed they are long-term positions the district will need to absorb into the general budget, though turnover and growth provide capacity to do so. - Dr. Null emphasized that aggressive early hiring helps teacher satisfaction and retention — teachers value being supported with adequate staffing. - The district is working to identify alternative funding sources Title funds, IMA, Medicaid to transition positions off ESSER before the funding cliff. - Board members confirmed that funds can be reallocated between line items if needs change, providing significant flexibility. Preliminary Budget Overview 2022–2023 Presented by Darren Rice, Chief Financial Officer Legislative Update - May ballot item: Voters will decide on an additional $15,000 homestead exemption increasing from $25,000 to $40,000 . Districts will be held harmless for the loss in value. - TEA attendance hold harmless: Approved for the 2021–22 school year 1st through 4th six-week periods , generating approximately $10 million for the district — essentially a break-even given attendance at ~92% vs. budgeted 94%. Budget Objectives - Meet needs for 2022–23, including opening Gordon Reed Elementary - Provide competitive compensation - Aggressive staffing allocations - Safe and productive learning environment Enrollment & ADA - Under state law, increased property tax revenue is offset dollar-for-dollar by decreased state funding — the district receives no net new funding from property value growth on the M&O side - Whether property values increase 2% or 30%, the district's total funding remains the same - Item 1: Employee Benefits and Extended Sick Leave Discussion Presenters: Darren Rice and Paula Green from HR/Benefits Background - Extended sick leave ESL was adopted by the Board in 1990. - Originally, the benefit was paid at half pay with a generous leave package 2 local leave days, 5 local sick days, 5 local funeral days . - Over time, the benefit was reduced to control costs. It was changed from half pay to half dock — a critical distinction that causes significant employee confusion. - Conroe ISD currently offers only 2 local leave days , compared to peer districts that offer 5-7 local leave days with accumulation. - Conroe ISD is believed to be the only district in the state that offers extended sick leave. How Extended Sick Leave Works - Available to full-time employees after all other leave is exhausted. - Provides up to 30 work days per school year at half-dock pay for personal illness/injury, family illness, birth/adoption of a child. - "Half dock" means half of the daily rate annual salary ÷ contract days , not half of the employee's standard paycheck — a source of major confusion. - The calculation is entirely manual — no automated system can handle the variables — creating significant administrative burden not included in the $750K annual cost figure. Key Statistics - Only 4.4% of employees ~350 people use ESL annually. - Annual cost: approximately $750,000 does not include substitute costs or administrative man-hours . - Largest ESL use category: maternity/adoption , with average usage of ~19-20 days. A significant number of employees who used ESL for this reason subsequently left the district. Peer District Comparison - A comparison chart showed peer districts offering 5-7 local leave days with accumulation rollover options. Some districts also offer separate paid funeral leave days. Conroe ISD offers neither accumulation nor separate funeral leave. Administration's Recommendation 1. Discontinue extended sick leave effective 2023-2024 school year allowing a transition period for employees to plan, accumulate days, and purchase disability insurance if desired . 2. Continue providing 3 additional local leave days approved for COVID-19 leave in 2021 for a total of 5 local leave days per year . - Estimated cost: $1 million/year based on all teachers using all 3 additional days , available to 100% of employees . - Incremental cost over current ESL: approximately $250,000 . 3. Allow employees to carry over bank unused local leave days or be paid at the substitute rate at end of fiscal year. Default would be rollover. 4. Pay out accumulated local leave days upon separation up to a $5,000 cap at substitute rate, currently ~$105/day for certified teachers . - Rationale: Prevents departing employees from burning through banked days unnecessarily; keeps teachers in classrooms. Board Discussion & Positions - Multiple board members asked clarifying questions about the math, the philosophy behind ESL, and the cost implications. - One board member questioned why the district would increase costs from $750K to $1M and expressed skepticism about the change as a recruiting tool, noting that a pay raise discussion would come in April/May anyway. - Another board member suggested keeping both ESL and additional local days. Administration explained this would remove the incentive to bank days, as employees would still burn through leave to access ESL. - Dr. Knoll emphasized the competitive disadvantage of only 2 local days and noted he'd hired ~1,000 people and never had a candidate mention ESL, but had frequent conversations about the low number of local days. - Mr. Inman stressed the importance of getting teacher feedback before making changes. - Administration confirmed next steps would include taking the proposal to the Employee Benefits Committee and District Level Planning and Decision Making Committee for feedback before returning to the Board. Important Policy Note - Mrs. Gladys noted that at the November meeting , the Board would adopt policy DEC, but clarified this relates to legislative session leave changes, not this ESL discussion. No vote was taken. This was a discussion/workshop item. Item 2: Student Support Services Overview Presenters: Dr. Chris Hines Deputy Superintendent , Dr. Tamika Taylor Assistant Superintendent for Student Support Services , Jeff Fuller and Hartwell Brown Directors of School Improvement & Transformational Leadership , Rod Chavez Director of Dropout Prevention & Recovery , Denise Apollo/Griffin Coordinator of Student Support Services , Lindsay Taylor Mental Health Specialist , and Kim Earthman Director of Student Support Services District Demographics Context Dr. Hines - District has grown from 37,000 to 67,000 students since 2003. - Current demographics: 43.1% White, 39.6% Hispanic, 9% African American, 4.9% Asian, 3.8% Two or More Races. - 17% Limited English Proficient 80+ languages spoken; estimated 25-30% of families don't speak English at home . - 39.6% economically disadvantaged up from ~27% in 2003 . - 172 students in foster care , 332 homeless students , 54 migrant family students , 381 military-connected students , 1,960 recent immigrants . - 36.2% at-risk , 9.3% receiving 504 services , 9.3% receiving special education services . School Improvement & Transformational Leadership Fuller & Brown Mission: Develop and promote exceptional leaders for student achievement through coaching. Key Activities: - Develop processes, protocols, and systems for campus leaders - Build positive culture and climate on campuses - Coach on data-driven instruction — shifting from merely discussing data to actively addressing misconceptions and modeling re-teaching strategies - Create observation and feedback cycles for principals — moving from summative to formative, small-chunk observations Transformational Leadership Pipeline 3 strands : 1. Aspire to Lead Teacher Leaders : 108 participants, 5 training sessions/year 2. Aspire to Lead Assistant Principals : 103-104 participants, 9 sessions 3. Aspiring Principals: 22-23 participants application process , 8 sessions External Partnerships: IntoLearning executive leadership , Holdsworth program leadership pipeline development Board member Mr. Hubert asked for concrete examples of transformation. Mr. Fuller provided a detailed example of transitioning campuses from data-discussion meetings to data-driven instruction meetings where teachers collaboratively analyze student misconceptions, script re-teaching approaches, and model instruction in real-time. Dropout Prevention & Recovery Mr. Chavez Key Statistics: - Attendance rate: 96.3% vs. state average pre-pandemic 2018-19 data - Cohort graduation rate: 95.3-96.4% vs. state's 90% - Dropout rate: 0.9% vs. state's 5.7-5.9% - Annual dropout rate: 0.2% vs. state's 1.9% Primary reasons for dropouts: Economic — students needing to work to support families, exacerbated by the pandemic. Key point: Once a student drops out, they cannot be counted as a dropout twice, creating an incentive for the district to continue pursuing them. Students have up to 6 years to complete graduation. Approval of Minutes — March 30, 2021 Meeting - Motion to approve: Mr. Moore - Second: Mr. McCord - Vote: Approved unanimously no opposition noted 2. 2020–2021 Campus and Facility Safety Audits Presenter: Michael Ferguson, School Safety Specialist Background on Ferguson: 23 years as an athletic trainer; opened College Park High School in 2005; served as assistant principal at Knox Junior High for four years; moved into safety specialist role in January 2020 approximately 1.5 years in position . Audit Overview: - The district completed its first three-year rotation of campus safety audits in 2019–2020. - A new rotation began in 2020–2021 covering district facilities, Conroe High School feeder zone, and Caney Creek feeder zone . - Audits were delayed to the spring semester to allow campuses to settle into the unusual pandemic school year. Audit Process: - Safety audit team typically includes Ferguson plus an assistant/associate principal; sometimes Ethan Martin or the campus principal. - Involves walkthroughs, teacher Q&A sessions about safety concerns and the Multi-Hazard Emergency Operating Procedure MEOP , and reviewing evacuation routes. - Intruder assessments are conducted: Ferguson attempts to gain access to campuses unannounced, noting whether and how quickly staff identify and stop an unrecognized person. Staff who properly challenge him receive positive recognition. - Findings are reported to campus leadership, with corrections made on-site when possible. Most Common Findings: 1. Mechanical rooms — Items stored improperly blocking access to equipment 2. Storage stacked too high — Fire code requires 18 inches clearance from sprinklers; district standard is 24 inches for consistency not all campuses have automatic sprinklers 3. Daisy-chaining electrical cords — Surge protectors plugged into each other 4. Unsecured personal belongings — Teacher purses/bags left in the open where medications or valuables could be accessed 5. Knives and sharp instruments — Particularly in CTE/culinary classrooms; a new check-in/check-out tracking system will be implemented next year in collaboration with the CTE department Key Takeaway: The district performs well overall; most findings are easily correctable issues. Committee Input: None offered at this time; members encouraged to provide input via email or phone. 3. Personal Protective Equipment PPE — Inventory and Replacement Schedule Presenters: Chris McCord and Rick Reeves Current Status: - The district is in a significantly better position regarding COVID-19 mitigation compared to one year ago. - Barbara Robertson was specifically recognized for her critical role in enabling the district to open for in-person instruction throughout the year. - Partners acknowledged: Memorial Hermann, Montgomery County, St. Luke's, Lone Star Family Health. Campus-Level PPE: - Campuses received funds in 2020–2021 to identify and purchase building-specific PPE needs. - Funds are budgeted again for 2021–2022 for continued PPE needs as necessary. District Warehouse/Inventory: - Approximately 60 pallets of hand sanitizer, masks, and gloves were received from the state in June 2020. - An off-site warehouse was secured with help from Sarah Blakelock to store state-provided supplies, as the main warehouse was staging Suchma Elementary opening supplies. - Items distributed district-wide include: gloves, wipes for Chromebooks/desks , sneeze guards/barriers, and hand sanitizer stands with refills. - Remaining inventory is substantial — still includes pallets of hand sanitizer, face shields, thermometers, gloves, masks, KN95s, and more. - Donations continue to arrive Home Depot donated two pallets of face masks; Sam's Club also donated . The district has begun declining additional hand sanitizer donations and referring donors to surrounding districts. Purchasing: - In October 2020, the district awarded a PPE bid to 120 vendors — one of few districts to do so — enabling rapid procurement 7–10 day turnaround when co-op vendors ran low. - Campuses also have access to the vetted vendor list for direct legal purchases using allocated funds. Generated 2026-03-28 13:42 UTC Unofficial Meeting Notes Conroe ISD Board Workshop April 6, 2021 These are unofficial notes based on an auto-generated transcript and may contain errors or omissions. Housekeeping - Next workshop scheduled for May 3 Monday — moved from the usual Tuesday due to Teacher Appreciation Day/Salute to Education on May 4. - Possible specially called board meeting may be gaveled in on May 3 as well. - No public comments were received; no one signed up to address the board. Item 2: TASB Pay Plans Presentation Presenters: Jennifer Barton and Amy Campbell TASB HR Services , joining remotely. Board member Mr. Moore participated in the internal pre-meeting this year Mr. Sanders in prior years . Market Overview & Process - TASB conducts annual salary studies comparing Conroe ISD to Houston-area peer districts. - Market group includes districts in the Houston area; nearly 100% participation in the survey. - For directors and above, statewide data from districts with 50,000+ enrollment is used. - Third-party data sources ERI, CompAnalyst used for non-school market comparisons. Teacher Pay Findings - Starting salary: $57,000 vs. market median of $56,000 102% of market . - Competitive at all benchmark years 0, 5, 10, 15, 20 years experience ; at or above market at every level. - Stipends offered: General Master's $1,800 vs. median $1,000 ; Special Ed self-contained $2,000 at median ; Bilingual $4,500 vs. median $4,200 . - No secondary math/science stipend currently offered; 6 of 11 peer districts do offer one. TASB recommends monitoring recruitment/retention and adding if needed. Exempt Administrative/Professional Pay - Administrative Business: 98% employee pay to market, 101% midpoint to market. - Administrative Education: 100% employee pay, 101% midpoint. - Select teacher schedule positions: 97% to market. - All groups competitive within the 90–110% range. Non-Exempt Hourly Pay - Administrative Support: 101% to market. - Instructional Support: 105% employee pay, 107% midpoint. - Auxiliary: 103% employee pay, 105% midpoint. - Police Department: 102% to market focus area from prior year; now above market . - All groups strong and competitive. TASB Recommendations 1. Adopt the proposed pay plan with adjustments. 2. 3% general pay increase across the board for all groups teachers, exempt, non-exempt . 3. Additional teacher schedule adjustments at years 7, 10, and 12–19 for market competitiveness. 4. Minimum starting rate of $12/hour for all non-exempt employees up from ~$10.85 previously ; some groups at $12.50. 5. Additional adjustments for employees below pay range minimums and for internal equity. 6. Placement scales applied for various groups 12 years to midpoint for some, 10 years for others . 7. Educator Career Pathway: ensure pay is 2% above daily teacher rate for same experience. 8. Proposed starting teacher salary: $58,500 up from $57,000 . 9. Cost model: 3% across the board = $12.191 million. Board Discussion on Pay - Mr. Sanders emphasized the importance of looking at non-school market data private sector to avoid mid-year corrections like the custodian pay adjustment earlier in the year. Custodian starting rate for next year: $12.79/hour. - Mr. Sanders asked about bus drivers — TASB confirmed bus driver pay is 1% above market; Conroe offers more duty days and guaranteed hours than most peers. Starting rate is even with market. - Mr. Sanders asked about police officer career designations master police officer, etc. — confirmed these have been incorporated into the pay file with different hourly amounts. Officer starting rates nearly $24/hour. - Mr. Moore confirmed no secondary math/science stipends are included in the $330,000 teacher adjustment figure. District has not seen a recruitment need to add these. - Mr. Williams Datren asked about the basis for the flat 3%. Explanation: TASB presented 1%, 2%, and 3% options; district leadership felt 3% was appropriate given market conditions and desire not to "short change" employees. Mr. Moore noted the 3% accounts for ~2% market fluctuation plus inflation and healthcare cost increases, so employees would realize an actual net raise. - Amy Campbell TASB noted Conroe has moved from below market to above market over five years; 3% helps maintain that position. - Dr. Null noted a retention stipend one-time payment will also be built into the budget, separate from the 3% raise. Historically $500–$675; this year could be approximately $750 . The stipend is a flat amount not percentage-based , which proportionally benefits lower-paid employees more. - Ms. Chase asked about the $15/hour minimum wage discussion. TASB responded that $15/hour is not expected to be enacted federally or at the state level in the next 2–3 years; the move to $12 is aggressive and competitive. Some Texas legislative bills would raise minimum wage for specific positions bus drivers, custodians but haven't advanced yet. Going to $15 today would cost approximately $5.7 million . No formal vote taken — workshop/informational item. Item 3: 2021–2022 Preliminary Budget Presentation Presenter: Darren Rice, Chief Financial Officer; Mrs. Garza Legislative Update - Both House and Senate budgets fully fund HB 3 state funding formula . - HB 1525 cleanup bill : Changes fast-growth allotment calculation. Conroe would drop from ~$15 million to ~$7.5 million. Being watched closely. Voted out of committee. - HB 3445 fund balance bill : Would limit unassigned fund balance to 110 days of operating expenses currently Conroe is at ~153 days . Minimum remains 90 days. Penalties for exceeding 110 days are unknown. Voted out of committee. - Unknowns: Virtual school funding mechanism; enrollment projections; COVID-19 impact on next school year. ESSER/Federal Relief Funding - ESSER I CARES Act : District received $6.5 million, but state reduced ADA to make it net zero. - ESSER II: Estimated ~$27.9 million allocation. - ESSER III ARP : Estimated ~$62.4 million allocation. State expected to recapture some, but amount is very large. - District taking a conservative approach — not spending ESSER money on recurring costs like pay raises . Dr. Null cautioned that other districts may announce large raises funded by temporary ESSER money, but Conroe prefers sustainability. - Some ESSER funds may be used by the state to shore up TRS Active Care and TRS retirement plans. Tax Rate - Current rate: $1.2125 - Projected 2021–22 rate: ~$1.19 approximately 2-cent decrease - Over three years, approximately 9-cent cumulative tax rate decrease — described as very significant for taxpayers. - HB 3 limits tax revenue growth to 2.5%; excess reduces the tax rate. - Bond promise kept: tax rate now 4 cents lower than when the 2019 bond was approved. Fund Balance - Unassigned fund balance: $153.7 million ~153 days of operating expenses - Target range: 20–25% of budget - At 20% target: $38.3 million available above target - District anticipates large fund balance increase this year from $20 million in CARES Act county funding. - Recommendation to transfer $10 million from fund balance to capital maintenance fund to complete the $50 million commitment currently at $30 million budgeted + $10 million this year = $40 million; $10 million from fund balance completes $50 million . May bring this recommendation at the April board meeting. Enrollment - 2019–20 ended at 64,799 students - 2020–21 budgeted for 66,298; opened at 64,565 due to COVID - Current enrollment as of April 5 : 65,374 — largest in district history - Growth of 809 students during the year - 2021–22 budget assumes 66,298 enrollment; could reach 67,000 - Kindergarten/Pre-K roundup: 886 students enrolled in two days — a record - ~11,000 students ~16% still remote; surveys suggest ~75% of remote students will return in person; ~3,000 may seek virtual school or other options - Virt

They are not official minutes of the Board of Trustees. Public Comment No members of the public signed up to speak. TASB Training Requirement — EISO Training for Board Members Presenter: Dr. Brian Zemlicka, Region 6 Education Service Center Dr. Zemlicka delivered the required board training mandated under Senate Bill 1566, now called EISO training covering board oversight of academic achievement. Key points included: Board Governance & Mindset - SB 1566 2017 shifted oversight of academic achievement to the board of trustees as a "team of eight" seven board members plus the superintendent , whereas previously oversight rested solely with the superintendent. - Board members are required to complete three hours of training every two years ; new members must be trained within 120 days. - Emphasized the importance of growth mindset , unity, and working together as a team even when board members disagree. - Noted that TASB adopted a new framework in November with five pillars : 1 Vision and Goal, 2 Systems and Processes, 3 Progress and Accountability, 4 Advocacy and Engagement, and 5 Synergy and Teamwork. - Stressed that the state wants boards to spend more time discussing academics in board meetings. Texas Accountability System A–F Ratings - Explained TEKS Texas Essential Knowledge and Skills → STAAR assessment → A through F accountability ratings. - Three domains determine ratings: - Student Achievement or School Progress best of the two counts for 70% of the grade - Closing the Gaps counts for 30% - At the elementary and middle school level, ratings are derived entirely from STAAR. High school ratings incorporate STAAR 40% , College/Career/Military Readiness 40% , and graduation rates 20% . - Discussed STAAR mastery levels : Masters Grade Level, Meets Grade Level, Approaches Grade Level, and Did Not Meet Grade Level. - Explained the relative performance measure that compares schools with similar socioeconomic demographics, calling it a significant improvement over the old pass/fail system. Significant Board Discussion on Equity in Accountability - Board Member Williams raised concerns about the equity implications of the accountability data, noting that talent and skill are equally distributed but opportunity is not. He argued the data can create misconceptions and drive inequitable decisions around school zoning. - Dr. Null responded that the new system is much better for schools serving economically disadvantaged populations, citing Armstrong Elementary as an example of a high-poverty school that can now earn an A by demonstrating growth, whereas under the old system it could never achieve recognition. - Dr. Null also discussed the district's tiered support system, with Houston Elementary highlighted as a success story where capacity-building efforts led to strong growth ratings. - Dr. Null noted the district added instructional coaches this year to more affluent schools that previously hadn't received coaching support, as a new investment. CCMR College, Career, Military Readiness - Outlined indicators: college readiness AP scores, dual credit, TSI criteria , career readiness industry certifications , and military readiness. - Military readiness is currently "dead in the water" because the military is not willing to release recruit data back to school districts. - Board Member unidentified asked whether JROTC programs should count as military readiness indicators; Dr. Zemlicka agreed they should but noted the issue is unresolved. - Dr. Null clarified that some districts inflated CCMR data by broadly claiming military interest, which drew TEA's attention and created the current impasse. Conroe ISD's data was accurate. - A board member asked if students can be counted multiple times across CCMR indicators — answer: no , it's a count of how many total graduates met at least one indicator. Statewide Initiatives 60 by 30 Goals: - 60% of Texans ages 25–34 to hold a certificate or degree by 2030 - 550,000 students per year completing postsecondary credentials by 2030 - All graduates having marketable skills by 2030 - Undergraduate student loan debt not exceeding 60% of first-year wages by 2030 - Board members expressed skepticism about tracking mechanisms and feasibility. Dr. Null noted that Senate Education and Higher Education committees are working on K-16/K-20 data collection. Science of Teaching Reading / Reading Academies: - State-mandated training for teachers in the science of reading instruction. - Conroe ISD applied to be its own authorized provider available only to the largest districts . - Dr. Edith Upshaw reported on pilot results: - Four pilot campuses completed the first round. - 100% of Conroe ISD teachers passed artifact submissions vs. a 46% state average for authorized providers. - Plan: approximately half of remaining elementaries next year, the other half the following year, then grades 4-5, then 6-8 — roughly a 10-year implementation . - Approximately 600-700 teachers to train next year, plus 300-400 new teachers annually. - Dr. Null noted this is an unfunded state mandate that will cost approximately $300,000-$400,000 annually for additional trainer positions due to caps on trainer-to-teacher ratios. He has asked legislators to extend the timeline but stated the district will comply regardless. - Dr. Null and board members emphasized reading as foundational to all testing performance. Required Board Goals Smart Goals - The 86th Legislature requires boards to adopt specific, quantifiable goals in three areas: 1 Early Childhood Literacy, 2 Early Childhood Math, 3 CCMR. - Goals must be five-year goals with annual targets for student groups of 25+, assigned to a district administrator, and reviewed annually at a public meeting. - Dr. Zemlicka presented example goals and goal progress measures 3 progress measures per goal . - Dr. Null stated the district's goals will be presented to the board at the April workshop. - Board discussion addressed whether students are tracked as cohorts — Dr. Null indicated data is viewed both as current-year snapshots and as cohort tracking. - TEA recommends a monitoring calendar with each goal reviewed at least four times per year. 2021-2022 Preliminary Budget Report Presenters: Darren Rice, Chief Financial Officer, and Dr. Curtis Null, Superintendent Legislative Context - State comptroller's biennial revenue estimate shows approximately a $1 billion shortfall improved from $4 billion estimate months earlier . - $112.53 billion in general fund revenue available for the biennium, down ~$400 million from current biennium. - Both House and Senate initial budgets fully fund House Bill 3 , but with no new money — meaning no additional funds for teacher raises or new programs. - Unknowns: virtual school funding, ESSER2 fund distribution, CARES Act funding flow, enrollment projections, and ongoing COVID-19 impacts. Budget Objectives for 2021-2022 - Meet needs for 2021-2022 school year - Open Hope Elementary - Provide competitive compensation plan - Continue COVID-19 mitigation support - Maintain safe environment supplies and equipment Enrollment - District has averaged 1,500 new students per year over the past decade. - 2020-2021 budget assumed 66,298 students; actual snapshot October was 64,565 — approximately 1,700 below budget. - As of January 28: 65,119 students up ~550 since October snapshot . - For 2021-2022, the district is budgeting enrollment at 66,298 — essentially flat with the prior year's budget, no net student growth assumed . ADA and State Funding - District budgets expenditures on enrollment but is funded by the state based on Average Daily Attendance ADA .

Public Hearing — Annual Performance Report Dr. Hines presented highlights of the annual performance report: - Rating: Not rated due to state disaster declaration; no STAAR scores for 2019-2020. - Special Education: Received "Needs Assistance" determination due to missed timelines during school closures. - Enrollment: 64,517 students end of 2019-2020 . - Disciplinary Placements: 10-year trend has leveled off after years of improvement. - Demographics: 80+ home languages; largest non-English is Spanish ~15,000 households . - Attendance 2019 : 96.3% above state average of 95.4% . - Dropout Rate 2019 : 0.2% annual rate grades 9-12 ; below state average. - Graduation Cohort 2019 : 96.4% four-year graduation rate; 97.7% five-year rate. - College Readiness: ~66% scored 3+ on AP exams; 23.3% earned dual credit; 61.3% met SAT/ACT criteria; average SAT score of 1153. - Financial: - Five-Star Smart Schools recipient for 11 consecutive years - Unassigned fund balance: $153,677,343 27% of budget - Tax rate: $1.21 one of the lowest in the region - Per-student instructional spending: $8,664 below most peer districts - Administration costs: 1.78% of budget state average: 3.25% - Staff: 63.6% professional staff; average teacher experience 11 years; turnover rate 13.9%; average salary $59,725. - Criminal Incidents: 37 reportable incidents at 8 campuses 32 were felony controlled substance ; all at secondary schools; numbers were down partly due to remote learning. - Counselors/Librarians: 158 counselors, 64 librarians; at least one of each at every campus. - No public comments were offered on the report. Corrective Actions presented by Dr. Horton, Fine Arts : - Mandatory annual inventory with established reporting dates, submitted to Dr. Horton. - Standardized written procedures for checking out and tracking instruments. - Principals will now be required to sign off on annual inventory reports previously, accountability ran only through Fine Arts . - Campuses that do not complete annual inventory will not be eligible for critical needs purchases the annual program to replenish/replace instruments . - Standardized purchasing specifications for instruments established with the Purchasing Department. - A central inventory system using Charms software with actual manufacturer serial numbers not district-assigned numbers for granular tracking. - Next phases will include 5th and 6th grade band instrument inventory and string instrument inventory. - Directors were informed of findings at a January 4th training; individual campus post-audit reports were shared. - The internal auditor confirmed a follow-up audit random sampling at high schools and junior highs would be conducted after approximately six months. Discussion on broader inventory management: - Rice noted that computer/technology inventory has been well-managed, but with COVID-related Chromebook and hotspot distribution, ensuring Tip Web the technology inventory system is fully updated is a current priority. Item 5A: District of Innovation Plan Presenter: Dr. Chris Hines The district's District of Innovation DOI designation, first established in 2015, requires renewal every five years. A public hearing was held October 20, 2020, and the District Level Planning and Decision-Making Committee developed the updated plan. The renewed plan includes two exemptions: 1. School Start Date — Allows the district to begin instruction before the fourth Monday in August, enabling more balanced semester calendars and concluding the fall semester before winter break. 2. Teacher Certification NEW — Allows the district to hire individuals without a standard teaching certificate for hard-to-fill CTE positions e.g., welding, automotive technology, health sciences if the individual has: - Relevant work experience - Formal training or education - Industry licensure, certification, or registration Safeguards built into the certification exemption: - Credential guidelines must be established for the position beforehand - A training plan pedagogy and classroom management is required - Superintendent approval is required - The exemption is only triggered after normal hiring processes fail to find certified applicants - The superintendent will periodically update the board on usage Board Discussion: - One board member noted the proposed language doesn't specifically mention CTE, though the explanation document part of the official record clarifies the intended narrow application. - Another member confirmed this exemption is separate from alternative certification programs and would only be used when normal hiring fails. - Dr. Hines indicated this would be used very rarely, citing a past example of being unable to start a healthcare science program due to lack of certified applicants. Item 1: Bond Sale Results & Enrollment/Budget Update Presented by: Mr. Darrin Rice, with comments from Dr. Null Bond Refunding Results Preliminary - The district completed a bond sale earlier that day, refunding approximately $170 million in 2012 bonds plus a $189 million new money sale. - Results were preliminary books had just closed , but the district achieved approximately $50.067 million in savings — exceeding the initial estimate of $43 million and hitting the hoped-for $50 million target. - Savings represent over 20% on refunded bonds . - The refunding did not extend the bond term — stayed within the existing 10-year window. - Overall new money interest rate: 2.47% , compared to the 4.5% used during the bond referendum, representing approximately $48 million in additional capacity for the bond program. - The market was volatile that morning; the team waited until 10:00 AM rather than the usual 8:30 AM to find a favorable entry point. Enrollment Update - Budgeted enrollment: 66,298 anticipated 1,500 student growth - Budgeted ADA: 62,452 using 94.2% attendance rate - Current enrollment as of Oct. 5 : 64,527 — 1,771 students below budget - Current ADA: 1,668 below budget - The state extended its hold harmless provision by an additional six weeks through the first semester , holding the district harmless at an ADA of 62,852 — actually 400 higher than what CISD budgeted. - This is the first year in recent history where enrollment trended downward; the district is 271 students below where it ended last year. - However, compared to the same date last year , enrollment is approximately 570 students higher . - The district is adding approximately 20 students per day about 100 per week . Dr. Null expressed confidence the district would exceed 65,000 by end of October. Enrollment by Grade Level - 930 of the missing students are in early education pre-K, kindergarten, and first grade — consistent with statewide and nationwide trends. - The district launched a social media campaign encouraging families to enroll pre-K and kindergarten students, emphasizing it's "not too late." - Dr. Null expressed concern that missing early childhood education will have long-term academic consequences. - Board discussion clarified that compulsory school attendance age in Texas is six first grade , not seven as initially stated. Dr. Hines corrected this during his later presentation. Budget Impact - Projected decrease in state funding: approximately $5.3 million including hold harmless . - The adopted budget included a $5.46 million surplus — essentially bringing the district to break even in the worst-case scenario. - Dr. Null emphasized this is the worst case; continued enrollment growth means the district will not be in a deficit and will maintain a surplus. - The fund balance remains strong , positioning the district well. - Budget preparation for 2021-2022 will be "unlike anything we've ever dealt with" due to uncertainty around remote learning policies and enrollment projections. Teacher Retirements Discussion - Board member asked about rumors of increased teacher retirements statewide. - Ms. Green confirmed more resignations and retirements than usual due to COVID, though she hadn't broken it down by age. - Dr. Null noted more "late resignations" just before or during school and expects more mid-year retirees than ever before. - Contributing factors include health concerns and the difficulty of adapting to new teaching models. - The district maintains a running spreadsheet tracking resignations weekly. - Despite increased turnover, the applicant pipeline remains strong — more applicants attending virtual job fairs than there are retirements. Student teachers also provide a hiring pool. Holdsworth Center Leadership Development Program Presented by: Dr. Taylor Overview - The Holdsworth Center was founded in 2017 by Charles Butt HEB chairman , named after his mother an educator , and funded by a $100 million investment . - The program provides free leadership development for school districts compared to alternatives costing $130,000–$230,000 . - Leadership philosophy: 70% from job experiences, 20% from coaching/interactions, 10% from formal education. - Curriculum covers personal leadership development, empowering others, and creating systemic change. Program Details - Cost: Free aside from in-state transportation, currently waived due to COVID — all virtual . - Participants: Superintendent + five district-level leaders. - Duration: Two-year program with site visits and coursework. - Current participants: 13 districts statewide across two cohorts, including comparably-sized districts: Round Rock ISD, Arlington ISD, and Klein ISD . - Currently accepting applications for Cohort 3 ; application due November 20, 2020 . - If selected, the district would begin in summer 2021 . Board Discussion - Board members asked about curriculum specifics Dr. Taylor has seen testimonials/videos but not the full curriculum , post-program obligations none beyond the two-year commitment , and selection competitiveness. - The Holdsworth Center directly reached out to Conroe ISD to apply for Cohort 3 they did not reach out for Cohorts 1 or 2 . - This does not require board approval — Dr. Null shared it as informational. There is no cost to apply. - Dr. Taylor expressed hope to be included among the six participants. Laughter from the board. Item 2: District of Innovation Plan Reauthorization Presented by: Dr. Hines assisted by Mr. Hartwell Brown and Dr. Pam Zoda Background - The 84th Texas Legislature 2015 created the District of Innovation option via HB 1842 . - Allows districts to design innovation plans for local flexibility without going through the state waiver system. - Must be renewed every five years — the current plan expires in January 2021 . Current Plan - Currently used only for the flexible early start calendar — allows starting before the fourth Monday in August. - The district is eligible to apply not in substandard achievement status . - Approval requires two-thirds board vote . Proposed Changes for New Plan The district is considering adding CTE Career and Technical Education certification flexibility : - Would allow hiring industry professionals without standard teaching certificates for hard-to-fill CTE positions e.g., welding, collision repair, auto technology, health sciences . - Would be narrowly defined — only for specific positions where certified applicants cannot be found. - Would likely require Dr. Null's individual approval for each hire. - Neighboring districts Aldine, Fort Bend, Humble, Katy, Klein, Spring, Tomball already include this in their innovation plans. Special Education/CTE: Would also allow existing special education life skills teachers who teach career courses to receive CTE course coding, enabling weighted pupil funding — a direct financial benefit without changing current practices. Board Discussion - Significant concerns raised about: - Board oversight of non-certified hires — Board member John appears to be Mr. Husbands emphasized the board should have direct knowledge of any such hires, not just macro-level awareness. - Compensation equity between certified and non-certified teachers. - Ensuring non-certified hires receive adequate pedagogical training and support. - Dr. Null clarified that non-certified hires would still go through the full interview process and would likely be known quantities from industry partnerships. - Dr. Hines noted the district has managed to fill all CTE positions for the past five years without this flexibility, but

Key Discussion Points: - Sam Houston Elementary's overall rating improved from 54 to 74 in prior years; the campus showed significant progress in Domain 2A student growth in reading/math and Domain 3 closing the gaps . - No 2020 STAAR test was administered due to COVID; therefore the campus did not receive a 2020 rating and must continue previously ordered school improvement sanctions. - The plan includes essential actions focused on instructional planning 5.1 , proactive behavior management 3.1 , and new climate surveys accounting for COVID-related learning loss . - Key supports: family engagement liaison 3rd year , positive behavior support liaison 4th year, grant-funded , content teacher leader training, and weekly district administrative support. - Mr. Hubert praised the district for not rezoning the school to change demographics but instead investing resources to improve the campus and engage the community. He asked what made the biggest difference — Dr. Taylor identified curriculum/content support and the family engagement liaison which transformed parent involvement from minimal to "packed to capacity" . - Dr. Sanders personally thanked Dr. Taylor for her leadership and acknowledged Principal Viviana Harris. - Mr. Williams expressed concern about long-term sustainability of resources once the campus reaches acceptable performance levels. Dr. Null assured the board that support would not be abandoned, emphasizing investment in building teacher capacity from within and that the campus will remain high-risk given its 80% economically disadvantaged population. - Mr. Husbands emphasized that family engagement is the true long-term solution. - Mr. Kidd encouraged documentation of the success story for replication. Dr. Null noted that Dr. Taylor now leads a full Department of School Improvement for that purpose. Vote: Motion and second on record. Passed unanimously. Item 4C — Asynchronous Instruction Plan for 2020–2021 Presented by Dr. Hines and Jeff Fuller. Key Points: - TEA requires districts using asynchronous remote instruction to submit a plan for approval to receive full-day ADA funding. - CISD selected the asynchronous model for its flexibility — allowing synchronous components, self-paced activities, evening engagement, etc. - The plan has four components: 1 Instructional Schedule ~300 minutes/day, exceeding TEA's 240-minute minimum , 2 Material Design TEKS-aligned, with supports for students with disabilities and English learners , 3 Student Progress daily/weekly feedback, grading consistent with face-to-face, trackable engagement for attendance , 4 Implementation parent communication, staff development — ~71 pages of PD documentation . - Region 6 reviewed the plan and called it "one of the best" they had seen. - ~90% of Texas districts are using the asynchronous model. Significant Board Discussion: - Mr. Hubert asked about teacher accessibility requirements; was assured teachers are NOT required to be available 24/7 and are encouraged to publish weekly schedules. - Dr. Sanders raised concerns about daily feedback demands on teachers, especially in high school classes of 35 students. Dr. Null noted feedback can include grading assignments and after-hours tutoring support. - Mr. Husbands questioned why teachers must teach both platforms simultaneously rather than assigning some teachers exclusively to online and others to in-person. Dr. Null explained this allows smaller class sizes for better social distancing e.g., 12 per class vs. 20 . Mr. Husbands expressed disagreement that online learning is a good long-term model for K-12, emphasizing children's socialization needs. He also expressed concern about teacher burnout from the dual-platform workload. - Mr. Hubert clarified that this plan is specifically required for TEA funding approval. Dr. Null confirmed: without approval, ~20,000 online families would have to return to face-to-face. He also noted that Texas law does not currently allow permanent online public education — this is a one-year-only authorization. - Dr. Hines noted TEA may request tweaks as part of a negotiated approval process. Vote: Motion by Mr. Moore, seconded by Mr. Husbands. Passed unanimously. Urged the board to remember that thousands of students have similar stories. Board member Sanders commended him for his courage in speaking. Carrie Frye Meyer – CISD employee, parent, and resident. Thanked Dr. Null and the board for the difficult decision to postpone in-person instruction. Described receiving numerous calls from fearful teachers, some of whom contracted COVID before spring break or have children with special needs. Acknowledged the challenges of adapting to TEA and CDC changes. Emphasized the importance of protecting the safety of all staff, including bus drivers and cafeteria workers. Consent Agenda Item 2 - Motion: Mr. Husbands - Vote: Passed unanimously no items removed Curriculum & Instruction Item 3A: Resolution to Suspend STAAR Testing and Modify Accountability Ratings for 2020–2021 Presented by: Mr. Moore trustee-initiated item Mr. Moore summarized a resolution calling upon TEA, the Governor, and the Legislature to suspend STAAR testing and the related accountability rating system for the 2020–2021 school year, citing the lack of any 2020 STAAR data and the disruption caused by pandemic-related closures. Key Discussion: - Dr. Null expressed support, emphasizing that lost instructional days to testing would be more impactful than the test itself, particularly given distance learning and a planned September 8 return to in-person instruction. Stated the District is "not afraid of testing" but every instructional moment will be precious. - Mr. Inman spoke in favor, noting the District's strong accountability track record. - Mr. Hubert discussed frustrations with COVID and emphasized that the health of all 8,000 employees and 65,000+ students must be considered. Expressed support for the delayed school start, acknowledging it was Dr. Null's call based on advice from TEA and the Montgomery County Public Health authority. - Dr. Null clarified the District's goal is to be in school on September 8 and to keep school open; rushing back August 12 could lead to additional closures. Approximately 35% of students were signing up for the online model. Explained the quarantine requirements 14-day isolation for positive cases and direct exposures as a key operational challenge. - Mr. Sanders supported the resolution, noting testing days take away from instruction and that students who missed 14 days shouldn't be immediately tested on content they weren't present to learn. - Mr. Husbands supported the resolution and commended Dr. Null for the "ramp up" approach, acknowledging the difficulty of going from zero to 66,000+ students. Noted teachers must simultaneously manage in-person and online instruction. - Dr. Null outlined plans to begin bringing highest-need students into buildings during the second or third week of online education, before full return on September 8. Vote: Passed unanimously Item 3B: Purchase of Branching Minds Web Application Multi-Tiered System of Support Presented by: Dr. Tamika Taylor Branching Minds is a web application to support the District's MTSS implementation, aligning to IDEA and ESSA requirements for academics, behavior, and social-emotional support. It uses data to identify struggling students and create intervention plans. - Cost: $249,500 funded from the previously approved ReStart Grant total grant: $1.94 million - Scope: All students, K–12 - Term: Three-year license; renewal funding would need to be identified separately Vote: Passed unanimously Administration Item 4A: Texas Education for Homeless Children and Youth Continuation Grant 2020–2021 Presented by: Dr. Upshaw Second year of a competitive discretionary grant providing approximately $63,750. Funds pay 84% of a professional service liaison salary, with the remainder covering mileage and related costs. Last year the program served 562 homeless students. Discussion: - Mr. Kidd commended the program and mentioned Purposity, a public-facing app for meeting student needs. - Mr. Inman asked about caseload; Dr. Upshaw clarified the liaison does not work alone but is part of a team. - Mr. Sanders asked about the youngest students served; Dr. Upshaw said services extend from pre-K and the District looks at whole-family needs. Vote: Passed unanimously Item 4B: Houston Galveston Area Council Clean Vehicles Grant Presented by: Chris McCord Grant award of $664,961 to scrap 10 buses 1995 and 1997 models without A/C or seatbelts and purchase 10 new buses with A/C and seatbelts at approximately $102,000 each. Goals include reducing emissions and improving air quality. The District has one year to use the funds and must submit quarterly monitoring reports for five years. Discussion: - Mr. Sanders asked about remaining buses without A/C and seatbelts. The District has approximately 575 total buses; roughly 100 lack A/C. Over 300 daily routes are run, and route buses are approximately 97%+ equipped with A/C. - Mr. Moore asked about fuel type; the new buses will be clean diesel. The District also operates 46 propane buses. Vote: Passed unanimously Item 4C: ESSER Grant Application Elementary and Secondary School Emergency Relief Fund Presented by: Mr. Rice The District is eligible for $6,323,624 under the CARES Act to replace lost FSP Foundation School Program funding due to ADA losses during COVID-19 school closures. The grant generates no additional revenue — the District's FSP entitlement will be reduced

Consulting Engagement: Construction Manager at Risk CMAR vs. Competitive Sealed Proposal CSP Internal Audit Recommendation: - Conroe ISD currently uses only the CMAR method for construction. - All peer districts use CSP , and of the 20 districts represented by Mickey Morris, Conroe ISD is the only one that does not use CSP . - Internal Audit recommends the full board approve using the CSP method and designate one of the four new elementary schools in the bond as a CSP pilot project . - Potential savings of up to 5% were cited — on a $30 million project, that could mean $1.5 million in savings . Discussion and Concerns: Mr. Foster Planning & Construction was brought into the call to provide input: - CSP is essentially a lump-sum bid — contractor gives a fixed price; changes require change orders. - CMAR is more of a contractor partnership — allows less detailed drawings with flexibility to achieve desired outcomes without as many change orders. - Mr. Foster expressed a preference for CMAR , citing his department's efficiency with the current method and concerns about CSP: - CSP is more staff-intensive during construction. His department has 5.5 staff compared to Katy ISD's approximately 16 staff , which uses CSP. - CSP could lead to less desirable contractors winning on price alone. - CMAR delivers final cost savings at project completion, while CSP aims for first cost savings . - He cited an example of Humble ISD bidding one elementary school three times under CSP before getting close to budget, and still having to increase the budget. - Mr. Foster confirmed he could successfully build a school under either method and noted that CSP is the state's default method under government code, while the Conroe ISD board previously set CMAR as its local default. Committee member Del asked whether the district can choose project-by-project — confirmed yes , the board can select the method on a per-project basis. Mr. Sanders raised concerns about contractor performance risks under CSP, including penalties for lateness and the risk of contractors walking away from projects. Dr. Knoll cautioned: - The next elementary Grand Oaks does not yet have land acquired and may be on a short timeline. - Recommended being strategic about timing for a CSP pilot — suggested the last elementary in the bond program may be a better candidate to avoid sabotaging the pilot. - Emphasized this was not the time for a full debate since not all board members were present; it will need to be placed on a future full board agenda. Outcome: No vote taken — this was presented as an Internal Audit recommendation/report only. Further discussion will occur at a future full board meeting. 7. PEIMS Attendance Audit Key Findings: - Not all schools were in compliance with TEA attendance-taking requirements. - Attendance-taking times were highly inconsistent — some recorded as early as 5:00 a.m. and as late as 6:00 p.m. Recommendations: - Schools should be reminded of the importance of compliance due to risk of fines and impact on revenue streams. - The PEIMS office will conduct audits of schools four times per year , incorporating the data-crunching methodology used by Internal Audit. Administration Response: - Dr. Knoll made it a priority at the next principals' meeting, quantifying the dollar amount at risk from attendance errors. - Dr. Hahn confirmed training was conducted at principals' meetings twice, reviewing standard practices, and working directly with specific campuses where problems were identified. - Dr. Knoll shared an example of Grand Oaks High School implementing a structured process where a student announcement reminds teachers to take attendance at the designated time. 8. Audit Report Format Mr. Hayden asked the committee for feedback on the report format being used, with a focus on being more data-driven quantifying costs and risks . Committee consensus: The format works well. Members appreciated having the full report in advance and the summary presentation during the meeting. 9. Campus Audits During Principal Transitions A committee member asked whether cursory audits during principal changes would continue, given the audit schedule changes. - Mr. Hayden: Currently not planned due to limited resources being redirected to higher-risk areas, but could be added back if the committee desires. - Dr. Knoll proposed that the finance office accountant who coordinates Activity Funds could perform high-level audits during principal/secretary transitions and flag concerns to Internal Audit. - Mr. Hayden supported this as a good internal control — audit can then review the control process rather than visiting each campus. - Darren Rice noted that reconciling accounts at transition points could involve some historical review, but agreed a robust audit process wasn't needed for routine transitions. 10. Procurement Audit — Status Update - Currently in progress and winding down . - Final report expected to be delivered to the committee by the end of May 2020 . 11. Next Meeting Date - Tentative date set: Wednesday, September 9, 2020 ahead of the September 15 board meeting . - Acknowledged uncertainty about what September may look like due to COVID-19; date will be firmed up over the summer as TEA provides more clarity. 12. Adjournment Vote: Motion to adjourn — Passed unanimously voice vote Summary of Key Actions & Takeaways | Item | Outcome | | | | | Prior meeting minutes | Approved unanimously | | EthicsPoint reports 3 closed | Accepted unanimously | | Audit plan changes payroll deferred, construction audits added, follow-ups reduced | Approved unanimously | | CSP vs. CMAR recommendation | Report received; no vote taken. To be discussed at future full board meeting | | PEIMS Attendance Audit | Report received; corrective actions already underway by administration | | Principal transition audits | Finance office to handle initial reviews; Internal Audit to audit the control | | Procurement Audit | In progre

Compensation / Pay Plan Presentation TASB HR Services Presenters: Jennifer Barton and Amy Campbell TASB HR Services , with acknowledgment of Board Member Ray Sanders' participation in the process. Market Competitiveness Findings - Conroe ISD remains competitive with the local Houston-area market across most employee groups. - Teacher pay at Year 0 starting salary : $55,500 — exactly even with the local market median. - Years 5 and 10: Slightly below market at 98% and 99% respectively gaps of $1,322 and $701 . - Years 15 and 20: Even with market. - Board members noted that these figures reflect base salary only , not total compensation insurance/benefits , which would further close the gap. - The Year-over-year market shifts were largely driven by House Bill 3 volatility , which required some districts to give large raises to experienced teachers. Teaching Area Stipends - Masters degree: $1,000–$1,800 at or above market median - Secondary math and science: No stipend currently offered; Dr. Sharples noted it's becoming increasingly challenging to fill these positions as the hiring season progresses. - Special education self-contained: $2,000 at market median - Bilingual: $4,500 above market Non-Exempt / Hourly Workers - Starting rates are at or above market across the board. - Targeted efforts have been made for special education aides and bus drivers. Other Exempt Groups Administrators, etc. - Administrative education group: Very competitive. - All groups generally competitive due to consistent annual pay increases. Police Department Pay - Police pay structure was noted at 86% of market for employee pay. - Internal equity is currently strong, but the department faces significant recruiting challenges: - 11 vacant officer positions currently - Losing candidates to other agencies by $8,000–$10,000 in total compensation - Experience credit policy is outdated 1 year credit for 5 years of experience, unchanged for ~18 years; most agencies now give 1-for-1 - No certification pay intermediate, advanced, master currently offered, unlike competing agencies - Applicant pool has dwindled overall for law enforcement - Chief and staff are developing a survey/proposal on certification pay and experience credit adjustments, expected within approximately one month. Recommended Pay Models Model 1: | Group | General Increase | | -| --| | Teachers | 2.5% | | Other Exempt | 2.0% | | Non-Exempt | 3.0% | | Bus Drivers | $0.75/hr 3.8% | | Overall with adjustments | 2.9% | Model 2: | Group | General Increase | | -| --| | Teachers | 3.0% | | Other Exempt | 2.5% | | Non-Exempt | 3.5% | | Bus Drivers | $1.00/hr 5.0% | | Overall with adjustments | 3.5% | Both models include 0.4% in targeted adjustments for: - Teachers at years 2–15 incremental, not full correction at once - 1% above minimum for employees falling below pay grade minimum - Placement scale improvements: reduced from 15 years to 12 years to midpoint for most support/auxiliary; 10 years to midpoint for instructional support aides and police - 2% above teacher daily rate for educator career pathway positions counselors, APs, principals Budget was built on Model 2. Significant Board Discussion on Compensation Philosophy - Multiple board members expressed strong support for paying employees well but emphasized fiscal responsibility and not exceeding 100% of market. - Board Member John argued that Conroe ISD's desirability as an employer means it shouldn't need to pay above market; noted total compensation including superior benefits makes the district more competitive than salary alone suggests. - Board Member Datren questioned whether the $500–$1,300 gaps were truly causing teacher attrition and urged a more holistic perspective. - Dr. Null cautioned that parts of the district those adjacent to higher-paying districts struggle to recruit teachers and the district cannot adopt a "land of plenty" attitude across the board. - Amy Campbell TASB noted that Active Care insurance volatility could affect competitiveness, as some districts are standing up alternative insurance programs. - Several board members expressed that administrators should receive the same percentage increase as teachers not less , citing the demands of their roles and the lean central office staff. - Board Member John specifically advocated for 3% across the board, possibly 3.5% , noting CPI inflation is 2.1–2.6%. - Board expressed interest in seeing a third model with more uniform percentages across employee groups. Preliminary Budget 2020–2021 Presenter: Darrin Rice, Chief Financial Officer Key Budget Assumptions - Enrollment growth: 1,400 new students total projected: 66,198 - Current enrollment as of meeting date: 64,916 - Significant board discussion about whether pre-K expansion would add students beyond the 1,400 projection; Dr. Null and staff noted projections are based on existing pilot data and are intentionally conservative - Property value growth: 5.5% estimated $2.1 billion increase; total: $40.2 billion - Opening Stockton Junior High School and adding 12th grade at Grand Oaks High School Fund Balance - Ended FY2019 at ~$140 million - Board transferred $32 million out: $12M for technology/2019 bond; $20M to establish capital maintenance fund - Unassigned fund balance: $134.7 million 24.5% of budget — within target range of 20–25% Tax Rate - Conroe ISD remains the second lowest tax rate in the greater Houston area at $1.23 - 15 cents below peer district average - Proposed 2020–21 tax rate: $1.2135 decrease of 1.65 cents on M&O; debt service unchanged at $0.26 - House Bill 3 now limits tax revenue growth to 2.5% annually regardless of actual property value growth - State will calculate the district's maximum compressed rate for the first time this year Revenue & Expenditure Summary Revenue: | Item | Amount | | | --| | Beginning Revenue | $555.62M | | Local Revenue 5.5% AV growth | $13.83M | | Net State Funding 1,400 students | $8.76M | | TRS In-Kind | $3.5M | | Total Revenue | $581.71M | Expenditures: | Item | Amount | | | --| | Beginning Expenditures amended | $552.33M | | Salary Increases Model 2 | $12.0M | | Substitute Pay Increase $5/day | $0.3M | | Additional Personnel for Growth | $12.0M | | Other Expenses utilities, fuel, etc. | $1.5M | | TRS In-Kind Offset | $3.5M | | Total Estimated Increase | $24.3M | | Remaining for Intermediate/Elementary Staffing & Retention Stipend | ~$5.08M | Intermediate and Elementary Staffing Needs - Dr. Null identified intermediates as having the highest student-to-staff ratios and struggling both academically and with climate/culture. - Consideration being given to adjusting staffing formula currently 26:1 and/or adding specialized positions e.g., student support teachers for testing, 504 . - Elementary schools with only one AP and one counselor also need relief, particularly those with specialized programs 504, bilingual/ESL . - ~$5 million placeholder; any remaining funds could be directed to an employee retention stipend . - A budget amendment of $2.75M will be brought to the board for the current year to cover 600+ additional students above projections. Capital Maintenance Fund - Built-in annual contribution of $10 million - Has been used to purchase land and buses Academic / Policy Updates Effective Schools Framework ESF Presenter: Dr. Taylor - State model for school improvement with five levers: strong school leadership, effective teachers, positive school culture, high-quality curriculum, and effective instruction. - Used at Sam Houston Elementary last year

Concerns heard: - Perceived luxury items e.g., artificial turf - Overall bond amount was too large/scary - Information was overly complicated - Misinformation was not adequately addressed - Historical projects caused concern Jet Center/Sam Houston, Conroe HS - Not all teachers were on board; some campuses felt they didn't benefit - Urgency of need was not communicated well enough - Timing May election vs. November - Specific items: turf, teacher training center, maintenance items in bond Suggestions for future bond: - KISS principle—keep it simple - Communicate differently and better educate the community - Clarify that 2015 bond was a 4-year bond - Communicate ramifications of bond failure - Remove "fluff," tighten the package - Consider Proposition A / Proposition B structure - Highlight safety and security more prominently Revised Bond Proposal "We Listened" Key Changes from the $807M May Package | Action | Impact | | | | | $20M from fund balance land, buses, capital maintenance | -$36M including avoided interest | | $10M/year to capital maintenance fund 5 years | -$50M from bond; avoids ~$40M interest | | Extended bond from 4 years to 5 years | Added 1 additional elementary school | | Removed teacher training center, ag complex, Jet Center | Reduction | | Removed Conroe HS 9th grade campus addition | -$11.9M | | Removed custodial/maintenance facility | -$10.1M funded by capital maintenance | | Hawke building conversion | Funded by capital maintenance cash | | Technology reduced from $36M to $20M | -$16M | | Buses & technology financed at 10 years; all else at 25 years | — | | Turf moved to standalone Proposition B | Voter choice | Proposed Package | Proposition | Amount | | | | | Proposition A schools, safety/security, infrastructure | $660 million | | Proposit
