Attendees - Dr. Curtis Null , Superintendent - Darrin Rice , Chief Financial Officer - Dr. Chris Heinz , Deputy Superintendent Academics - Dr. Winkler , district administrator early education - EZ likely Erich Ziehe , Facilities/Construction - Board Members referenced by name : Skeeter President Williams , John Husbands, Scott Moore, Scott Kidd, Ray last name unclear , Datren last name unclear , Mr. Inman, Mr. Sanders Linda also briefly mentioned Key Topics Discussed 1. House Bill 3 HB 3 Legislative Update Overview: HB 3 represents a major overhaul of Texas school finance. Mr. Rice emphasized that information is still arriving daily and is subject to change. A phone call received at 3:30 PM the day of the meeting revealed a $7 million error to the district's disadvantage in funding calculations from Region 13's spreadsheet — specifically a Golden Penny miscalculation related to the district's prior tax rate swap from $1.06 to $1.04. Key HB 3 Provisions Discussed: - Basic Allotment Increase: From $5,140 to $6,160 per ADA - Tax Compression: 7-cent compression of Tier 1 M&O tax rate from $1.04 to $0.97 - Net Revenue Increase: Approximately $33.6 million after accounting for lost local tax revenue first decrease in local tax revenue since 2005 - Property Tax Relief: $5 billion statewide; district will see first tax revenue decrease since 2005 - Teacher Pay: $2 billion statewide; requirement to use 30% of revenue gain for compensation increases - Full-Day Pre-K: Mandated for eligible 4-year-olds discussed in detail below New/Changed Allotments: - Compensatory Education: New tiered index based on census blocks weights .225 to .275 — still awaiting details from TEA - Bilingual/Dual Language: New weights generating ~$1.5 million - Career & Technology CTE : Extended to 7th grade from 9th ; generates ~$1.5 million - Transportation: Changed from linear density to $1.00/mile — roughly break-even at $7.3 million - Dyslexia Allotment NEW : Weight of .01; district dyslexia students grew from 2,342 2016-17 to 3,244 current year - Fast-Growth School District Allotment NEW : Weight of .04 for total ADA; generates $14 million — state's offset for moving to current-year property values and repealing Cost of Education Index - Dropout Recovery: Still determining if district qualifies Repealed Allotments: - Gifted and Talented: $2 million lost program must still be maintained - High School Allotment: $4.7 million lost — significant impact on high school budgets - Staff Allotment: ~$1.6 million lost helped with insurance costs Other Significant Changes: - Move to current-year property values elimination of one-year lag — district will feel this impact in 2020-21; major concern expressed - Cost of Education Index repealed — study ordered for possible future reinstatement - Golden Pennies increased from 6 to 8 — district currently accesses 4; can add one penny with unanimous board vote no TRE required ; additional pennies would require a Tax Ratification Election - Recapture: District has moved further from Chapter 41/49 threshold 2. Full-Day Pre-Kindergarten Mandate Dr. Heinz presented implementation challenges: - Current enrollment projection: 1,468 pre-K students in half-day sessions across 25 campuses - Expected growth: ~850 current kindergarteners would have been eligible for pre-K; going full-day will increase enrollment - Eligibility expanded: Now includes children of any CISD teacher in addition to existing criteria: socioeconomic need, English language learners, military, foster, homeless, Texas Star Award recipients - Classroom need: Approximately 65-70 additional classrooms needed roughly another campus worth of space - Staffing: Currently 49.5 allocations 29.5 regular ed, 20 bilingual ; 19 PPCD allocations — numbers would roughly double - Current payroll: ~$3.25 million; would increase significantly - Classroom outfitting cost: ~$22,000 per classroom - Transportation impact: Currently ~400 AM / ~500 PM riders ~60-70% ; would essentially double morning pickup and afternoon dropoff - Additional impacts being studied: Clinic staffing, lunch capacity, specials rotation, conference period solutions 11 campuses lack gyms , curriculum development for full-day program Waiver Options: Districts may seek two 3-year waivers. Administration recommended exercising the first waiver, noting the district cannot implement fully overnight. Discussion of possible pilot programs at 1-2 campuses. Board Discussion: - Mr. Moore asked about eligibility changes — confirmed same as current plus teacher's children - Mr. Kidd asked about the rationale — Dr. Heinz and Dr. Winkler cited research on early education impact, social-emotional readiness, and learning readiness - Mr. Husbands asked about the classroom need's impact on prior bond/demographic planning — Dr. Heinz confirmed this was NOT planned for in the last bond ; represents roughly another campus worth of space - Discussion of district-wide early childhood center concept — acknowledged feasibility in dense areas but not district-wide due to geographic spread - Board member asked about pushback from other districts — administration noted the legislature did not consult districts; "it wasn't really a conversation" 3. Teacher/Employee Compensation HB 3 Requirement: 30% of FSP revenue gain $10.08 million minimum must go to compensation increases for non-administrative full-time employees. Of that, 75% $7.56 million must go to teachers, counselors, nurses, and librarians, prioritizing those with 5+ years experience. The remaining 25% $2.52 million may go to other full-time non-administrative employees. Five scenarios presented after $7M adjustment : | Scenario | Teachers/Librarians/Nurses/Counselors | All Other FT non-admin | Administrators | Total Cost | | -| --| --| -| | | 1 | 3.5% $8.4M | 3.0% $2.4M | 2.5% $1.6M | $12.4M | | 2 | 4.0% $9.6M | 3.0% $2.4M | 2.5% $1.6M | $13.6M | | 3 | 4.0% $9.6M | 3.5% $2.8M | 3.0% $1.96M | $14.36M | | 4 | 4.5% | 3.5% | 3.0% | ~$15.4M | | 5 | 5.0% | 4.0% | 3.0% | ~$17-20M | Note: Scenario 3 was initially recommended before the $7M error; after the error, administration recommended backing down to approximately Scenario 3 level or below. Significant Board Discussion: - Mr. Moore raised concerns about classification of LSSPs, speech pathologists, instructional coaches, and RTI specialists — where do they fall in these categories? - Dr. Null acknowledged this is an active debate; campus-based people in top line, AE pay scale positions LSSPs, diagnosticians still being sorted - Mr. Husbands noted historical raises have been ~3% per year; expressed caution about long-term sustainability given uncertain future funding - Skeeter President Williams confirmed this is new state money requiring raises; without HB 3, no annual raise mandate exists - Mr. Sanders leaned toward Scenario 1 $12.4M , citing uncertainty about funding beyond two years and other unfunded needs - Mr. Kidd suggested 4%/4%/3% ~$14.7M - Mr. Husbands suggested 3.5%/3.5%/2.5%, expressing concern about unknown future costs - Discussion about classification: Board members debated whether people who interact directly with students should receive the higher raise tier regardless of their pay scale classification - Mr. Williams suggested a principle: "If kids sit in front of them" they should be in the higher raise category - Mr. Inman suggested 3.5% across the board for all categories to eliminate classification disputes - Dr. Null proposed a possible solution: use AE pay scale boxes — counselors are in box 3, so everyone in AE boxes 1-3 gets the teacher raise; boxes 4+ get the administrative raise No vote

Public hearing concluded. Call to Order Meeting called to order at 6:13 PM. Quorum confirmed; proper notice posted per Texas Open Meetings Act. Dr. Stockton or administration explained: - The 30-day period was relatively new and somewhat arbitrary - Shortening to 15 days allows earlier conversations with families, many of whom end up qualifying for free/reduced lunch - Does not change the commitment to serving meals to all students daily - District currently averages slightly over $15,000/month in uncollected lunch charges Motion: Approve Item 3G. Vote: Approved unanimously voice vote, all in favor . Public Hearing 2: Turnaround Plan for Sam Houston Elementary School Board recessed at 6:20 PM for public hearing. Dr. Phillips confirmed that specific staffing requests from Principal Harris had been met, including behavioral support and tutoring, to free coaches to focus on working with teachers. - Mr. Husbands publicly commended Principal Harris for leaving a high-performing school to take on the challenge at Sam Houston. - Dr. Stockton emphasized that placing Principal Harris at Sam Houston was a major part of the plan and publicly committed to providing whatever the school needs. Motion: Approve the turnaround plan. Vote: Approved unanimously voice vote, all in favor . Vote: Approved unanimously voice vote, all in favor . Item 5A: 2018-19 School Calendar Presenter: Dr. Null Process: District-level planning and decision-making committee began work in October; two draft calendars posted in November for public comment; nearly 3,000 comments received. Committee unanimously recommended Draft 2 , which received approximately 60% of public responses. Principals also unanimously preferred Draft 2.

Racial Incident at The Woodlands High School / Anti-Bullying Multiple speakers addressed a Snapchat incident in which a student at The Woodlands High School sent a racially threatening message containing the N-word and referencing lynching to an African-American student. - Dr. Enrique Rosero – Urged the Board to reject racism, intolerance, and white supremacy and to be decisive in confronting such behaviors. Noted the incident did not occur in isolation. - Deborah Leiber – Volunteer with the Anti-Defamation League ADL . Shared her own daughter's experience with anti-Semitic bullying in CISD schools. Recommended the Board encourage all CISD schools to participate in the ADL "No Place For Hate" Initiative , noting that 365 Houston-area schools participated last year and that Cy-Fair ISD is encouraging district-wide adoption. Provided the ADL website for more information. - Amy Hamrick – Interracial family with children at Powell Elementary. Expressed concern that the district did not issue a strong independent public statement condemning the incident. Noted that high school students she's spoken with said such incidents are not uncommon. Encouraged the Board to push schools toward adopting the No Place For Hate program. - Jennifer Majors Baca – Co-founder of The Woodlands Coalition for Equality. Read a letter from community member Chris J. Miller detailing multiple racially charged incidents in the area, referencing SB 179 David's Law on cyberbullying , and urging the Board to implement the No Place For Hate Initiative in CISD high schools. - Randall Kallinen – Civil rights attorney and former president of the Houston ACLU. Described the Snapchat message content. Criticized the school's response, stating the principal suggested the victim transfer schools and that no meaningful discipline was imposed on the offending student. Urged the Board, as the governing body above the superintendent and principals, to take decisive action. - RJ King – Father of the targeted student. Stated his family moved from Michigan over a year ago and that his daughter had previously experienced a racial comment at McCullough Junior High after the 2016 election. Described his daughter as traumatized by the Snapchat threat. Said the administration's response of rearranging the offending student's schedule was "clearly not enough." Called for zero tolerance, removal of the offending student, and a district-wide communication rejecting such behavior. Stated: "If you don't take action, guess what that says — you condone what's going on." - Cynthia Cole – Board member of the Greater Houston Coalition for Justice. Referenced SB 179 effective September 1, 2017 requiring school districts to have cyberbullying policies and consistent disciplinary actions. Urged the Board to act, stating "Fix it. Because it can happen." Other Public Comments - Matt Beasley – Presented a petition with approximately 1,200 signatures opposing the district's transportation policy, specifically regarding students who must walk up to two miles through ditches, carrying instruments without bus service. Referenced the district's $128 million in savings and asked for equal transportation services. Submitted petition for the record. - Jason Millsaps – Chairman of Montgomery County Young Republicans. Read a unanimously passed resolution calling on Conroe ISD to approve reappraisals of Hurricane Harvey-damaged properties. Noted that Willis ISD, The Woodlands Township, Montgomery County Hospital District, and others had already approved reappraisals. Referenced the district's $166 million unobligated surplus and urged the Board to provide relief to affected taxpayers. - George Young – Homeowner in Benders Landing. Complained about construction of Woodson's Reserve Middle School behind his home, including: removal of all buffer trees in violation of community setback rules, construction work occurring at 2–4 a.m. with floodlights and noise, and unresponsiveness from CISD's construction division. Stated police told him CISD is exempt from noise ordinances. Consent Agenda Motion: Mr. Sanders moved to approve; Mr. Moore seconded. Vote: Passed unanimously no dissent noted . Item 4A: Texas Accountability Summary — SAT, ACT, AP, TELPAS Results for 2017 Presented by: Dr. Curtis Null, Deputy Superintendent Key Highlights: TEA Accountability: - District rating: Met Standard highest possible rating - Index 1 Student Achievement : 85 state: 75; target: 60 - Index 2 Student Progress : 45 state target: 22 - Index 3 Closing Performance Gaps : 49 state: 40; target: 28 - Index 4 Post-Secondary Readiness : 82 state: 76; target: 60 System Safeguards: Met 74 of 86 standards 86% . Target areas remain Special Education and English Language Learner performance — noted as a common challenge across comparable districts. District Distinctions: Scored 32% of total measures 55% needed for distinction . Campuses earned 37% of total possible distinctions. TELPAS English Language Learners : - Students in early-exit bilingual/ESL model outperform monolingual peers on English STAAR tests in their first and second monitored years. Graduation Rate: - Believed to be the highest ever in district history ; Class of 2015 rate was 95.7%. - Students on more rigorous graduation plans continue to increase. - All current seniors now on the new Foundations plan with endorsement and distinguished designation as default. SAT: District outpaces state and national averages test was renormed . National Merit Scholars: 24 semifinalists to be honored at the next meeting. ACT: Composite score of 23.5 very high for a district . 45% of testers deemed college ready vs. 26% statewide. Advanced Placement: 4,544 students took 9,367 exams. Average score of 2.89 exceeded state and national averages. Most popular: World History, with AP Human Geography notable as primarily a freshman course. Dual Credit Lone Star College : Enrollment continues to rise without sacrificing AP participation. English is the top dual-credit course. CTE/Workforce: Programs include welding, phlebotomy, CNC, auto-tech, and EMT. Students earn industry certifications e.g., ServSafe . Post-Graduation Plans: Majority plan four-year college; Texas A&M and UT remain top destinations. Upcoming Events: - College Night recently held ~150 universities, ~5,000 attendees - Career Expo: October 26, 6–8 p.m. at Lone Star Convention Center Mrs. Bush asked about the number of dual-credit students; Dr. Null committed to providing that figure. Campus administrators and teachers present were recognized and thanked. Item 5A: Design-Build Firm for Stadium Scoreboard Replacement Presented by: Easy Foster, Director of Planning & Construction - Project replaces end-of-life scoreboards at both stadiums and the Natatorium. - Three firms responded to RFQ; two Daktronics and NEFCO were interviewed. - Daktronics selected as best value based on published criteria. Motion: Dr. Stockton moved; Mr. Sanders seconded. Vote: Passed

others in private, out-of-state, or trade schools - Nearly 900 had a GPA of 3.0 or above in their first year - 382 students had GPAs below 2.0 Financial Performance - Texas Smart Schools formerly FAST Rating: 5-Star rating for all 6 years of the system's existence — only three districts in Texas have achieved this Conroe ISD, Cy-Fair, and Friendswood - Instruction spending: 60.42% of operating budget above state average; A quorum was confirmed and proper notice was posted per the Texas Open Meetings Act. - Invocation led by Mrs. Bush; Pledge of Allegiance led by Mr. Williams. Item 2a: Special District Recognition – 2016 UIL 6A Boys Cross-Country State Champions - Greg Colson campus administrator introduced the team and coaching staff from The Woodlands High School . - Head Coach Juris Green presented the team, noting this was the school's 19th state cross-country title and 38th consecutive state meet appearance . - The team won the 2016 state meet by 72 points scoring 51 , despite losing 5 of 7 runners from the 2015 championship team to graduation. - Team members recognized: Gavin Hawpar 6th place , Noah Wells 7th , Daniel Baker 11th , Jacob Rayford ~22nd , Ethan Marcotte ~24th , Austin Childress 26th . Assistant Coach Chris Bales was also introduced. - Danny Green , the original/founding cross-country coach, was recognized in the audience — he coached for 30 years and won 15 state championships . Combined with his son Juris's 4 titles in 8 years , the father-son legacy totals 19 state championships in ~38 years . - A plaque was presented to the team. Item 2b: Special District Recognition – Energy Solutions 2015 Outstanding RMS Partner of Excellence Award - Dr. Hines introduced the recognition. - Conroe ISD received the Outstanding Resource Management System RMS Partner for Excellence Award from Energy Solutions/NTX Energy Texas for achievements in energy and resource management. - District achieved a 3% target savings goal of approximately 80,978,717 kilowatt hours through behavioral changes turning off lights, adjusting thermostats — no capital spending required. - Savings equated to approximately: - $200,000/year off the electric bill - Equivalent of 4 teacher salaries - Removing 368 cars from the road for one year - Offsetting 196,000 gallons of gasoline - Energy Manager Roger Garvey and Assistant Director of Maintenance Dwight Martin were recognized for their participation. - Award presented by Phelan Ayer Energy Texas , Amanda Cambree RMS Program Manager , and David Rubick Program Consultant . Item 2c: Citizen Participation - No citizens registered to address the board. Item 3: Consent Agenda - No items were pulled for separate discussion. - Motion to approve the consent agenda as presented. - Vote: Approved unanimously all in favor by show of hands . Item 4a: Consider Approval of District of Innovation Plan - Dr. Nolan presented the item, summarizing the process since October when the board authorized exploration of a District of Innovation designation. - The District-Level Planning and Decision-Making Committee developed the plan with input from community members, teachers, parents, and administrators. The plan was posted online for public feedback. - The plan includes only one element: Flexible School Calendar — allowing the board the option to start the school year earlier than the state-mandated fourth Monday in August which falls on August 28 in 2017 . This is not a mandate to start earlier, but provides the option. - Motion to approve; seconded . - Vote: Approved unanimously all in favor by show of hands . Item 4b: Consider Approval of Guaranteed Maximum Price GMP Amendment – Knox Junior High School & Woodlands Transportation Center - Ms. Foster presented the item. - Ramallah Construction was selected as construction manager at risk in March. - Negotiated GMP: $11,120,157 for Knox Junior High additions and Woodlands Transportation Center renovations. - Clarification provided: The "amendment" is the standard contractual term used to establish the GMP under the master agreement — it is not amending a previous price. - Motion to approve; seconded . - Vote: Approved unanimously all in favor by show of hands . Item 4c: Consider Approval of Guaranteed Maximum Price – 2017 Life Cycle Projects - Ms. Foster presented. - GTT was selected as construction manager at risk in February. - Projects cover mechanical, electrical, plumbing, roofing, and building envelope maintenance items. - Negotiated GMP: $8,167,886 . - Motion to approve; seconded . - Vote: Approved unanimously all in favor by show of hands . Item 4d: Update on Attendance Zones for Oak Ridge Feeder Zone Schools This was an informational update; no vote was taken. Dr. Hines provided a detailed update on the rezoning process necessitated by three new schools opening: New Schools Opening: 1. Lucile J. Bradley Elementary K–4 – Opening Fall 2017 at 4200 Falls Lake Drive The Falls ; ~1,000 student capacity. 2. Flex 18 Intermediate Grades 5–6 – Opening August 2018 at Woodson's Reserve Parkway and Tench Lane; ~1,000 student capacity. 3. Grand Oaks High School Grades 9–10 initially, phasing to 9–12 by 2020 – Opening Fall 2018 at 4800 Riley Fuzzel Road; ~3,100 student capacity. Process: - Attendance Boundary Committee ABC composed of principals and parents from affected schools. - Six public meetings held to date with hundreds of attendees. - Over 850 high school comments and ~130 elementary/intermediate comments received, plus several petitions. - Two scenarios developed for each school level elementary, intermediate, high school . Elementary Scenarios: - Key challenges: Schools located very close together along Birnam Woods Drive; large neighborhoods difficult to keep intact; goal of not opening Bradley at capacity ~800 target . - Scenario 1: Populates Bradley from multiple existing zones; splits Harmony neighborhood using Birnam Woods Drive; moves The Meadows from Birnam Woods to Kaufman which would also change their feeder pattern to Vogel Intermediate, Irons Junior High, and Oak Ridge High School . - Scenario 2: Very similar to Scenario 1 but splits Harmony at Lexington Boulevard instead of Rayford Road, resulting in fewer students moved ; Broadway retains more students; Snyder has more room for growth from Woodson's Reserve. This scenario had significantly more community support according to Dr. Hines. Intermediate Scenarios: - Scenario 1: Splits some neighborhoods Harmony, Birnam Woods area, Lockridge Farms to maintain walkability to Cox; moves The Meadows to Vogel. - Scenario 2 with committee-recommended modification : Moves most of the Bradley feeder zone to Flex 18; keeps Legends Trace 57G with the Bradley cohort going to Flex 18 instead of splitting them. This produces nearly identical enrollment at Cox and Flex 18 and similar socio-economic balance between the two schools. Committee strongly favored this approach. High School Scenarios: - Described as the most difficult conversations of the process. - Scenario 1: Largely mirrors the current junior high boundary; only change is The Meadows moving to Oak Ridge. Results in ~2,420 at Grand Oaks and ~2,270 at Oak Ridge in 2020 when fully phased in . - Scenario 2: Moves Forest Village/Spring Forest currently Irons JH area to Grand Oaks; keeps Legends Trace/Legends Ranch at Oak Ridge. Results in a larger Oak Ridge and smaller Grand Oaks . Produces better socio-economic balance Oak Ridge 33% vs. Grand Oaks 22% economically disadvantaged, compared to 38%/19.5% in Scenario 1 . However, ~300 students would attend a different high school than their junior high feeder pattern suggests. - Key discussion points raised by board members: - Growth concerns: Mr. Husbands raised concerns about future development along FM 242 and FM 1488 potentially overwhelming Oak Ridge again under Scenario 2, while Scenario 1 leaves more capacity for future growth. - Portables: The Woodlands HS has ~14 portables; College Park has 2 — growth pressure is increasing. -

Anna Hal — The Woodlands High School Graduate Extended Remarks - Shared her personal experience with a suicide attempt during her junior year - Described feeling unsupported by school counselors when she asked to drop AP classes for her mental health; was told it "would look bad for college" - Praised individual teachers especially the physics department for their personal support - Key proposals to the board: - Increase mental health education/awareness training for counselors and teachers - Improve communication between teachers and counselors regarding at-risk students - Prioritize hiring mental health professionals psychologists, social workers on campuses - Make mental health awareness an ongoing effort, not a once-a-year event - Referenced Fairfax County, Virginia's model as an example - Expressed concern that the district sends a message that grades matter more than student well-being - Board response: Dr. Stockton noted the topic is taken "very seriously," that the new assistant superintendent came from Fairfax County, that a crisis counselor was added and another would be added mid-year - Board Member later in meeting echoed support, noted a Monday meeting among community leaders, Montgomery County nonprofits, and CISD's crisis counselor addressing these issues Consent Agenda Item 3 Items A through H, J, and K approved together. Vote: Unanimous all in favor Item 3I — Award of RFP 16-07-03: Property, Boiler & Machinery and Crime Insurance - Pulled from consent agenda for separate discussion - President Husbands abstained employer involvement ; Vice President Kid presided Discussion: - Mr. Rice explained: - The district historically has had one of the lowest property insurance rates in the state - Market constriction due to massive flooding and hail losses statewide $250 million across carriers; affiliated carrier alone had $100 million in losses - RFP sent to 37 vendors; only 1 response received Souls Insurance, the previous carrier - Actual premium increase was only $32,000, but the new policy included a 5% hail/wind deductible per building - District purchased a deductible buy-down for $384,000 to maintain $100,000 per occurrence per building with $500,000 max cap - Total premium with buy-down: $1,026,400 — approximately 41% overall increase - District had no claims during the April/May 2016 flooding - Rates still favorable compared to Harris County districts Vote: Approved Mr. Husbands abstaining; remaining members in favor Item 4A — 2016 Preliminary STAAR 3–8 and EOC Results Presented by: Dr. Debbie Phillips Elementary and Jim Kaker Secondary Key Points: - Number of test items required to pass increased this year - Despite higher standards, CISD maintained high academic achievement - Elementary highlights: - 4th grade: Excellent gains in reading and math; writing at 81% well above state - Slight dips in some areas mirrored statewide trends - 5th grade science: maintained at 88% - Secondary highlights: - 7th grade showed consistent results; 8th grade showed gains as students matured - CISD approximately 10% above state average across content areas at junior high - End of Course EOC : Strong across algebra, biology, US history; English 1 and 2 noted as challenging - Passing standards will be raised again for 2016–17 - Staff development focused on T-TESS new teacher evaluation system and growth mindset - District emphasized continuity: "no new big initiatives — we do what we do and get better every day" Item 5B — Naming New Facilities Presentation Presented by: Dr. N Deputy Superintendent - Facilities to be named: New High School and Flex 17 new elementary school - Community naming campaign received: - 173 submissions for the high school 74 unique names - 126 submissions for the elementary school 72 unique names - Policy reminder: High schools should be named for geographic regions; elementary schools may be named for geographic regions or individuals - Board is not limited to submitted names - This was an informational presentation only — formal consideration and vote scheduled for September meeting Discussion on Process: -
